SWCR017-04.Offering ID: An ultra-low temperature (ULT) freezer primarily used in labs at universities, biotech companies, biopharmaceutical companies, hospitals, and medical testing centers to store samples at temperatures between -94°F and -112°F that meets ENERGY STAR® Program Requirements Product Specification for Laboratory Grade Refrigerators and Freezers Version 1.1.
SWCR017-04.Base Case Description: A standard efficiency ULT freezer with a standard efficiency dual cascade refrigeration system.
SWCR017-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A qualifying ultra-low-temperature (ULT) freezer must meet the following criteria:
  • Upright ULT freezer designed for laboratory application that will maintain a setpoint storage temperature between -94°F and -112°F (-70°C and -80°C)
  • ENERGY STAR certification or the maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) of an ULT freezer at -103°F (-75°C) is 0.55 kWh/day/cu.ft.
  • 15 cu.ft. < Volume ≤ 29 cu.ft.
SWCR017-04.Important Notes: eTRM only specifies Education-University (EUn), Health/Medical-Hospital (Hsp), Manufacturing Pharmaceutical (IPH), and Manufacturing Biotech (MBT) building types. However, measure savings are not building type specific, and measure is eligible for other building types.

SWCR017-05.Offering ID: An ultra-low temperature (ULT) freezer primarily used in labs at universities, biotech companies, biopharmaceutical companies, hospitals, and medical testing centers to store samples at temperatures between -94°F and -112°F that meets ENERGY STAR® Program Requirements Product Specification for Laboratory Grade Refrigerators and Freezers Version 1.1.
SWCR017-05.Base Case Description: A standard efficiency ULT freezer with a standard efficiency dual cascade refrigeration system.
SWCR017-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A qualifying ultra-low-temperature (ULT) freezer must meet the following criteria:
  • Upright ULT freezer designed for laboratory applications that will maintain a setpoint storage temperature between -94°F and -112°F (-70°C and -80°C)
  • ENERGY STAR certification, or the maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) of an ULT freezer at -103°F (-75°C) is 0.55 kWh/day/cu.ft.
  • 15 cu.ft. < Volume ≤ 29 cu.ft.
SWCR017-05.Important Notes: eTRM only specifies Education – University (EUn), Health/Medical – Hospital (Hsp), Manufacturing Pharmaceutical (IPH), and Manufacturing Biotech (MBT) building types. However, measure savings are not building type specific, and measure is eligible for other building types.

SWCR017-06.Offering ID: An ultra-low temperature (ULT) freezer primarily used in labs at universities, biotech companies, biopharmaceutical companies, hospitals, and medical testing centers to store samples at temperatures between -94°F and -112°F that meets ENERGY STAR® Program Requirements Product Specification for Laboratory Grade Refrigerators and Freezers Version 2.0.
SWCR017-06.Base Case Description: A standard efficiency ULT freezer that does not meet the measure case criteria. In the absence of mandatory state and federal regulations for ULTs, there is little incentive on the part of equipment manufacturers to test their equipment. As such, the parameters for base case equipment use the Energy Star v1.1 Maximum Daily Energy Consumption (MDEC) Requirement.
SWCR017-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A qualifying ultra-low-temperature (ULT) freezer must meet the following criteria:
  • Upright ULT freezer designed for laboratory applications that will maintain a setpoint storage temperature between -94°F and -112°F (-70°C and -80°C)
  • ENERGY STAR certification or meet the ULT maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) criteria as specified in the ENERGY STAR Product Specification for Laboratory Grade Refrigerators and Freezers Eligibility Criteria Version 2.0.
    • 0<Volume<20: MDEC ≤0.46 kWh/day/cu.ft.
    • Volume≥20: MDEC ≤0.35 kWh/day/cu.ft.
  • 15 cu.ft. < Volume ≤ 29 cu.ft.
SWCR017-06.Important Notes: eTRM lists specific building types. However, measure savings are not building type specific, and measure is eligible for other building types.

SWCR018-04.Offering ID: A commercial reach-in refrigerator or freezer that meets the ENERGY STAR® Program Requirements Product Specification for Commercial Refrigerators and Freezers Version 5.0 replacing a standard efficiency unit of the same configuration and capacity.
SWCR018-04.Base Case Description: A standard-efficiency, reach-in solid or glass door refrigerator or freezer that complies with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) daily energy consumption requirements.
SWCR018-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /: Units with remote refrigeration systems do not qualify and used or rebuilt equipment are not eligible.

SWCR018-05.Offering ID: A commercial reach-in refrigerator or freezer that meets the ENERGY STAR® Program Requirements Product Specification for Commercial Refrigerators and Freezers Version 5.0 replacing a standard efficiency unit of the same configuration and capacity.
SWCR018-05.Base Case Description: A standard-efficiency, reach-in solid or glass door refrigerator or freezer that complies with the U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) daily energy consumption requirements.
SWCR018-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /: Units with remote refrigeration systems do not qualify and used or rebuilt equipment are not eligible.

SWCR019-03.Offering ID:
A high-efficiency, low-temperature (freezer, case temperature <32°F) reach-in refrigerated display case. Offerings are defined by the base case coffin’s width where:
  • “wide” means the measure case is ⅓ the base case length (e.g., 20-foot reach-in case replacing 60-foot coffin case).
  • “narrow” means the measure case is ¼ the base case length (e.g., 15-foot reach-in case replacing 60-foot coffin case).
SWCR019-03.Base Case Description: A federal code-compliant, low-temperature coffin (open) refrigerated display case.
SWCR019-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Installation must meet the following criteria:
  • If the display width is ≤3 ft, the coffin must be replaced by a reach-in case that is ¼ of the total length of the coffins or less.
  • If the display width is >3 ft, the coffin must be replaced by a reach-in case that is ⅓ of the total length of the coffin or less.

The replacement case must include triple-pane doors with low or no anti-sweat heat or with anti-sweat-heat controls.

SWCR019-04.Offering ID:
A high-efficiency, low-temperature (freezer, case temperature <32°F) reach-in refrigerated display case. Offerings are defined by the base case coffin’s width where:
  • “wide” means the measure case is ⅓ the base case length (e.g., 20-foot reach-in case replacing 60-foot coffin case).
“narrow” means the measure case is ¼ the base case length (e.g., 15-foot reach-in case replacing 60-foot coffin case).
SWCR019-04.Base Case Description: A low-temperature coffin (open) refrigerated display case that complies with Title 10 maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) requirement.
SWCR019-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Installation must meet the following criteria:
  • If the display width is ≤3 ft, the coffin must be replaced by a reach-in case that is ¼ of the total length of the coffins or less.
  • If the display width is >3 ft, the coffin must be replaced by a reach-in case that is ⅓ of the total length of the coffin or less.

The replacement case must include triple-pane doors with low or no anti-sweat heat or with anti-sweat-heat controls.

SWCR020-03.Offering ID: A medium-temperature (cooler, case temperature ≥32°F), open, vertical refrigerated display case with a high efficiency evaporator coil.
SWCR020-03.Base Case Description: A medium temp, open vertical refrigerated display case with a standard evaporator coil that complies with Title 10 maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) requirement.
SWCR020-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The medium-temperature display case must meet or exceed specifications and efficiency requirements through the use of a high efficiency evaporator coil.
The new display case must replace an existing medium-temperature, open, vertical refrigerated display case of the same style with an equivalent setpoint temperature and not higher than the replaced unit's setpoint temperature. The new display case must not have a lower total display area and higher lighting and/or anti-condensate heater wattage than the replaced case.
SWCR020-03.Quality Assurance: The manufacturer specification sheet for the new display case should be submitted to confirm eligibility.

SWCR020-04.Offering ID: A medium-temperature (cooler, case temperature ≥32°F), open, vertical refrigerated display case with a high efficiency evaporator coil.
SWCR020-04.Base Case Description: A medium temperature, open, vertical (or multi-deck) refrigerated display case with a standard evaporator coil that complies with Title 10 maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) requirement.
SWCR020-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The medium-temperature display case must meet or exceed specifications and efficiency requirements through the use of a high efficiency evaporator coil.
The new display case must replace an existing medium-temperature, open, vertical refrigerated display case of the same style with an equivalent setpoint temperature and not higher than the replaced unit's setpoint temperature. The new display case must not have a lower total display area and higher lighting and/or anti-condensate heater wattage than the replaced case.
SWCR020-04.Quality Assurance: The manufacturer specification sheet for the new display case should be submitted to confirm eligibility.

SWCR021-03.Offering ID: A low-temperature (freezer, case temperature <32°F) or a medium-temperature (cooler, case temperature ≥32°F), reach-in, glass door display case.
SWCR021-03.Base Case Description: A low- or medium-temperature, open, vertical (or multi-deck) display case that complies with Title 10 maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) requirement.
SWCR021-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure is applicable for supermarkets and grocery stores with stand-alone and/or multiplex compressor racks that provide refrigeration to remote display cases and walk-in coolers/freezers. This measure is applicable to supermarkets and grocery stores with both medium-temperature and low-temperature open multi-deck refrigeration cases. Requirements include:
  • Must replace an existing, open, multi-deck display case with a new reach-in unit with doors, electronically commutated motor (ECM) evaporator fan(s), and LED lighting.
  • Medium-temperature cases must have no heat in the door. Heat is allowed in the frames of medium temperature cases.
  • Low-temperature case door and frame heaters combined may not consume more than 50 W/ft of case length.
  • The new case length must be less than or equal to the original case length.
  • The new case setpoint temperature cannot be lower than the original case setpoint temperature.
  • This measure is only for display cases served by a remote refrigeration system.

The following are not eligible:
  • Specialty deli cases, custom coolers/freezers, and walk-in boxes with reach-in doors
  • Refurbished cases unless compliant with the 2012 Federal Efficiency Standards
  • Display case replacements that are part of large-scale store remodels – involving 50% of the linear feet of refrigerated casework or 32 linear feet of casework replacements, whichever is less – and any new construction project.
SWCR021-03.Quality Assurance: Salvage, disposal or photographic records of replaced equipment should be part of program application requirements when early replacement or open-to-closed case conversion savings are being utilized to ensure the correct baseline is assumed for these measures.

SWCR021-04.Offering ID: A low-temperature (freezer, case temperature <32°F) or a medium-temperature (cooler, case temperature at or above ≥32°F), reach-in, glass door display case.
SWCR021-04.Base Case Description: A low- or medium-temperature, open, vertical (or multi-deck) display case that complies with Title 10 maximum daily energy consumption (MDEC) requirement.
SWCR021-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure is applicable for supermarkets and grocery stores with stand-alone and/or multiplex compressor racks that provide refrigeration to remote display cases and walk-in coolers/freezers. This measure is applicable to supermarkets and grocery stores with both medium-temperature and low-temperature open multi-deck refrigeration cases. Requirements include:
  • Must replace an existing, open, multi-deck display case with a new reach-in unit with doors, electronically commutated motor (ECM) evaporator fan(s), and LED lighting.
  • Medium-temperature cases must have no heat in the door. Heat is allowed in the frames of medium temperature cases.
  • Low-temperature case door and frame heaters combined may not consume more than 50 W/ft of case length.
  • The new case length must be less than or equal to the original case length.
  • The new case setpoint temperature cannot be lower than the original case setpoint temperature.
  • This measure is only for display cases served by a remote refrigeration system.

The following are not eligible:
  • Specialty deli cases, custom coolers/freezers, and walk-in boxes with reach-in doors
  • Refurbished cases unless compliant with the 2012 Federal Efficiency Standards
  • Display case replacements that are part of large-scale store remodels – involving 50% of the linear feet of refrigerated casework or 32 linear feet of casework replacements, whichever is less – and any new construction project.
SWCR021-04.Quality Assurance: Salvage, disposal or photographic records of replaced equipment should be part of program application requirements when early replacement or open-to-closed case conversion savings are being utilized to ensure the correct baseline is assumed for these measures.

SWCR022-04.Offering ID:
Measure includes the following offerings:
  • Offering A includes saturated condensing temperature (SCT) controls, in wet mode and dry mode, to reset based on outdoor dry-bulb temperature (variable condensing setpoint control) on an existing code-compliant standard efficiency (45 Btu/W) adiabatic condenser serving a commercial refrigeration system using halocarbon refrigerants.
  • Offering B is defined as installing an efficient adiabatic condenser rated at ≥65 Btu/W replacing a code-compliant standard efficiency (45 Btu/W) adiabatic condenser serving a commercial refrigeration system using halocarbon refrigerants.
SWCR022-04.Base Case Description:
The base cases are:
  • Offering A: A code-compliant standard efficiency adiabatic condenser rated at 45 Btu/W with variable speed fan control and a fixed saturated condensing temperature setpoint.
  • Offering B: A 2022 Title 24 compliant adiabatic condenser rated at 45 Btu/W with variable speed fan control and a variable condensing setpoint. The measure case for Offering A is the base case for measure Offering B.
SWCR022-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The variable condensing setpoint controls (Offering A) must meet the following requirements:
  • When operating in wet and dry mode, SCT reset is based on the outdoor dry-bulb temperature.
  • A rated temperature difference of 10°F when operating in dry mode, and 30°F when operating in wet mode.

The efficient adiabatic condenser (Offering B) must meet the following requirements:
  • The new condenser must have an efficiency rating ≥65 Btu/W.
  • The new condenser must also have variable condensing setpoint controls installed.

The following are not eligible:
  • Non-grocery building types.
  • Grocery building condensers serving HVAC systems.
  • Carbon dioxide (CO2) and ammonia (NH3) based refrigeration systems.

    • Full Size, ≥5 pans: ≤1.4 kW Idle Energy Rate and ≥76% Cooking Energy Efficiency

    • Full Size Tier 2: ≤7,500 Btu/hr Idle Energy Rate and ≥51% Cooking Energy Efficiency

SWFS001-03.Quality Assurance: Verify unit meets the eligibility requirement in the Offering ID table.

SWFS007-04.Offering ID: An energy efficient half-size (<15 cu.ft.) or full-size (≥15 cu.ft.) insulated hot food holding cabinet (HFHC).
SWFS007-04.Base Case Description: A half-size or full-size HFHC that complies with the maximum normalized idle energy rate requirement of California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20).
SWFS007-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure includes new full-size (≥15 cu.ft.) and half-size (<15 cu.ft.) insulated food holding cabinets. Eligible products must meet the following requirements:
  • Qualifying cabinets must not exceed the maximum idle energy rate of 25 W/cu.ft. for full-size cabinets and 35 W/cu.ft. for half-size cabinets, in accordance with the American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) Standard Test Method for the Performance of Hot Food Holding Cabinets (F2140).
  • All measures must be electric hot food holding cabinets that are fully insulated (i.e., on all sides) and have solid or transparent doors designed to maintain the temperature of hot food that has been cooked using a separate appliance.
  • Eligible holding cabinets must be listed on the Food Service Technology Center (FSTC) qualified product list (https://caenergywise.com/rebates/).
  • Must have an electric resistance heating element.

Cook-and-hold or retherm, used or rebuilt, and passive holding cabinets with no heating elements equipment are not eligible.
SWFS007-04.Quality Assurance: Verify unit meets the eligibility requirement in the Offering ID table.

SWFS012-03.Offering ID: A demand-controlled kitchen ventilation (DCKV) system that varies the exhaust and supply fan speed based on the cooking appliances’ energy and effluent output.
SWFS012-03.Base Case Description: A standard commercial kitchen ventilation system with single-speed exhaust and makeup air fans and a manual ON/OFF control.
SWFS012-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible installation must meet the following requirements:
  • The measure requires the installation of a sensor(s) in the hood exhaust collar or within the hood, that senses cooking conditions and allows the system to automatically vary the rate of exhaust and make-up airflow. Advanced systems that can communicate directly with cooking appliances varying the ventilation rate based on cook cycles are allowed in lieu of hood sensors.
  • The control system must be used in conjunction with a variable-speed drive (VSD) or directly vary the speed of an EC motor on the exhaust and supply fans. Systems with a single exhaust fan installed on multiple hoods must use balancing dampers to vary the airflow in the separate hoods.
  • The control system must be able to control the supply airflow proportional to the exhaust airflow on the makeup air side by either varying the speed of the MUA or DOAS fan motor or by varying the amount of outside air by controlling a damper. This can be done indirectly by the DCKV system through EMS integration or direct communication with the MUA or DOAS.
  • Installation of new exhaust hoods (i.e., NC) must have a total of all kitchen hoods’ exhaust be ≤5,000 cfm, due to Title 24 mandate.

Automatic on and off scheduling done with hood controls or EMS is considered the base case. Exhaust systems with a VFD or ECM that is not controlled by cooking sensors are not considered DCKV.
CFM qualifications in the measure include all hoods in a single kitchen and shall have all cooking hood cfms added for the purpose of calculation. In the context of DCKV, a kitchen is defined as a cooking space with a single or multiple exhaust hoods. Kitchen hoods in separate cooking spaces connected by ductwork and exhausted by the same fan are considered a part of the same kitchen. Kitchen spaces on a campus or in a large building that are on separate floors or in distinctly different spaces without connecting exhaust duct work are considered separate kitchens. Food courts with kitchens that have separate spaces and do not share the same exhaust fan for cooking are considered separate kitchens. Food courts with kitchens that have a single kitchen space and that share exhaust fans for cooking are considered the same kitchen. Ghost kitchens shall have their cooklines considered to be separate kitchens only if the cooking hoods are not connected to the same exhaust duct work and are not sharing the same exhaust fan. Ghost kitchens may share point of sale infrastructure and dishwashing ventilation system.
Used or rebuilt equipment is not eligible.
SWFS012-03.Quality Assurance: Ventilation system controls must be properly installed and commissioned to ensure optimal performance and prevent control system override by end user.

SWFS012-04.Offering ID: A demand-controlled kitchen ventilation (DCKV) system that varies the exhaust and supply fan speed based on the cooking appliances’ energy and effluent output.
SWFS012-04.Base Case Description: A standard commercial kitchen ventilation system with single-speed exhaust and makeup air fans and a simple ON/OFF control.
SWFS012-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:

Advanced systems that can communicate directly with cooking appliances varying the ventilation rate based on cook cycles are allowed in lieu of hood sensors.
  • The control system must be used in conjunction with a variable-speed drive (VSD) or directly vary the speed of an EC motor on the exhaust and supply fans. Systems with a single exhaust fan installed on multiple hoods must use balancing dampers to vary the airflow in the separate hoods.
  • The control system must be able to control the supply airflow proportional to the exhaust airflow on the makeup air side by either varying the speed of the MUA or DOAS fan motor or by varying the amount of outside air by controlling a damper. This can be done indirectly by the DCKV system through EMS integration or direct communication with the MUA or DOAS.
  • Installation of new exhaust hoods (i.e., NC) must have a total of all kitchen hoods’ exhaust be ≤5,000 cfm, due to Title 24 mandate.

Automatic on and off scheduling done with hood controls or EMS is considered the base case. Exhaust systems with a VFD or ECM that is not controlled by cooking sensors are not considered DCKV.
In the context of DCKV, a kitchen is defined as a cooking space with a single or multiple exhaust hoods. Kitchen hoods in separate cooking spaces connected by ductwork and exhausted by the same fan are considered a part of the same kitchen. Kitchen spaces on a campus or in a large building that are on separate floors or in distinctly different spaces without connecting exhaust duct work are considered separate kitchens. Food courts with kitchens that have separate spaces and do not share the same exhaust fan for cooking are considered separate kitchens. Food courts with kitchens that have a single kitchen space and that share exhaust fans for cooking are considered the same kitchen. Ghost kitchens shall have their cooklines considered to be separate kitchens only if the cooking hoods are not connected to the same exhaust duct work and are not sharing the same exhaust fan. Ghost kitchens may share point of sale infrastructure and dishwashing ventilation system.
Used or rebuilt equipment is not eligible.
SWFS012-04.Quality Assurance:
The customer must provide verification of total exhaust cfm controlled by the new or existing kitchen hood to verify eligibility of the measure.
Ventilation system controls must be properly installed and commissioned to ensure optimal performance and prevent control system override by end user.

  • 5.0 ounce-force (ozf) ≤ spray force ≤ 8.0 ozf: flow rate ≤1.07 gpm

SWFS032-01.Offering ID: A prep table evaluated under a modified version of ASTM Standard F2143-16, in which it is tested to have a total daily energy consumption ≤3.0kWh and maintain temperature of ≥33°F and ≤41°F for 95% or greater of the test period.
SWFS032-01.Base Case Description: A prep table unable to meet either the energy consumption criteria of ≤3kWh or temperature holding criteria of ≥33°F and ≤41°F for 95% or greater of the test period when evaluated under the modified version of ASTM Standard F2143-16.
SWFS032-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The measure pertains to all refrigerated prep tables that have been tested to the qualifying measure case criteria via the modified ASTM Standard F2143-16 test method.
The following products are not eligible:
  • Used or rebuilt equipment
  • Countertop refrigerated prep tables
SWFS032-01.Important Notes:
The testing procedure for evaluating prep table performance specifically follows section 10.5 of ASTM F2143-16 along with the following modifications:
  • Product used for holding: 99.9% propylene glycol within ½ in. from top of holding pans.
  • Test is to be conducted without consideration of the initial and final weight of test product (test product quantity is determined by volume).
  • Initial test product temperature for each pan to target 35°F ± 2°F.

The test runs for 24 hours. The time factor provides a reasonable factor for units to exceed 41°F during defrost cycles. The total daily energy consumption is a combination of the 8-hour active period and 16-hour standby period.
A regression analysis of the laboratory testing data, from a variety of prep tables with various technologies; construction; pan capacities; and top display area, showed a very weak correlation between capacity and total daily energy consumption. Ranging in pan capacity and display area were often fitted with compressors of the same capacity and rating, therefore not varying greatly in their energy consumption. As a result, the measure will not normalize for unit size or capacity.

SWHC001-04.Offering ID: A high efficiency residential fan wall furnace or gravity wall furnace that meets the input capacity, in Btu/hr, and the annual fuel utilization efficiency (AFUE) described in the Offering ID table. Offering D is a traditional fan wall furnace that requires an external power source to power the fans and replaces a base case fan wall furnace. Offering E is a new fan type wall furnace technology that uses a thermoelectric generator on the flue gas to power the fans with waste heat, thus allowing for an easy replacement of an existing gravity wall furnace without upgrading electrical wiring.
SWHC001-04.Base Case Description: A gravity or fan type wall furnace that meets the federal and Title 20 minimum efficiency requirements. Measure is a like-for-like replacement.
SWHC001-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:

The following are ineligible:

  • Gravity wall furnaces ≤19,000 Btu/hr and ≥60,000 Btu/hr.
  • Fan type wall furnaces ≤19,000 Btu/hr and ≥42,000 Btu/hr.

SWHC002-03.Offering ID: Intermittent pilot light (IPL) controls, installed as an add-on unit to an existing host furnace or as an integrated unit in a new wall furnace, that will turn off the gravity wall furnace pilot light during times of no heat demand.
SWHC002-03.Base Case Description: A wall furnace with a continuous pilot light.
SWHC002-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligibility requirements for this measure are:
  • The wall furnace or fireplace insert shall be functional prior to the IPL retrofit.
  • The IPL control cannot replace an existing functional or non- functional pilot light control on a furnace or fireplace insert.
  • A furnace manufactured with integrated IPL controls can only replace a gravity wall furnace with a standing pilot light.
  • The IPL controls shall be capable of either using a battery or voltage line to effectively operate as intended.
Used or rebuilt intermittent pilot lights (IPL) are not eligible.
SWHC002-03.Quality Assurance:
Any technology manufacturer qualifies for measure offerings specified, if safety and all other regulations are met and the technology is commercially available.
Proof of purchase of a gravity wall furnace manufactured with an integrated intermittent pilot light must be provided.
SWHC002-03.Important Notes: The savings for the new construction measure offering represent the savings associated with the IPL controls only, and do not represent the savings due to the installation of a high-efficiency furnace.

SWHC004-06.Offering ID:
An efficient (non-condensing) or high efficiency (condensing) space heating boiler that meets the specifications described in the Offering ID table. Measure offerings are characterized by:
  • Boiler type: Hot water versus Steam
  • Boiler capacity (kBtu/hr)
  • Measure case efficiency: Annual Fuel Utilization Efficiency - AFUE, Thermal Efficiency – TE, or Combustion Efficiency – CE
  • Reset control temperature: Efficient boilers often use controls strategies to reset supply water temperature based on outdoor air (OA) temperature. This is the temperature range of the reset.
SWHC004-06.Base Case Description: A space heating boiler that meets the minimum efficiency stipulated by Title 20, Title 24, and federal code.
SWHC004-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /: The boiler must be a natural gas equipment, used for space heating to induce human comfort. Domestic hot water boilers and service hot water boilers are not eligible for this measure.

SWHC005-03.Offering ID: A nonresidential water-cooled chiller with full load efficiency (in kW/ton) and integrated part load value (IPLV, in kW/ton) at or lower than the values specified in the Offering ID table.
SWHC005-03.Base Case Description: A variable speed water-cooled chiller that meets Title 24 maximum kW/ton load efficiency.
SWHC005-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Title 24 also specifies alternate efficiency compliance paths for chiller technology. Path A requires a high full-load efficiency while Path B sets a lower minimum full-load efficiency than Path A but requires a higher minimum IPLV. This measure falls under Path B compliance. The equipment selection must be based on both full-load and part-load conditions.
Water-cooled chillers with a leaving evaporator fluid temperature higher than 32°F shall show Path B compliance with Table 110.2-D of Title 24 when tested or certified with water at standard rating conditions.
In a central or multi-chiller cooling plant with lead-lag configuration, "lead” chillers are not eligible. Energy impacts are determined with the assumption that the chiller is not a lead chiller.
All measures related to constant speed screw, constant speed centrifugal, and frictionless chillers are excluded due to the stringency of the tiers specified for the 2023 update of the Database of Energy Efficient Resources (DEER) and equipment availability in the market.

SWHC005-04.Offering ID:
A nonresidential water-cooled chiller with full load efficiency (in kW/ton) and integrated part load value (IPLV, in kW/ton) at or lower than the values specified in the Offering ID table.
  • Tier 1, full-load rating (kW/ton) and integrated part load value (IPLV) must be 10% more efficient than Title 24 requirement.
  • Tier 2, full-load rating (kW/ton) and integrated part load value (IPLV) must be 15% more efficient than Title 24 requirement.
SWHC005-04.Base Case Description: A variable speed water-cooled chiller that meets Title 24 maximum kW/ton load efficiency.
SWHC005-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Title 24 also specifies alternate efficiency compliance paths for chiller technology. Path A requires a high full-load efficiency while Path B sets a lower minimum full-load efficiency than Path A but requires a higher minimum IPLV. This measure falls under Path B compliance. The equipment selection must be based on both full-load and part-load conditions.
Water-cooled chillers with a leaving evaporator fluid temperature higher than 32°F shall show Path B compliance with Table 110.2-D of Title 24 when tested or certified with water at standard rating conditions.
In a central or multi-chiller cooling plant with lead-lag configuration, "lead” chillers are not eligible. Energy impacts are determined with the assumption that the chiller is not a lead chiller.
All measures related to constant speed screw, constant speed centrifugal, and frictionless chillers are excluded due to the stringency of the tiers specified for the 2023 update of the Database of Energy Efficient Resources (DEER) and equipment availability in the market.
SWHC005-04.Quality Assurance: Verify that the unit meets the full load and integrated part load efficiency requirement in the Offering ID table.

SWHC006-03.Offering ID: The installation of demand control ventilation (DCV) with carbon dioxide (CO2) sensor to an existing packaged single zone HVAC unit with an economizer. There is also an option to add an advanced digital economizer controller (ADEC) with DCV and CO2 sensor capability, if unit does not have one,
SWHC006-03.Base Case Description: Fixed position ventilation, with or without ADEC, corresponding to the 2022 Title 24 requirement or 20% of supply air, whichever is greater.
SWHC006-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing system must ventilate continuously during occupied hours and may not have any other device previously installed that is intended to perform DCV such as an occupancy sensor that controls ventilation rate.
The measure shall only be applied where it will result in a reduction to the overall ventilation that is supplied for the space.
The existing system must have an operable airside economizer installed. The economizer high limit must be optimized for the climate per Title 24 2022 Table 140.4-G.
The CO2 sensor can be either mounted in the return air duct or the zone. Measure case equipment must meet the following eligibility:
  • For CO2 sensor-only offerings, existing economizer control must be a digital type control and must have the capability to control the damper in response to a CO2 sensor signal.
  • For the ADEC with CO2 sensor offerings, a program-qualifying ADEC must be installed in addition to the CO2 sensor.
Although this measure does not trigger the 2022 Title 24 code, it is recommended, but not required, to follow the practices outlined below to align with Title 24:
  • For each system with DCV, CO2 sensors shall be installed in each room with no less than one sensor per 10,000 sq.ft. of floor space. When a zone or a space is served by more than one sensor, signal from any sensor indicating that CO2 is near or at the setpoint within a space, shall trigger an increase in ventilation to the space. This requirement is not applicable if there is a return air duct-mounted CO2 sensor installed.
  • Upon detection of sensor failure, the system shall provide a signal which resets to supply the minimum quantity of outside air to the level required if DCV were not installed.
  • CO2 sensors shall be certified by the manufacturer to be accurate within plus or minus 75 ppm at a 600 and 1,000 ppm concentration when measured at sea level and 25°C, factory calibrated and certified by the manufacturer to require calibration no more frequently than once every 5 years.
Systems with existing devices, such as occupancy sensors, intended to perform DCV operation are not eligible.
The existing system must be packaged single zone DX cooling unit with gas heat, cooling only unit, or heat pump.

SWHC006-04.Offering ID: The installation of demand control ventilation (DCV) with carbon dioxide (CO2) sensor to an existing packaged single zone HVAC unit with an economizer.
SWHC006-04.Base Case Description: An existing single zone unitary cooling units with gas heat, cooling only units, and heat pumps with airside economizers, but no ability to use DCV.
SWHC006-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing system must ventilate continuously during occupied hours and may not have any other device previously installed that is intended to perform DCV such as an occupancy sensor that controls ventilation rate.
The measure shall only be applied where it will result in a reduction to the overall ventilation that is supplied for the space.
The existing system must have an operable airside economizer and a digital economizer controller with the capability to control the damper in response to a CO2 sensor signal. Otherwise, an advanced digital economizer controller (ADEC) may be installed, but this measure does not assume the cost for new ADEC in its analysis.
The economizer high limit must be optimized for the climate per 2025 Title 24 Table 140.4-G. Economizer repair and adjustment scopes may be incentivized through measure packages SWSV005 and SWSV010.
The CO2 sensor can be either mounted in the return air duct or the zone.
Systems with existing devices, such as occupancy sensors, intended to perform DCV operation are not eligible.
The existing system must be packaged single zone DX cooling unit with gas heat, cooling only unit, or heat pump.

SWHC006-05.Offering ID: The installation of demand control ventilation (DCV) with carbon dioxide (CO2) sensor to an existing packaged single zone HVAC unit with an economizer. There is also an option to add an advanced digital economizer controller (ADEC) with DCV and CO2 sensor capability, if unit does not have one.
SWHC006-05.Base Case Description: Fixed position ventilation, with or without ADEC, corresponding to the 2022 Title 24 requirement or 20% of supply air, whichever is greater.
SWHC006-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing system must ventilate continuously during occupied hours and may not have any other device previously installed that is intended to perform DCV such as an occupancy sensor that controls ventilation rate.
The measure shall only be applied where it will result in a reduction to the overall ventilation that is supplied for the space.
The existing system must have an operable airside economizer installed. The economizer high limit must be optimized for the climate per 2022 Title 24 Table 140.4-G.
The CO2 sensor can be either mounted in the return air duct or the zone. Measure case equipment must meet the following eligibility:
  • For CO2 sensor-only offerings, existing economizer control must be a digital type control and must have the capability to control the damper in response to a CO2 sensor signal.
  • For the ADEC with CO2 sensor offerings, a program-qualifying ADEC must be installed in addition to the CO2 sensor.
Although this measure does not trigger the 2022 Title 24 code, it is recommended, but not required, to follow the practices outlined below to align with Title 24:
  • For each system with DCV, CO2 sensors shall be installed in each room with no less than one sensor per 10,000 sq.ft. of floor space. When a zone or a space is served by more than one sensor, signal from any sensor indicating that CO2 is near or at the setpoint within a space, shall trigger an increase in ventilation to the space. This requirement is not applicable if there is a return air duct-mounted CO2 sensor installed.
  • Upon detection of sensor failure, the system shall provide a signal which resets to supply the minimum quantity of outside air to the level required if DCV were not installed.
  • CO2 sensors shall be certified by the manufacturer to be accurate within ±75 ppm at a 600 and 1,000 ppm concentration when measured at sea level and 25°C, factory calibrated and certified by the manufacturer to require calibration no more frequently than once every 5 years.
Systems with existing devices, such as occupancy sensors, intended to perform DCV operation are not eligible.
The existing system must be packaged single zone DX cooling unit with gas heat, cooling only unit, or heat pump.

SWHC006-06.Offering ID: The installation of demand control ventilation (DCV) with carbon dioxide (CO2) sensor to an existing packaged single zone HVAC unit with an economizer.
SWHC006-06.Base Case Description: An existing single zone unitary cooling units with gas heat, cooling only units, and heat pumps with airside economizers, but no ability to use DCV.
SWHC006-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing system must ventilate continuously during occupied hours and may not have any other device previously installed that is intended to perform DCV such as an occupancy sensor that controls ventilation rate.
The measure shall only be applied where it will result in a reduction to the overall ventilation that is supplied for the space.
The existing system must have an operable airside economizer and a digital economizer controller with the capability to control the damper in response to a CO2 sensor signal. Otherwise, an advanced digital economizer controller (ADEC) may be installed, but this measure does not assume the cost for new ADEC in its analysis.
The economizer high limit must be optimized for the climate per 2025 Title 24 Table 140.4-G. Economizer repair and adjustment scopes may be incentivized through measure packages SWSV005 and SWSV010.
The CO2 sensor can be either mounted in the return air duct or the zone.
Systems with existing devices, such as occupancy sensors, intended to perform DCV operation are not eligible.
The existing system must be packaged single zone DX cooling unit with gas heat, cooling only unit, or heat pump.

SWHC008-02.Offering ID: The installation of a variable speed drive (VSD) on a chilled water pump (CHWP) or on a condenser water pump (CWP) in a water-cooled central plant
SWHC008-02.Base Case Description: The base case is the existing customer equipment or Title 24-compliant equipment based on building vintage. Title 24 does not require VSDs on CWPs but does require VSDs on a CHWP since 2005; hence, this measure is only eligible for CHWP vintages before 2005 including proof of existing condition.
SWHC008-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
All new variable speed drives (VSDs) and motor equipment must be UL Listed and specifically rated for the application.
For building types not listed in this measure but use water-cooled central plant systems, measure savings can be mapped under “Office Small,” which has relatively lower savings.
Constant speed Condenser Water Pump (CWP) and Chilled Water Pumps (CHWP) not already equipped with a VSD are eligible.
CHWP offerings are only eligible if the host equipment (CHWP) was installed before 2005 and the chilled water coils are controlled by three-way valves.
The simultaneous operation of the two measure offerings has interactive effects and impacts energy savings. If both measures are implemented for vintages 96 and 03, then the measures must be cascaded, and the total (aggregated) estimated savings will be slightly less than sum of the savings of the individual measure offerings. Given the vintage restrictions on the CHWP VSD measure offering, it is expected that this measure primarily will support CWP VSD implementation.
This measure excludes central plant equipment under any type of plant optimization and/or retro-commissioning at both the central plant and/or system HVAC levels; and/or central plants supporting “process” load; and/or industrial applications serving non-HVAC “space comfort” loads; and/or central plant operating with thermal energy storage. All non-standard central plant applications shall be incentivized under custom programs.
SWHC008-02.Quality Assurance:
The installation contractor or customer must:
  • Verify and document central plant vintage via “as-built” drawings or similar
  • Verify and document that existing incentivized pump operates at constant volume excluding modulating speed controls.
  • Verify and document that zone distribution system (e.g., fan coil and/or air handler unit) is controlled by 3-way CHW valves.
Successful implementation of this measure will depend greatly on the characteristics of the installed system. For a VSD addition to the CWP special attention must be given to the condenser water flow turndown capabilities of the existing chiller(s). It is imperative that chiller manufacturer selection data and retrofit information be reviewed carefully to ensure that the existing chiller(s) is (are) rated to operate at the selected minimum condenser water flow, and that the control strategy includes capability to increase the flow as needed to keep the leaving condenser temperature from exceeding an upper limit dependent on the existing chiller.
For a CHWP, the designers should evaluate the flow control at the cooling/heating coil in the air-handling units and should consider a potential retrofit from 3-way to 2-way valve and/or pressure differential sensors and controls that may be needed to adequately complete the measure.
In all cases, measure installation must meet all applicable regulations including but not limited to the current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) and the National Electrical Code.

SWHC009-04.Offering ID: A supply fan that is set to “Auto” or intermittent during unoccupied periods.
SWHC009-04.Base Case Description: Existing HVAC equipment with the supply fan operating continuously during unoccupied periods.
SWHC009-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied.
Additional technician verification of thermostat wiring and the number of cooling stages that should be performed to ensure that the first stage of cooling is dedicated to economizer operation and two-stage thermostat operation is enabled where possible.
The controller changeover setpoint should be adjusted appropriately based on the available number of thermostat cooling stages.
Contractors and technicians that implement the measure must meet all certification and training requirements in accordance with program requirements.
This measure does not apply if:
  • The rooftop unit (RTU) has a fully operational and/or non-snapdisc sensor and is adjusted to the appropriate changeover setpoint based on the number of thermostat stages available for cooling.
  • The unoccupied supply fan operation is already set to “Auto” or intermittent
  • Supply fan is switched OFF during unoccupied periods.
SWHC009-04.Important Notes:
This measure is applicable for all existing nonresidential buildings served by unitary direct expansion (DX) and split systems that do not serve process load, with the following exclusion due to the model not having these building prototypes:
  • Offering SWHC009C (cDXHP) excludes building type WRf.
  • Offering SWHC009D (cPVVG) excludes building types Asm, EPr, ERC, Gro, MLI, RFF, RSD, RtL, RtS, SCn and WRf.

SWHC009-05.Offering ID: A supply fan that is set to “Auto” or intermittent during unoccupied periods.
SWHC009-05.Base Case Description: Existing HVAC equipment with the supply fan operating continuously during unoccupied periods.
SWHC009-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied.
Additional technician verification of thermostat wiring and the number of cooling stages that should be performed to ensure that the first stage of cooling is dedicated to economizer operation and two-stage thermostat operation is enabled where possible.
The controller changeover setpoint should be adjusted appropriately based on the available number of thermostat cooling stages.
Contractors and technicians that implement the measure must meet all certification and training requirements in accordance with program requirements.
This measure does not apply if
  • The rooftop unit (RTU) has a fully operational and/or non-snap disc sensor and is adjusted to the appropriate changeover setpoint based on the number of thermostat stages available for cooling.
  • The unoccupied supply fan operation is already set to “Auto” or intermittent
  • Supply fan is switched OFF during unoccupied periods.
Process load is not eligible.
SWHC009-05.Quality Assurance: Ensure that the existing unit does not already have the supply fan in automatic mode or switched OFF during unoccupied periods.
SWHC009-05.Important Notes:
The energy savings analysis excluded the following building types due to the model not having these building prototypes:
  • Offering SWHC009C Heat Pump (cDXHP): Warehouse – Refrigerated (WRf).
  • Offering SWHC009D Variable Volume AC Unit with Gas Heat (cPVVG): Assembly (Asm), Education - Primary School (EPr), Education - Relocatable Classroom (ERC), Grocery (Gro), Manufacturing Light Industrial (MLI), Restaurant - Fast-Food (RFF), Restaurant - Sit-Down (RSD), Retail - Single-Story Large (RtL), Retail - Small (RtS), Storage - Conditioned (SCn), and Warehouse – Refrigerated (WRf).

SWHC011-03.Offering ID: A high-efficiency central natural gas furnace, with a built-in variable speed motor (VSM), meeting the specified annual fuel utilization efficiency – AFUE.
SWHC011-03.Base Case Description: A nonresidential gas furnace that meets the 2015 federal standard requirements of 81% AFUE for weatherized furnaces.
SWHC011-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Measure must meet the following eligibility:
  • For normal replacement (NR), the new furnace must replace an existing gas furnace.
  • The replacement furnace must be a weatherized unit.
  • Furnaces that meet or exceed the eligibility requirements are listed on the Furnace Rebate Table (https://www.ahridirectory.org/ahridirectory/pages/home.aspx)
  • Combustion condensate management must meet local codes and demonstrate adequate freeze protection in colder climate rooftop environments. Manufacturer installation instructions must be followed.
  • Because combustion condensate is acidic, it must be neutralized by an approved dilution or neutralizing device before discharging into the plumbing system.
  • Only residential furnaces installed in a small commercial establishment qualify for the rebate.
  • A brushless DC motor, also known as an electronically commutated motor (ECM), is also eligible.
SWHC011-03.Important Notes: There are a limited number of units on the market that meet the measure case description, and the units which meet it currently do not meet the low NOx spec to be sold in California.

SWHC012-03.Offering ID: A programmable thermostat with an integrated occupancy sensor that controls a packaged HVAC unit that serves an education classroom. The thermostat has unoccupied setback temperature control during closed hours (evenings, weekends, furlough days, and holidays) and reverts to unoccupied setback temperatures when there is no occupancy sensed for a predetermined duration.
SWHC012-03.Base Case Description: A programmable thermostat without an occupancy sensor and has unoccupied setback temperature control during closed hours.
SWHC012-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This control equipment includes, but is not limited to:
  • An occupancy sensing thermostat
  • An occupancy sensor (wired or wireless)
  • Wiring (if wired)
  • Signal receiver and power pack (if wireless)
The measure is only applicable for classrooms in primary schools (EPr), secondary schools (ESe), and re-locatable classrooms (ERC). Community colleges and universities are not eligible, unless they have relocatable classrooms with packaged HVAC units. Additionally, this measure is applicable only for the HVAC systems noted in the measure and base case tables.
SWHC012-03.Important Notes: Measure offerings are specified for various education building types and the corresponding default HVAC systems.

SWHC012-04.Offering ID: A programmable thermostat with an integrated occupancy sensor that controls a packaged HVAC unit that serves an education classroom. The thermostat has unoccupied setback temperature control during closed hours (evenings, weekends, furlough days, and holidays) and reverts to unoccupied setback temperatures when there is no occupancy sensed for a predetermined duration.
SWHC012-04.Base Case Description: A programmable thermostat without an occupancy sensor and has unoccupied setback temperature control during closed hours.
SWHC012-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This control equipment includes, but is not limited to:
  • An occupancy sensing thermostat
  • An occupancy sensor (wired or wireless)
  • Wiring (if wired)
  • Signal receiver and power pack (if wireless)
Existing occupancy sensors that are not operational or are not meeting the existing service requirements are eligible for this measure under normal replacement.
The measure is only applicable for classrooms in primary schools (EPr), secondary schools (ESe), and re-locatable classrooms (ERC). Community colleges and universities are not eligible, unless they have relocatable classrooms with packaged HVAC units.
Any existing HVAC type is eligible for this measure, however, installed thermostats must be able to control the temperature of the classroom separately from the rest of the building.
SWHC012-04.Important Notes: Measure offerings are specified for various education building types and the corresponding default HVAC systems.

SWHC013-07.Offering ID: A nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with a cooling capacity ≥65 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified minimum full-load energy efficiency ratio (EER) and/or coefficient of performance (COP).
SWHC013-07.Base Case Description: An existing nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with cooling capacity >65 kBtu/hr that meets Title 20, Title 24, and federal minimum efficiency standards.
SWHC013-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
All unitary direct expansion (DX) equipment is eligible. For the normal replacement measure application type, the replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., a heat pump for a heat pump). Likewise, for the new construction measure application type, although there is no existing system being replaced, the baseline fuel type must be the same as the equipment being installed (i.e., no fuel substitution) for the purposes of savings claims.
Because of the large number of evaporator/condenser combinations available for split systems (several thousand combinations) some averages and/or minimum efficiencies may be used for certain groups of equipment.
Equipment must use refrigerants that are compliant with all applicable federal and state standards. Residential and light commercial air conditioning (AC) and heat pump (HP) units manufactured or imported on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥700 global warming potential (GWP). Commercial AC and HP units manufactured on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥750GWP.
SWHC013-07.Quality Assurance:
The model number is used to ascertain the equipment efficiency of a package system. The equipment efficiency of a split system will be based on a combination of the condenser and evaporator model numbers provided by a distributor. The efficiency rating will then be verified according to the Air-Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute (AHRI) or Consortium for Energy Efficiency (CEE) database or manufacturers specification sheets.
Field inspections should be performed to verify the condenser/unit model number for quality assurance purposes.
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC013-06.Offering ID: A nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with a cooling capacity ≥65 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified minimum full-load energy efficiency ratio (EER) and/or coefficient of performance (COP).
SWHC013-06.Base Case Description: An existing nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with cooling capacity >65 kBtu/hr that meets Title 20, Title 24, and federal minimum efficiency standards.
SWHC013-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
All unitary direct expansion (DX) equipment is eligible. For the normal replacement measure application type, the replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., a heat pump for a heat pump). Likewise, for the new construction measure application type, although there is no existing system being replaced, the baseline fuel type must be the same as the equipment being installed (i.e., no fuel substitution) for the purposes of savings claims.
Because of the large number of evaporator/condenser combinations available for split systems (several thousand combinations) some averages and/or minimum efficiencies may be used for certain groups of equipment.
Equipment must use refrigerants that are compliant with all applicable federal and state standards. Residential and light commercial air conditioning (AC) and heat pump (HP) units manufactured or imported on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥700 global warming potential (GWP). Commercial AC and HP units manufactured on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥750GWP.
SWHC013-06.Quality Assurance:
The model number is used to ascertain the equipment efficiency of a package system. The equipment efficiency of a split system will be based on a combination of the condenser and evaporator model numbers provided by a distributor. The efficiency rating will then be verified according to the Air-Conditioning, Heating, and Refrigeration Institute (AHRI) or Consortium for Energy Efficiency (CEE) database or manufacturers specification sheets.
Field inspections should be performed to verify the condenser/unit model number for quality assurance purposes.
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC014-07.Offering ID: A nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with a cooling capacity 18 - 65 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified minimum seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER2).
SWHC014-07.Base Case Description: An existing nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with cooling capacity 18 - 65 kBtu/hr that meets federal minimum efficiency standards.
SWHC014-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Equipment must use refrigerants that are compliant with all applicable federal and state standards. Residential and light commercial air conditioning (AC) and heat pump (HP) units manufactured or imported on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥700 global warming potential (GWP). Commercial AC and HP units manufactured on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥750GWP.
For the normal replacement measure application type, the replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., a heat pump for a heat pump). Likewise, for the new construction measure application type, although there is no existing system being replaced, the baseline fuel type must be the same as the equipment being installed (i.e., no fuel substitution) for the purposes of savings claims.
Package terminal air conditioning units, which are units manufactured for installation through a wall or window and are usually ≤2 tons.
SWHC014-07.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC014-06.Offering ID: A nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with a cooling capacity 18 - 65 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified minimum seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER2).
SWHC014-06.Base Case Description: An existing nonresidential air-cooled air conditioner or heat pump with cooling capacity 18 - 65 kBtu/hr that meets federal minimum efficiency standards.
SWHC014-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Equipment must use refrigerants that are compliant with all applicable federal and state standards. Residential and light commercial air conditioning (AC) and heat pump (HP) units manufactured or imported on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥700 global warming potential (GWP). Commercial AC and HP units manufactured on or after 1/1/2025 may not use a refrigerant with ≥750GWP.
For the normal replacement measure application type, the replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., a heat pump for a heat pump). Likewise, for the new construction measure application type, although there is no existing system being replaced, the baseline fuel type must be the same as the equipment being installed (i.e., no fuel substitution) for the purposes of savings claims.
Package terminal air conditioning units, which are units manufactured for installation through a wall or window and are usually ≤2 tons.
SWHC014-06.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC018-04.Offering ID: An installation of a variable speed drive (VSD) and associated controls on an existing constant speed HVAC supply or return fan in a variable volume air handling unit.
SWHC018-04.Base Case Description: An AC-powered motor connected to forward curved fans with discharge dampers, otherwise known as a damper-controlled variable air volume (VAV) system.
SWHC018-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligibility requirements include:
  • The fan motor rated hp must be ≤100 hp.
  • The existing fan motor must operate at constant speed.
  • As an add-on equipment installation, the VSD must be applied to the HVAC supply or return air system.
  • Any other throttling devices, such as inlet vanes or bypass dampers, and throttling valves, must be removed or permanently disabled.
  • If the existing constant speed fan motor must be replaced because it cannot accommodate a VSD, such as it not being inverter rated, then a new fan motor may be installed that works with the VSD. This is allowed because fan motor replacements are considered repairs and do not trigger Title 24 code. However, no additional energy savings shall be claimed through this measure package for increased fan motor efficiency and the fan motor replacement cost shall not be considered as part of the measure install cost.
The following are not eligible:
  • Replacement of multiple-speed or variable speed motors (VSM).
  • Variable frequency drives on cooling tower fans.
  • Exhaust fans.

SWHC018-05.Offering ID: An installation of a variable speed drive (VSD) and associated controls on an existing constant speed HVAC supply or return fan.
SWHC018-05.Base Case Description: A constant speed AC-powered motor connected to forward curved fans with discharge dampers, otherwise known as a damper-controlled variable air volume (VAV) system.
SWHC018-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligibility requirements include:
  • The fan motor rated hp must be ≤100 hp.
  • The existing fan motor must operate at constant speed.
  • As an add-on equipment installation, the VSD must be applied to the HVAC supply or return air system.
  • Any other throttling devices, such as inlet vanes or bypass dampers, and throttling valves, must be removed or permanently disabled.
  • If the existing constant speed fan motor must be replaced because it cannot accommodate a VSD, such as it not being inverter rated, then a new fan motor may be installed that works with the VSD. This is allowed because fan motor replacements are considered repairs and do not trigger Title 24 code. However, no additional energy savings shall be claimed through this measure package for increased fan motor efficiency and the fan motor replacement cost shall not be considered as part of the measure install cost.
The following are not eligible:
  • Multiple-speed or variable speed motors (VSM)
  • Exhaust fans.
  • Installations in constant air volume (CAV) systems

SWHC020-04.Offering ID: A nonresidential constant speed air-cooled (screw) chiller meeting the Path A full load efficiency (in energy efficiency ratio - EER) and integrated part load value (IPLV) specified in the Offering ID table.
SWHC020-04.Base Case Description: A constant speed air-cooled (screw) chiller that meets 2022 Title 24 minimum efficiency requirements in both full load and integrated part load conditions.
SWHC020-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The energy and demand impacts of this measure are applicable only to commercial buildings with chiller equipment serving space comfort. Other chiller measures serving non-space comfort and/or processes and/or industrial applications are not eligible.
In a central or multi-chiller cooling plant with lead-lag configuration, "lead” chillers are not eligible. Energy impacts are determined with the assumption that the chiller is not a lead chiller.
Per Section 140.4 (j) of the 2022 California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title-24), chilled water plants shall not have more than 300 tons provided by air cooled chillers, except where the water quality at the building site fails to meet the manufacturer specifications for the use of water-cooled chillers, chillers that are used to charge a thermal energy storage system with a design temperature of less than 40°F, or systems serving healthcare facilities.

SWHC020-05.Offering ID: A nonresidential constant speed air-cooled (screw) chiller meeting the Path A full load efficiency (in energy efficiency ratio - EER) and integrated part load value (IPLV) specified in the Offering ID table.
SWHC020-05.Base Case Description: A constant speed air-cooled (screw) chiller that meets 2022 Title 24 minimum efficiency requirements in both full load and integrated part load conditions.
SWHC020-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The energy and demand impacts of this measure are applicable only to commercial buildings with chiller equipment serving space comfort. Other chiller measures serving non-space comfort and/or processes and/or industrial applications are not eligible.
In a central or multi-chiller cooling plant with lead-lag configuration, "lead” chillers are not eligible. Energy impacts are determined with the assumption that the chiller is not a lead chiller.
Per Section 140.4 (j) of the 2022 California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title-24), chilled water plants shall not have more than 300 tons provided by air cooled chillers, except where the water quality at the building site fails to meet the manufacturer specifications for the use of water-cooled chillers, chillers that are used to charge a thermal energy storage system with a design temperature of less than 40°F, or systems serving healthcare facilities.

SWHC023-04.Offering ID: The installation of a variable frequency drive (VFD) to an existing packaged single-zone HVAC unit with an economizer to provide cooling when conditions permit. The measure also includes the options to add NEMA premium efficiency motor, permanent magnet motor (PMM), advanced digital economizer controller (ADEC), and CO2 sensor.
SWHC023-04.Base Case Description: The existing single-zone package HVAC unit with a functional economizer, a standard induction motor, and no VFD. The measure includes the options to replace the induction motor with NEMA or PMM motor.
SWHC023-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure must replace existing motor for some offerings with the addition of controls for existing units. Contractors and technicians that implement these measures must ensure that the existing unit does not already have these measures.
Terms and conditions are noted below. Other terms and conditions are set by individual programs.
  • The existing system must be packaged single zone DX cooling unit with gas heat, cooling only unit, or heat pump.
  • The existing system must have a constant volume supply fan.
  • The existing system must have an operable airside economizer installed, and economizer high limit must be optimized for the climate per 2022 Title 24 Table 140.4-G.
  • Maintenance, and repairs to economizer should be completed prior to or in conjunction with this measure.
SWHC023-04.Quality Assurance:
This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied.
Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that the fan speed is controlled appropriately.

SWHC023-05.Offering ID: The installation of a variable frequency drive (VFD) to an existing packaged single-zone HVAC unit with an economizer to provide cooling when conditions permit. The measure also includes the options to add a new code efficient inverter rated motor or a permanent magnet motor (PMM) motor.
SWHC023-05.Base Case Description: The existing single-zone package HVAC unit with a functional economizer and single speed supply fan motor.
SWHC023-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Existing system requirements include:
  • The existing system must have a constant volume supply fan. Existing two-speed or multi-speed fans are not eligible.
  • The existing system must have an operable airside economizer installed. Maintenance and repairs to economizer should be completed prior to or in conjunction with this measure. Economizer repair and adjustment scopes may be incentivized through measure packages SWSV005 and SWSV010.
Installation requirement:
  • “Existing motors” offerings must only install the new VFD, not a new motor.
  • &“New standard efficient inverter rated motor” and “New PMM” offerings must have:
    • A new code efficient inverter rated motor. Although this measure offering was designed for code efficient induction motors, new non-induction motors that do not meet the requirements for the PMM offerings can be eligible for these offerings, so long as they meet or exceed code efficiency, or
    • A new high efficiency permanent magnet motor compatible with VFDs. This motor must also exceed federal codes.
  • The installed VFDs must be programmed to vary motor speed based on packaged unit's operational requirements. The motors cannot be set to operate at a single speed.
Although not required as part of this measure package, the following recommendations are being provided as best practices for implementation.
  • Economizer high limit should be optimized for the climate per 2025 Title 24 Table 140.4-G.
  • Ensure proper setup of the damper limits and fan speeds to provide ventilation in accordance with 2025 Title 24.
  • Verify the total unit airflow for at least one fan speed and verify the percentage of outdoor air for each unit operating mode. This includes heating and cooling stages, as well as the ventilation-only mode.
  • Minimum airflows should be set such appropriate heat transfer is possible and damage due to coil freezing is avoided.
The VFD (and motor if applicable) must be installed on a packaged or split single-zone DX cooling unit with a gas furnace (cDXGF), cooling only unit (cDXOH), or heat pump (cDXHP).
This measure is not eligible for systems that serve process or refrigeration loads.
SWHC023-05.Quality Assurance:
This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied.
Verified the VFD logic is functioning and modifying fan speed as intended.

SWHC024-04.Offering ID: A cogged (or notched) V-belt for nonresidential package rooftop HVAC systems.
SWHC024-04.Base Case Description: A smooth fan belt for nonresidential package rooftop HVAC systems.
SWHC024-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A cogged V-belt can be installed on supply air and return air fans in rooftop units that do not already have a cogged V-belt.
Only “A” and “B” V-belts, typically used in rooftop units (RTUs), are applicable for this measure. The “A” belt is ½ inch wide and 5/16 inch thick. The “B” belt is larger, 21/32 inches wide and 12/32 inches thick so it can carry more power. “L”, “C”, and “D” type cogged V-belts are not eligible.
SWHC024-04.Quality Assurance: A part/trade number or specifications must be submitted with projects to verify eligibility.

SWHC024-05.Offering ID: A cogged (or notched) V-belt or timing belt for nonresidential package rooftop HVAC systems.
SWHC024-05.Base Case Description: A smooth fan belt for nonresidential package rooftop HVAC systems.
SWHC024-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A cogged V-belt can be installed on supply air and return air fans in rooftop units that do not already have a cogged V-belt.
Only “A” and “B” V-belts, typically used in rooftop units (RTUs), are applicable for this measure. The “A” belt is ½ inch wide and 5/16 inch thick. The “B” belt is larger, 21/32 inches wide and 12/32 inches thick so it can carry more power. “L”, “C”, and “D” type cogged V-belts are not eligible.
Timing belts must have matching pulleys to be eligible.
Replacement of broken/non-functioning cogged V-belts or timing belts is eligible for normal replacement (NR).
SWHC024-05.Quality Assurance: A part/trade number or specifications must be submitted with projects to verify eligibility.

SWHC024-06.Offering ID: A cogged (or notched) V-belt or timing belt for nonresidential package rooftop HVAC systems.
SWHC024-06.Base Case Description: A smooth fan belt for nonresidential package rooftop HVAC systems.
SWHC024-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The belt(s) must be installed on the supply-air and/or return-air fan(s) of belt-driven rooftop/packaged units that do not already have a cogged V-belt or a timing belt. Replacement of broken or non-functioning cogged V-belts or timing belts is eligible as Normal Replacement (NR).
For cogged V-belt, only “A” and “B” section belts (the two types typically used in rooftop/packaged units) are eligible for this measure. “L”, “C”, and “D” type cogged V-belts are not eligible.
Timing belts must have matching pulleys to be eligible.
SWHC024-06.Quality Assurance: A part/trade number or specifications must be submitted with projects to verify eligibility.

SWHC027-05.Offering ID: A through-the-wall, self-contained, and less than 2 tons (≤24 kBtu/hr) packaged terminal air conditioner (PTAC) or packaged terminal heat pump (PTHP) that has an energy efficiency rating (EER) and, for PTHP heating mode, a coefficient of performance (COP) that are 20% higher than the base case.
SWHC027-05.Base Case Description: A through-the-wall, self-contained, and less than 2 tons (≤24 kBtu/hr) PTAC or PTHP unit that meets 2022 Title 24 minimum efficiency.
SWHC027-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
PTAC units are eligible only without gas heating. Products with gas heating are not eligible for this measure.
PTACs and PTHPs are typically used to condition small areas that have wide swings in occupancy levels. As a result, they are most commonly installed in hotels and motels where individual guest room zone control is necessary.
Ductless mini-split AC and HP are not eligible.
SWHC027-05.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC027-06.Offering ID: A through-the-wall, self-contained, and less than 2 tons (≤24 kBtu/hr) packaged terminal air conditioner (PTAC) or packaged terminal heat pump (PTHP) that has an energy efficiency rating (EER) and, for PTHP heating mode, a coefficient of performance (COP) that are 20% higher than the base case.
SWHC027-06.Base Case Description: A through-the-wall, self-contained, and less than 2 tons (≤24 kBtu/hr) PTAC or PTHP unit that meets 2025 Title 24 minimum efficiency.
SWHC027-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
PTAC units are eligible only without gas heating. Products with gas heating are not eligible for this measure.
PTACs and PTHPs are typically used to condition small areas that have wide swings in occupancy levels. As a result, they are most commonly installed in hotels and motels where individual guest room zone control is necessary.
Ductless mini-split AC and HP are not eligible.
SWHC027-06.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC029-04.Offering ID: A residential central HVAC system with an evaporator fan controller that uses built-in logic or a manually set time delay to delay the evaporator fan cycle off time. The built-in control logic is based on equipment operation and cycling to improve HVAC system efficiency.
SWHC029-04.Base Case Description: A residential central HVAC system with standard fan controls and operation without a manual or logic-based evaporator fan controller
SWHC029-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Both automated (logic-based) fan controllers and manually set time-delay fan controllers are eligible. For manual fan controllers, the delay timer must be programmed to four minutes in order to maximize savings associated with the fan-delay, in accordance with the results of the SCE ET Study titled Effects of Delaying Evaporator Fan Cycle Off Time for Residential Air-conditioning Units.
The installation of a manually set time-delay fan controller requires the invoice and/or installation contract that verifies the equipment was installed by an approved California (C-20) HVAC licensed contractor.
This measure only applies to a fully functional central residential air conditioning unit equipped with cooling/evaporator (DX) coils.
All installations shall comply with all current applicable regulations, code, and standards including (but not limited to) the California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), the California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), and the National Electrical Code (NEC).
The following are not eligible:
  • A fan controller used for solely optimizing and improving heating efficiency including those for gas furnaces.
  • The existing air conditioning system equipped with a previously installed add-on retrofit fan-delay controller.
SWHC029-04.Important Notes: In the eTRM, measure is only eligible for installation on HVAC systems manufactured prior to 2010. HVAC systems manufactured in 2010 and thereafter are expected to include some level of fan control delay capability as indicated by a high level industry standard practice (ISP) effort supported as part of this measure update.

SWHC031-03.Offering ID: A residential central gas furnace with a variable speed motor (VSM) meeting the specified annual fuel utilization efficiency (AFUE) rating.
SWHC031-03.Base Case Description: A non-condensing central gas furnace that meets the minimum AFUE of 80% and minimum code fan energy rating (FER) rating for a furnace with input capacity ≤225 kBtu/hr. PSC motors are used prior to 2019; the 2014 federal code requires VSM, which is a electronically commutated motor (ECM).
SWHC031-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A list of eligible furnaces can be found at https://www.ahridirectory.org/ ahridirectory/pages/home.aspx(search for qualifying products that meet or exceed the measure case specification).
Used or rebuilt equipment is not eligible.
Replacement of an electric heater with a gas heater (fuel substitution) is not eligible.
SWHC031-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit meets minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWHC031-07.Offering ID: A residential high-efficiency central gas furnace with an electronically commutated motor (ECM) meeting the specified annual fuel utilization efficiency (AFUE) rating.
SWHC031-07.Base Case Description: A non-condensing central gas furnace that meets the minimum AFUE of 80% and minimum code fan energy rating (FER) rating for a furnace with input capacity ≤225 kBtu/hr. Since 2019, the U.S. Department of Energy has mandated that new furnace manufacturers use efficient EC motors, making them the standard baseline.
