SWHC038-04.Offering ID: A central brushless fan motor (BFM, or DC Motor) specifically configured as a drop-in retrofit for a standard permanent split capacitor (PSC) residential fan (blower) motor serving a central HVAC system.
SWHC038-04.Base Case Description: A permanent split capacitor (PSC) residential fan motor serving a HVAC system meeting 2022 Title 24 fan performance (W/cfm) requirement.
SWHC038-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The HVAC system must be functional/operable and drawing power.
The HVAC system shall be capable of delivering a supply air flow rate of ≥350 cfm/ton. The airflow requirement ensures that the refrigerant system can be properly diagnosed and charged as part of a maintenance service. If the system is not delivering 350 cfm/ton upon initial inspection, an assessment should be made to determine if the system will be able to deliver 350 cfm/ton by implementing repairs related to airflow. If it is determined that the supply fan and duct system in place do not have the capability to deliver at least 350 cfm/ton with or without airflow repairs, the savings in this measure are invalid.
The replacement brushless fan motor (BFM) motor capacity (hp or W) and rated voltage shall match that of existing fan motor being replaced.
The replacement brushless fan motor and motor controls assembly shall be “UL Listed.”
The replacement brushless fan motor shall have a warranty of at least two years from date of installation.
The measure shall include HVAC system start-up. During the start-up, the installer shall ensure that system maintain programmed level airflows under both cooling and/or heating and that these are adequate.
The installation (as applicable) shall comply with all applicable regulations including but not limited to California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title-24), California Electrical Code, and NEC, and any applicable local jurisdiction regulations. See Code Requirements.
This measure only supports HVAC (evaporator) fan motors with nominal capacities of ½-hp or less typically serving HVAC systems with nominal capacities of 3.5 ton (or less).
The measure applies to units with shaded pole (SHP) or permanent split capacitor (PSC) motors currently installed. The replacement motor is to be a brushless direct current motor with selectable speed control designed to replace a PSC motor in a residential direct drive fan application.
SWHC038-04.Important Notes: For this measure, a brushless fan motor is compared to a PSC motor as if they were both installed on identical systems (duct and furnace) at the same air flow rate and external static pressure.

SWHC038-05.Offering ID: A central brushless fan motor (BFM, or DC Motor) specifically configured as a drop-in retrofit for a standard permanent split capacitor (PSC) residential fan (blower) motor serving a central HVAC system.
SWHC038-05.Base Case Description: A permanent split capacitor (PSC) residential fan motor serving a HVAC system meeting 2025 Title 24 fan performance (W/cfm) requirement.
SWHC038-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The square footage served by each HVAC unit shall be confirmed and should fall within the below range of tonnage to square footage. The weighted average can be taken given the total square footage of the home and the provided HVAC unit information to find the approximate square footage served by the unit on where the BFM was installed.
  • 1 ton: 400 - 600 square feet
  • 2 ton: 800 - 1,200 square feet
  • 3 ton: 1,200 - 1,800 square feet
  • 4 ton: 1,600 - 2,400 square feet
  • 5 ton: 2,000 - 3,000+ square feet
The replacement brushless fan motor and motor controls assembly shall be “UL Listed.”
This measure only supports HVAC (evaporator) fan motors with nominal capacities of ½-hp or less.
The measure applies to units with shaded pole (SHP) or permanent split capacitor (PSC) motors currently installed. As stated previously, the replacement motor is to be a brushless direct current motor with selectable speed control designed to replace a PSC motor in a residential direct drive fan application.
SWHC038-05.Important Notes: For this measure, a brushless fan motor is compared to a PSC motor as if they were both installed on identical systems (duct and furnace) at the same air flow rate and external static pressure.

SWHC039-08.Offering ID: The installation of a residential smart thermostat with two-way communication and automatic scheduling capabilities
SWHC039-08.Base Case Description: A setback programmable thermostat per the Impact Evaluation of Smart Thermostats. Residential Sector - Program Year 2018.
SWHC039-08.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Smart thermostat must be in full compliance with the ENERGY STAR® Program Requirements and Product Specification for Connected Thermostats products Version 1.0.
The customer must use the thermostat to control heating and/or cooling equipment.
Eligible HVAC system types include:
  • Central air conditioning with gas forced air furnace
  • Central heat pump
  • Central air conditioning with heating systems other than gas furnace. These include, but are not limited to, electric forced air furnaces, hydronic heating, wall or floor gas furnaces, and wall or floor electric furnaces. No savings have been explicitly provided for heating systems other than gas furnaces in Resolution E-5221. Savings for the heat pump offering adopt the cooling savings from the air conditioning with gas furnace measure with no claimed savings for heating. Similarly, when other heating systems are paired with central air conditioning, those should claim the heat pump offering (cooling only) savings.
  • Central gas furnaces without cooling. These offerings can claim gas savings only.
  • Primary heating systems other than gas furnaces or heat pumps, such as electric resistance and hydronic heating systems, are excluded if they are not paired with a central DX cooling system.

If a common “c-wire” is not available as part of the low-voltage control wiring, it is required that the installation of the measure includes a common wire adapter. While most newer home vintages may include the “c-wire,” many of the older home vintages may not. All installations shall fully comply with the manufacturer’s written installation requirements.
SWHC039-08.Quality Assurance:
Ensure that the thermostat is installed in an eligible home and is attached to the type of HVAC equipment that is being incentivized, whether it is for natural gas or electricity savings.
Confirm that the customer has a newly purchased smart thermostat and confirm the purchase date is on or after the program start date.
Per Impact Evaluation of Smart Thermostats. Residential Sector - Program Year 2019, program implementers are recommended to “support Direct Install programs to include or strengthen contractor training and customer education about settings (auto-away) and device use (pre-heating/pre-cooling) that will help save energy.” Confirm that the smart thermostat is connected to Wi-Fi or has internet access upon installation.

SWHC041-05.Offering ID: A software-controlled SRM in an HVAC packaged unit. The normal replacement (NR) MAT is applicable to fan motors between 1 hp to 3 hp (nameplate) and the accelerated replacement (AR) MAT is applicable to fan motors between 1 hp to 15 hp (nameplate). The motor horsepower for NR offerings is capped at 3 hp because above 3 hp SRMs have similar nominal efficiencies as the baseline NEMA Premium efficiency motors. The motor horsepower for AR offerings is capped at the largest commercially available SRM motor capacity found in the market.
SWHC041-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a single speed fan induction motor and VFD controller in a Title 24 compliant HVAC packaged unit. The standard base case fan motor is an induction NEMA Premium® efficiency motor.
SWHC041-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The product must be a switch reluctance motor and must be programmed to vary its speed based on packaged unit operational requirements. The motors cannot be set to operate at a single speed.
For NR offerings, the project must use a 1 hp to 3 hp switch reluctance motor to replace the existing 1 hp to 3 hp induction supply fan motor in a packaged HVAC system. Replacing a 2-speed fan motor is eligible for NR offerings and can claim savings based on a VSD fan baseline.
For AR offerings:
  • Host packaged unit equipment must have a single speed fan motor.
  • Induction fan motor must be within 1 hp to 15 hp.

For all offerings, the SRM must be installed in a packaged or split air conditioning system with a gas furnace.
The new motor and controls must be tested for the relevant UL safety standards by a Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratory (NRTL), including, but not limited to UL, Intertek, or NSF. The installation of this measure shall meet all applicable regulations, including but not limited to the current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), federal code, and the National Electrical Code® (NEC).
The following are ineligible:
  • Packaged and split heat pump systems
  • Packaged and split air conditioning systems without a gas furnace
  • Supply fan motors rated <1 hp and >3 hp (nominal) for NR offerings
  • For AR offering:
    • Supply fan motors rated <1 hp and >15 hp (nominal).
    • Base case fans with variable speed or 2-speed motors.
  • Other fans, such as the return fan or outdoor air fan of a packaged HVAC unit.
SWHC041-05.Quality Assurance: Commissioning or operating documentation, including, but not limited to, screenshots from control software, sequence of operations, or a controls description from the installer can be provided to support measure offering requirements.

SWHC041-06.Offering ID: A software-controlled switch reluctance motor (SRM) in an HVAC packaged unit. The normal replacement (NR) MAT is applicable to fan motors between 1 hp to 3 hp (nameplate) and the accelerated replacement (AR) MAT is applicable to fan motors between 1 hp to 15 hp (nameplate). The motor horsepower for NR offerings is capped at 3 hp because above 3 hp SRMs have similar nominal efficiencies as the baseline NEMA Premium efficiency motors. The motor horsepower for AR offerings is capped at the largest commercially available SRM motor capacity found in the market.
SWHC041-06.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a single speed fan induction motor; the motor efficiency is representative of the minimum federal motor efficiency during the installation year of the packaged unit (2011). The standard base case is a federal code efficient supply fan induction motor and VFD controller in a HVAC packaged unit that meets the Title 24 requirement.
SWHC041-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The product must be a switch reluctance motor and must be programmed to vary its speed based on packaged unit operational requirements. The motors cannot be set to operate at a single speed.
For NR offerings, the project must replace a 1 hp to 3 hp induction supply fan motor in a packaged HVAC system. The measure may replace a variable speed or two-speed fan motor; note that a two-speed motor can claim savings based on a variable speed baseline.
For AR offerings:
  • Host packaged unit equipment must have a single speed fan motor.
  • Induction fan motor must be 1 hp to 15 hp and be replaced with an SRM that is 1 hp to 15 hp

The SRM must be installed in a packaged or split air conditioning system. The existing system must be a packaged or split single zone AC unit with a gas furnace (cDXGF), cooling only unit (cDXOH), or heat pump (cDXHP).
The new motor and controls must be tested for the relevant UL safety standards by a Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratory (NRTL), including, but not limited to UL, Intertek, or NSF. The installation of this measure shall meet all applicable regulations, including but not limited to the current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), federal code, and the National Electrical Code® (NEC).
The following are ineligible:
  • Supply fan motors rated <1 hp and >3 hp (nominal) for NR offerings
  • For AR offering:
    • Supply fan motors rated <1 hp and >15 hp (nominal).
    • Base case fans with variable speed or 2-speed motors.
  • Other fans, such as the return fan or outdoor air fan of a packaged HVAC unit.
  • Systems that serve process or refrigeration loads.
SWHC041-06.Quality Assurance: Commissioning or operating documentation, including, but not limited to, screenshots from control software, sequence of operations, or a controls description from the installer can be provided to support measure requirement.

SWHC041-08.Offering ID: A software-controlled switch reluctance motor (SRM) in an HVAC packaged unit used in a commercial or industrial building. The normal replacement (NR) MAT is applicable to fan motors between 1 hp to 3 hp (nameplate) and the accelerated replacement (AR) MAT is applicable to fan motors between 1 hp to 15 hp (nameplate). The motor horsepower for NR offerings is capped at 3 hp because above 3 hp SRMs have similar nominal efficiencies as the baseline NEMA Premium efficiency motors. The motor horsepower for AR offerings is capped at the largest commercially available SRM motor capacity found in the market.
SWHC041-08.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a single speed fan induction motor; the motor efficiency is representative of the minimum federal motor efficiency during the installation year of the packaged unit (2011). The standard base case is a federal code efficient supply fan induction motor and VFD controller in a HVAC packaged unit that meets the Title 24 requirement.
SWHC041-08.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The product must be a switch reluctance motor and must be programmed to vary its speed based on packaged unit operational requirements. The motors cannot be set to operate at a single speed.
For NR offerings, the project must replace a 1 hp to 3 hp induction supply fan motor in a packaged HVAC system. The measure may replace a variable speed or two-speed fan motor; note that a two-speed motor can claim savings based on a variable speed baseline.
For AR offerings:
  • Host packaged unit equipment must have a single speed fan motor.
  • Induction fan motor must be 1 hp to 15 hp and be replaced with an SRM that is 1 hp to 15 hp

The SRM must be installed in a packaged or split air conditioning system. The existing system must be a packaged or split single zone AC unit with a gas furnace (cDXGF), cooling only unit (cDXOH), or heat pump (cDXHP).
The new motor and controls must be tested for the relevant UL safety standards by a Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratory (NRTL), including, but not limited to UL, Intertek, or NSF. The installation of this measure shall meet all applicable regulations, including but not limited to the current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), federal code, and the National Electrical Code® (NEC).
The following are ineligible:
  • Supply fan motors rated <1 hp and >3 hp (nominal) for NR offerings
  • For AR offering:
    • Supply fan motors rated <1 hp and >15 hp (nominal).
    • Base case fans with variable speed or 2-speed motors.
  • Other fans, such as the return fan or outdoor air fan of a packaged HVAC unit.
  • Systems that serve process or refrigeration loads.
SWHC041-08.Quality Assurance: Commissioning or operating documentation, including, but not limited to, screenshots from control software, sequence of operations, or a controls description from the installer can be provided to support measure requirement.

SWHC042-04.Offering ID: An evaporative pre-cooler installed on the condenser air intake of an existing or new HVAC packaged unit.
SWHC042-04.Base Case Description: An existing or new Title 24 compliant HVAC packaged unit on an existing building without an existing evaporative pre-cooler
SWHC042-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The HVAC packaged units and the evaporative pre-cooler system and controls must meet the following requirements:
  • For the add-on equipment (AOE) measure, the existing packaged unit on which the evaporative pre-cooler system will be installed must be less than 10 years old.
  • A water treatment system must be used in conjunction with the evaporative pre-cooler system to maintain water quality and performance.
  • Real time monitoring of the evaporative pre-cooler system must be implemented to ensure the system is working properly.
  • As a standard procedure, the contractor must perform condenser coil cleaning prior to installation of the pre-cooler system.

The eligible climate zones are dependent on the building type:
  • CZ10, CZ11, CZ12, CZ13, CZ14, and CZ15: All commercial buildings excluding Hospital (Hsp), Nursing Home (Nrs), and Refrigerated Warehouse (WRf)
  • CZ11, CZ13, CZ14, and CZ15: Hospital (Hsp), Nursing Home (Nrs), and Refrigerated Warehouse (WRf)

SWHC043-07.Offering ID: A unitary air-cooled AC unit with a multi-speed indoor fan and a compressor with either 1) variable speed compressor(s), 2) multiple-step compressor(s), or 3) multiple compressors operating on a single refrigeration circuit (tandem compressors). The minimum energy efficiency ratio (EER) and integrated energy efficiency ratio (IEER) for each capacity category are specified below.
SWHC043-07.Base Case Description: A unitary air-cooled AC equipment with an EER/IEER that meets the minimum Title 20, Title 24, and federal code requirements.
SWHC043-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Products must use refrigerants that are compliant with all applicable federal and state standards (see Code Requirements section).
The AC equipment must include advanced compressor capacity control (either a variable speed compressor, tandem compressors on a single refrigeration circuit, or multiple-step compressors).
The AC equipment must include a multi-speed (three steps or greater) indoor fan.
Unitary heat pumps (HPs) and unitary ACs with electric resistance heating for commercial applications are not eligible.
Central chilled water hydronic systems and domestic hot water systems are not eligible.

SWHC043-06.Offering ID: A unitary air-cooled AC unit with a multi-speed indoor fan and a compressor with either 1) variable speed compressor(s), 2) multiple-step compressor(s), or 3) multiple compressors operating on a single refrigeration circuit (tandem compressors). The minimum energy efficiency ratio (EER) and integrated energy efficiency ratio (IEER) for each capacity category are specified below.
SWHC043-06.Base Case Description: A unitary air-cooled AC equipment with an EER/IEER that meets the minimum Title 20, Title 24, and federal code requirements.
SWHC043-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Products must use refrigerants that are compliant with all applicable federal and state standards (see Code Requirements section).
The AC equipment must include advanced compressor capacity control (either a variable speed compressor, tandem compressors on a single refrigeration circuit, or multiple-step compressors).
The AC equipment must include a multi-speed (three steps or greater) indoor fan.
Unitary heat pumps (HPs) and unitary ACs with electric resistance heating for commercial applications are not eligible.
Central chilled water hydronic systems and domestic hot water systems are not eligible.

SWHC044-04.Offering ID: A ductless heat pump (mini or multi-split) with cooling capacity less than 65,000 Btu/hr meeting the specified seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER or SEER2) and heating season performance factor (HSPF or HSPF2) requirement.
SWHC044-04.Base Case Description: A residential HVAC system with a natural gas gravity wall furnace and an electric window air conditioning unit, or a system with only a natural gas gravity wall furnace and no existing cooling unit or load. The base case annual fuel utilization efficiency (AFUE) rating and energy efficiency ratio (EER) meet Title 20 and federal code requirement.
SWHC044-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The mini-split heat pump unit must meet or exceed the SEER2 and HSPF2 requirements (or SEER and HSPF requirements if applicable) outlined in the Measure Case Description section. Note that due to a code update effective January 1, 2023, the previously offered SEER 15 efficiency tier has been retired. The mini-split heat pump must be a model suitable for residential use with an appropriate capacity (not more than 7.5 tons cooling capacity) and the customer's electrical service (typically single phase).
The existing furnace and air conditioning equipment (if applicable) must be removed and disposed of rather than refurbished and sold. Existing gas line(s) serving removed gas equipment must be capped off and removed. The installation of the measure must be performed by all state and local regulations, including but not limited to the California Building Standards Code (Title 24, which includes e.g. the California Building Code, California Energy Code, and California Mechanical Code), the Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), the National Electrical Code (NEC), and EPA regulations (40 CFR Part 82, Subpart F).
This measure is not eligible if there is no natural gas to electricity fuel substitution for space heating. Ground up constructions are not eligible.
SWHC044-04.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
SEER2 vs SEER Efficiency Ratings: The test procedure for EER/SEER/HSPF ratings is defined in Appendix M to 10 CFR 430 Subpart B. In 2022, manufacturers began rolling out new equipment rated and labeled according to a new test procedure defined in Appendix M1, with ratings labeled differently to avoid confusion (i.e., SEER2 or SEERM1). The Appendix M1 ratings are the basis for compliance with new code minimum efficiency requirements that take effect on January 1, 2023. A given piece of equipment may be rated under both test procedures with different test result values, so some equipment may bear ratings with both labels, and the two ratings should not be compared.

SWHC044-05.Offering ID: A ductless heat pump (mini or multi-split) with cooling capacity less than 65,000 Btu/hr meeting the specified seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER or SEER2) and heating season performance factor (HSPF or HSPF2) requirement.
SWHC044-05.Base Case Description: A residential HVAC system with a natural gas gravity wall furnace and an electric window air conditioning unit, or a system with only a natural gas gravity wall furnace and no existing cooling unit or load. The base case annual fuel utilization efficiency (AFUE) rating and energy efficiency ratio (EER) meet Title 20 and federal code requirement.
SWHC044-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The installed equipment must meet the requirements for the selected measure offering ID as follows:
  • The ductless mini-split or multi-split heat pump must meet or exceed the SEER2 and HSPF2 requirements (or SEER and HSPF requirements if applicable)
  • Note: due to a code update effective January 1, 2023, the previously offered SEER 15 efficiency tier has been retired. The mini-split heat pump must be a model suitable for residential use with an appropriate capacity (not more than 7.5 tons cooling capacity) and the customer's electrical service (typically single phase).

The base equipment type must match the description (wall furnace or Window AC and wall furnace).
The existing furnace and air conditioning equipment (if applicable) must be removed and disposed of rather than refurbished and sold.
Existing gas line(s) serving the base equipment must be capped off and/or removed.
The installation of the measure must be performed by all state and local regulations including but not limited to the California Building Standards Code (Title 24, which includes e.g. the California Building Code, California Energy Code, and California Mechanical Code), the Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), and the National Electrical Code (NEC).
Refrigerant must be handled and disposed of in accordance with EPA regulation, 40 CFR Part 82, Subpart F.
This measure is not eligible when there is no natural gas to electricity fuel substitution for space heating.
SWHC044-05.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
SEER2 vs SEER Efficiency Ratings: The test procedure for EER/SEER/HSPF ratings is defined in Appendix M to 10 CFR 430 Subpart B. In 2022, manufacturers began rolling out new equipment rated and labeled according to a new test procedure defined in Appendix M1, with ratings labeled differently to avoid confusion (i.e., SEER2 or SEERM1). The Appendix M1 ratings are the basis for compliance with new code minimum efficiency requirements that take effect on January 1, 2023. A given piece of equipment may be rated under both test procedures with different test result values, so some equipment may bear ratings with both labels, and the two ratings should not be compared.

SWHC045-03.Offering ID: An all-electric residential central air-source heat pump with optional electric resistance heating if supplemental heating is required. The measure case must meet the specified seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER or SEER2) and heating season performance factor (HSPF or HSPF2) requirement.
SWHC045-03.Base Case Description: The existing case is a residential split air-conditioner with SEER = 13 & gas furnace with thermal efficiency (TE) 80%. The standard base case is a system that meets all code minimum requirements (i.e., SEER2 = 14.3 or SEER = 15 & gas furnace TE = 80%).
SWHC045-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing furnace and air conditioning equipment (if applicable) must be removed and disposed of rather than refurbished and sold. Existing gas line(s) serving removed gas equipment must be capped off and removed refrigerant must be handled and disposed in accordance with all state and local regulations, including but not limited to the California Building Standards Code (Title 24, which includes e.g. the California Building Code, California Energy Code, and California Mechanical Code), the Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), the National Electrical Code (NEC), and EPA regulations (40 CFR Part 82, Subpart F).
Existing equipment incentivized under accelerated replacement (AR) must be fully operational and serving intended application under qualified building type.
SWHC045-03.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
In the eTRM, the “Gas Furnace Only” permutations failed the CPUC Fuel Substitution Test for CZ15 and do not have climate zone-specific values reported.
SEER2 vs SEER Efficiency Ratings: The test procedure for EER/SEER/HSPF ratings is defined in Appendix M to 10 CFR 430 Subpart B. In 2022, manufacturers began rolling out new equipment rated and labeled according to a new test procedure defined in Appendix M1, with ratings labeled differently to avoid confusion (i.e., SEER2 or SEERM1). The Appendix M1 ratings are the basis for compliance with new code minimum efficiency requirements that took effect on January 1, 2023. A given piece of equipment may be rated under both test procedures with different test result values, so some equipment may bear ratings with both labels, and the two ratings should not be compared.

SWHC045-04.Offering ID: An electric residential central air-source heat pump that utilizes auxiliary heat (i.e., electric resistance heating) when additional heating is required. The measure case must meet the specified seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER2) and heating season performance factor (HSPF2) requirement.
SWHC045-04.Base Case Description: The existing case is a residential split air-conditioner with SEER2 = 12.4 & gas furnace with annual fuel utilization efficiency (AFUE) = 78 or thermal efficiency (TE) = 80% (at manufacturer's option). The standard base case is a system that meets all code minimum requirements (i.e., SEER2 = 14.3 or SEER = 15 & gas furnace TE = 80%).
SWHC045-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing furnace and air conditioning equipment (if applicable) must be removed and disposed of rather than refurbished and sold. Existing gas line(s) serving removed gas equipment must be capped off and removed refrigerant must be handled and disposed in accordance with all state and local regulations, including but not limited to the California Building Standards Code (Title 24, which includes e.g. the California Building Code, California Energy Code, and California Mechanical Code), the Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), the National Electrical Code (NEC), and EPA regulations (40 CFR Part 82, Subpart F).
Existing equipment incentivized under accelerated replacement (AR) must be fully operational and serving intended application under qualified building type.
SWHC045-04.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
In the eTRM, the “Gas Furnace Only” permutations failed the CPUC Fuel Substitution Test for CZ15 and do not have climate zone-specific values reported.
SEER2 vs SEER Efficiency Ratings: The test procedure for EER/SEER/HSPF ratings is defined in Appendix M to 10 CFR 430 Subpart B. In 2022, manufacturers began rolling out new equipment rated and labeled according to a new test procedure defined in Appendix M1, with ratings labeled differently to avoid confusion (i.e., SEER2 or SEERM1). The Appendix M1 ratings are the basis for compliance with new code minimum efficiency requirements that took effect on January 1, 2023. A given piece of equipment may be rated under both test procedures with different test result values, so some equipment may bear ratings with both labels, and the two ratings should not be compared.

SWHC046-03.Offering ID: An all-electric commercial air-source heat pump with electric resistance supplemental heating when necessary. The measure case must meet or exceed the specified energy efficiency ratio (EER) or integrated energy efficiency ratio (IEER) for cooling mode and the coefficient of performance (COP) for heating mode. For ≥65 kBtu/hr offerings, effective in 2018, the IEER-rated requirement for cooling efficiency supersedes the EER-rated requirement.
SWHC046-03.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a commercial packaged or split-system AC unit with gas furnace meeting Title 24 and Title 20 requirements prevailing in 2014. The standard base case is a unit meeting the code requirement effective in 2023.
SWHC046-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure involves fuel substitution and must replace an existing less efficient packaged or split AC and natural gas furnace. The existing furnace equipment must be removed and disposed of rather than refurbished and sold. Existing gas line(s) serving removed gas equipment must be capped off and/or removed refrigerant must be handled and disposed of in accordance with all state and local regulations including but not limited to the California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) and the Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), the California Building Standards Code, the California Plumbing Code, and the National Electrical Code (NEC).
Existing equipment incentivized under accelerated replacement (AR) must be fully operational and serve the intended application under qualified building type. Non-operational and/or broken equipment cannot be incentivized under AR and must be incentivized under normal replacement (NR).

SWHC046-05.Offering ID: An all- electric commercial air-source heat pump (HP) using electric resistance heating only if supplemental heating is required. The measure case must meet or exceed the specified seasonal energy efficiency ratio (SEER2) or integrated energy efficiency ratio (IEER) for cooling mode and the coefficient of performance (COP) for heating mode. For ≥65 kBtu/hr offerings, effective in 2018, the IEER-rated requirement for cooling efficiency supersedes the EER-rated requirement.
SWHC046-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a commercial packaged or split-system AC unit with gas heating meeting the code requirements prevailing in 2014. The standard base case is a commercial packaged or split-system air conditioning (AC) unit with electric direct expansion (DX) cooling and gas heating meeting the code requirements effective in 2023.
SWHC046-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure involves fuel substitution and must replace an existing less efficient packaged or split AC and natural gas furnace.
Measure case and base case equipment must meet the prescribed requirement for the selected offering ID.
The existing furnace equipment must be removed and disposed of rather than refurbished and sold. All installation and removal must be in accordance with all state and local regulations including but not limited to the California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) and the Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), the California Building Standards Code, the California Plumbing Code, the National Electrical Code (NEC), EPA regulations.
Existing equipment incentivized under accelerated replacement (AR) must be fully operational and serve the intended application under qualified building type. Non-operational and/or broken equipment cannot be incentivized under AR and must be incentivized under normal replacement (NR).
For industrial building types, the unitary HP must be used solely for space comfort conditioning, such as serving office spaces within the industrial building.

SWHC047-04.Offering ID: A high efficiency gas fireplace meeting the specified fireplace efficiency (FE) rating. Accelerated replacement (AR) offerings involves changing the continuously operating pilot light (CPL) to an intermittent pilot light (IPL).
SWHC047-04.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a gas fireplace with a continuously operating pilot light (CPL). The standard base case is a gas fireplace with an intermittent pilot light (IPL).
SWHC047-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /: This measure applies to gas fireplaces manufactured with burner-control thermostats, integrated IPL controls, and fireplace efficiency (FE) values within the specified ranges. Any technology manufacturer qualifies to be used if safety and all other regulations are met; and the technology is commercially available.

SWHC047-05.Offering ID: A high efficiency gas fireplace meeting the specified fireplace efficiency (FE) rating. Accelerated replacement (AR) offerings involves changing the continuously operating pilot light (CPL), which constantly burns gas to enable ignition, to an intermittent pilot light (IPL), which only ignites the pilot light when burner ON signal is received.
SWHC047-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a gas fireplace with a CPL. The standard base case is a gas fireplace with an IPL meeting Title 24 and federal code requirement.
SWHC047-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure applies to gas fireplaces manufactured with burner-control thermostats, integrated IPL controls, and fireplace efficiency (FE) values within the specified ranges. Any technology manufacturer qualifies to be used if safety and all other regulations are met; and the technology is commercially available.
Ventless/vent-free fireplaces are not allowed.
SWHC047-05.Important Notes: The CPL system can be operated without electricity, and the IPL system requires an electrical supply from either a battery or hardwired into the building electricity supply.

SWHC047-06.Offering ID: A high efficiency gas fireplace meeting the specified fireplace efficiency (FE) rating. Accelerated replacement (AR) offerings involves changing the continuously operating pilot light (CPL) to an intermittent pilot light (IPL).
SWHC047-06.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a gas fireplace with a continuously operating pilot light (CPL). The standard base case is a gas fireplace with an intermittent pilot light (IPL).
SWHC047-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /: This measure applies to gas fireplaces manufactured with burner-control thermostats, integrated IPL controls, and fireplace efficiency (FE) values within the specified ranges. Any technology manufacturer qualifies to be used if safety and all other regulations are met; and the technology is commercially available.

SWHC047-07.Offering ID: A high efficiency gas fireplace meeting the specified fireplace efficiency (FE) rating. Accelerated replacement (AR) offerings involves changing the continuously operating pilot light (CPL), which constantly burns gas to enable ignition, to an intermittent pilot light (IPL), which only ignites the pilot light when burner ON signal is received.
SWHC047-07.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a gas fireplace with a CPL. The standard base case is a gas fireplace with an IPL meeting Title 24 and federal code requirement.
SWHC047-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure applies to gas fireplaces manufactured with burner-control thermostats, integrated IPL controls, and fireplace efficiency (FE) values within the specified ranges. Any technology manufacturer qualifies to be used if safety and all other regulations are met; and the technology is commercially available.
Ventless/vent-free fireplaces are not allowed.
SWHC047-07.Important Notes: The CPL system can be operated without electricity, and the IPL system requires an electrical supply from either a battery or hardwired into the building electricity supply.

SWHC048-04.Offering ID: A packaged air conditioner with heat recovery capabilities, i.e., that is equipped with an air-to-water heat exchanger that uses the waste heat from the rooftop unit (RTU) to preheat service hot water, thus, reducing hot water heating load.
SWHC048-04.Base Case Description: A packaged air conditioner rooftop unit without an air-to-water heat exchanger.
SWHC048-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /: Due to this technology being new, there are currently no studies that document the energy savings benefits of preheating water with an air-to-water heat exchanger in a rooftop air conditioning unit (RTU). The emerging technology study, Portfolio of the Future: Field Testing Report – Rheem H2AC at a Quick-Serve Restaurant, summarized the results of a field test for which an RTU unit with heat recovery was installed and water temperature and water consumption data was monitored and recorded for 114 days. Therefore, measure is only eligible for a fast food restaurant that is capable of utilizing waste heat to preheat incoming water from the main.
SWHC048-04.Important Notes: There are a limited number of units on the market that meet the measure case description, and the product has been discontinued due to it not meeting the federal minimum requirements for IEER efficiency. Manufacturer is working on getting this product updated to meet the minimum requirements by adding a VFD, but there is no timeline yet.

SWHC049-05.Offering ID:
An efficient central, single-packaged or split, air conditioner (AC) or heat pump (HP) unit that meets the specified energy efficiency (EER) or seasonal energy efficiency rating (SEER or SEER2) for cooling and heating seasonal performance factor (HSPF or HSPF2) rating for heating.
  • AC: SEER2 ≥15.2 and EER2 ≥12.2. SEER and HSPF rated equipment are not eligible
  • SEER2 rated HP: SEER2 ≥15.2 and HSPF2 ≥7.7
  • SEER-rated HP: SEER ≥16 and HSPF ≥9.0
SWHC049-05.Base Case Description:
The existing base cases are:
  • AC, 18 - 44 kBtu/hr:
    • 2006 – 2014: SEER = 13 (modeled as EER = 11.08), one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 12.5 and EER2 = 10.66)
    • 2015 - 2022: SEER = 14 and EER = 12.2, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 13.4 and EER2 = 11.78)
  • AC, 45 - 65 kBtu/hr:
    • 2006 - 2014: SEER = 13 (modeled as EER = 11.08), one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 12.5 and EER2 = 10.66)
    • 2015 - 2022: SEER = 14 and EER = 11.7, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 13.4 and EER2 = 11.2)
  • HP:
    • 2006 - 2014: SEER = 13 and HSPF = 7.7, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 12.5 and HSPF2 = 6.4)
    • 2015 - 2022: SEER = 14 and HSPF = 8.2, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 13.4 and HSPF2 = 6.9)

The standard base cases meet all code minimum efficiency requirements effective 1/1/2023 as well as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) refrigerant requirements effective 1/1/2025.

  • AC, 18 - 44 kBtu/hr: SEER2 = 14.3 and EER2 = 11.7 (9.8 if installed SEER2 ≥15.2). Equivalent to: SEER-Rated AC with SEER ≥15.0 and EER ≥12.1 (10.0 if installed SEER ≥16)
  • AC, 45 - 65 kBtu/hr: SEER2 ≥13.8 and EER2 ≥11.2 (9.8 if installed SEER2 ≥15.2). Equivalent to: SEER-Rated AC with SEER ≥14.4 and EER ≥11.7 (10.0 if installed SEER ≥16)
  • HP: SEER2-Rated HP with SEER2 ≥14.3 and HSPF2 ≥7.5. Equivalent to: SEER-Rated HP with SEER ≥15.0 and HSPF ≥8.8
SWHC049-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Central air conditioners and central air conditioning heat pumps manufactured on or after January 1, 2023, must have a SEER2 and a HSPF2 rating.
Only equipment covered by the Air-conditioning Heating and Refrigeration Institute (AHRI) 210/240 2008 are eligible.
The replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., an HP for an HP). Replacing an AC unit and gas furnace with an HP is considered fuel substitution and is not eligible. Refer to SWHC045 and SWHC044 for related residential HVAC fuel substitution measures.
SWHC049-05.Quality Assurance:
Verify the measure application type (MAT) and the appropriate baseline application. If MAT is accelerated replacement, document the existing base case
Verify that new unit meets the minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWHC049-05.Important Notes:
SEER2 vs SEER Efficiency Ratings: The test procedure for EER/SEER/HSPF ratings is defined in Appendix M to 10 CFR 430 Subpart B. In 2022, manufacturers began rolling out new equipment rated and labeled according to a new test procedure defined in Appendix M1, with ratings labeled differently to avoid confusion (i.e., SEER2 or SEERM1). The Appendix M1 ratings are the basis for compliance with new code minimum efficiency requirements that take effect on January 1, 2023. A given piece of equipment may be rated under both test procedures with different test result values, so some equipment may bear ratings with both labels, and the two ratings should not be compared.
For split systems the certification will specify an evaporator coil model and may specify additional components. In some cases, a furnace is specified in the certificate. In this case since the furnace is specified as part of the AC system it will not be eligible for a separate incentive.
Although the measure case offerings include distinct labels for SEER-rated and SEER2-rated equipment, these are not different types of equipment. Hence the base case does not vary depending on whether measure case equipment is SEER-rated or SEER2-rated. Because the supply of SEER-rated units is limited to existing stock manufactured before 2023, the code minimum requirements pertaining to these units are not a reasonable basis to define a baseline. Instead, for all measure offerings the base case reflects code minimum requirements effective for equipment manufactured in 2023, using SEER2 ratings.
The code requirements for split and packaged units may differ. For this measure package, both types of units are lumped together and the more stringent requirement is used to define the base case for the purpose of cost and energy savings calculations.
The Energy Standard or ISP baseline can be gas-fired. However, measure’s energy savings and cost evaluation and reporting will be based on the difference between the electric Energy Standard baseline and electric measure case (including AC and HP equipment).

SWHC049-06.Offering ID:
An efficient central, single-packaged or split, air conditioner (AC) or heat pump (HP) unit that meets the specified energy efficiency (EER) or seasonal energy efficiency rating (SEER2) for cooling and heating seasonal performance factor (HSPF2) rating for heating.
  • AC: SEER2 ≥15.2 and EER2 ≥12.2.
  • HP: SEER2 ≥15.2 and HSPF2 ≥7.7
SWHC049-06.Base Case Description:
The existing base cases are:
  • AC, 18 - 44 kBtu/hr:
    • 2006 – 2014: SEER = 13 (modeled as EER = 11.08), one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 12.5 and EER2 = 10.66)
    • 2015 - 2022: SEER = 14 and EER = 12.2, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 13.4 and EER2 = 11.78)
  • AC, 45 - 65 kBtu/hr:
    • 2006 - 2014: SEER = 13 (modeled as EER = 11.08), one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 12.5 and EER2 = 10.66)
    • 2015 - 2022: SEER = 14 and EER = 11.7, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 13.4 and EER2 = 11.2)
  • HP:
    • 2006 - 2014: SEER = 13 and HSPF = 7.7, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 12.5 and HSPF2 = 6.4)
    • 2015 - 2022: SEER = 14 and HSPF = 8.2, one-speed fan (equivalent to SEER2 = 13.4 and HSPF2 = 6.9)

The standard base cases meet all code minimum efficiency requirements effective 1/1/2023 as well as the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) refrigerant requirements effective 1/1/2025.

  • AC, 18 - 44 kBtu/hr: SEER2 = 14.3 and EER2 = 11.7 (9.8 if installed SEER2 ≥15.2).
  • AC, 45 - 65 kBtu/hr: SEER2 ≥13.8 and EER2 ≥11.2 (9.8 if installed SEER2 ≥15.2).
  • HP: SEER2-Rated HP with SEER2 ≥14.3 and HSPF2 ≥7.5.
SWHC049-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Central air conditioners and central air conditioning heat pumps manufactured on or after January 1, 2023, must have a SEER2 and a HSPF2 rating.
Only equipment covered by the Air-conditioning Heating and Refrigeration Institute (AHRI) 210/240 2008 are eligible.
The replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., an HP for an HP). Replacing an AC unit and gas furnace with an HP is considered fuel substitution and is not eligible. Refer to SWHC045 and SWHC044 for related residential HVAC fuel substitution measures.
SWHC049-06.Quality Assurance:
Verify the measure application type (MAT) and the appropriate baseline application. If MAT is accelerated replacement, document the existing base case.
Verify that new unit meets the minimum efficiency requirements in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWHC049-06.Important Notes:
Although the measure case offerings include distinct labels for SEER-rated and SEER2-rated equipment, these are not different types of equipment. Hence the base case does not vary depending on whether measure case equipment is SEER-rated or SEER2-rated. Because the supply of SEER-rated units is limited to existing stock manufactured before 2023, the code minimum requirements pertaining to these units are not a reasonable basis to define a baseline. Instead, for all measure offerings the base case reflects code minimum requirements effective for equipment manufactured in 2023, using SEER2 ratings.
The code requirements for split and packaged units may differ. For this measure package, both types of units are lumped together and the more stringent requirement is used to define the base case for the purpose of cost and energy savings calculations.
The Energy Standard or ISP baseline can be gas-fired. However, measure’s energy savings and cost evaluation and reporting will be based on the difference between the electric Energy Standard baseline and electric measure case (including AC and HP equipment).

SWHC050-05.Offering ID: A ductless, mini or multi-split, heat pump (HP) with cooling capacity less than 65,000 Btu/hr and meeting the specified energy efficiency (EER) or seasonal energy efficiency rating (SEER or SEER2) for cooling and heating seasonal performance factor (HSPF or HSPF2) rating for heating.
SWHC050-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a room/window or ductless, HP or air-conditioning (AC) with electric resistance space heater unit meeting Title 20 requirements prevailing in 2013. The standard base case is a unit meeting the Title 20 code requirement effective in 2023.
SWHC050-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) restricts electric resistance space heaters for primary heating. Hence, the standard equipment that would be installed to replace an existing room AC (or ductless AC coupled with electric resistance space heater) is deemed to be room HP (or Ductless HP, respectively). Hence, HSPF applies for heating mode efficiency to all standard base cases.
The replacement equipment must be “like for like” (e.g., a ductless HP for a ductless HP).
Only equipment covered by the Air-conditioning Heating and Refrigeration Institute (AHRI) 210/240 and 1230 and Department of Energy Code (DOE) Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) 429.16 are eligible.
SWHC050-05.Quality Assurance:
Verify the measure application type (MAT) and the appropriate baseline application. If MAT is accelerated replacement, document the existing base case
Verify that new unit meets the minimum efficiency requirements.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWHC050-05.Important Notes: For room ACs and electric resistance space heaters and for room HPs, code only specifies EER. Here, a standard practice HSPF was defined from typically seen HSPF-to-EER ratios corresponding to the capacity and EER range of SEER-rated ductless HPs.

SWHC054-01.Offering ID: The installation of an energy efficient – rated by apparent sensible effectiveness (ASEF), sensible recovery efficiency (SRE), and W/cfm – heat recovery ventilator (HRV), with a sensible efficiency (SRE), to the forced-air heating and ventilating air-conditioning system. Tier 1 denotes high-efficiency HRV; Tier 2 denotes best-in-class HRV, which may require additional modifications to the standard system configuration by improving recovery effectiveness and fan efficiency in having an integrated cross-counter flow heat exchanger and an automatic summer cooling bypass function (economizer) and adding efficient supply and exhaust ECM fan motors.
SWHC054-01.Base Case Description: A standard efficiency heat recovery ventilation system for multifamily buildings and a standard exhaust fan for single family buildings.
SWHC054-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires the installation of an air-to-air heat recovery ventilator (HRV) unit that draws in fresh outside air, while stale, humid, air is exhausted.
The following installations are not eligible:
Single Family
  • Tier 1 and 2 HRV are not eligible in rDXGF systems in CZ03, CZ04, CZ13, and CZ14 due to prescriptive Title 24 Prescriptive Code heating requirements.
  • Tier 1 HRV are not eligible in rDXGF systems in CZ08 and CZ15 as the additional fan energy outweighs the increased heating and cooling efficiency savings.
  • Tier 1 HRV are not eligible in rDXHP systems except in CZ01, CZ02 and CZ16 as the additional fan energy outweighs the increased heating and cooling efficiency savings.
Multifamily
  • Tier 1 HRV are not eligible in CZ01, CZ02, and CZ11 thorough CZ16 due to prescriptive Title 24 Prescriptive Code ventilation requirements.
  • Tier 1 and 2 HRV are not eligible in rDXGF systems in CZ01 through CZ15 due to prescriptive Title 24 Prescriptive Code heating requirements.
  • Tier 1 HRV are not eligible in rDXHP systems in CZ08, CZ09, and CZ10 as the additional fan energy outweighs the increased heating and cooling efficiency savings.

Tier 1 denotes high-efficiency HRV; Tier 2 denotes best-in-class HRV, which may require additional modifications to the standard system configuration by improving recovery effectiveness and fan efficiency in having an integrated cross-counter flow heat exchanger and an automatic summer cooling bypass function (economizer) and adding efficient supply and exhaust ECM fan motors.

SWHC054-03.Offering ID: The installation of an energy efficient – rated by apparent sensible effectiveness (ASEF), sensible recovery efficiency (SRE), and W/cfm – heat recovery ventilator (HRV), with a sensible efficiency (SRE), to the forced-air heating and ventilating air-conditioning system. Tier 1 denotes high-efficiency HRV; Tier 2 denotes best-in-class HRV, which may require additional modifications to the standard system configuration by improving recovery effectiveness and fan efficiency in having an integrated cross-counter flow heat exchanger and an automatic summer cooling bypass function (economizer) and adding efficient supply and exhaust ECM fan motors.

SWHC054-03.Important Notes: A single-phase air-source electric heat pump for space heating and space cooling HVAC systems were used for this measure.

SWHC057-01.Offering ID: A gas absorption heat pump (GAHP) water heater system, sized to satisfy the peak load of the space heating system and improve the efficiency of the conventional gas-fired boiler system, with coefficients of performance (COP) ≥1.25 at standard conditions (i.e., 70°F outdoor air and 130°F outlet temperature).
SWHC057-01.Base Case Description: A centralized natural gas hot water boiler meeting the 2022 Title 24 standards.
SWHC057-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Systems must conform to manufacturers’ specifications and with all applicable mechanical, plumbing and building codes and standards.
Rebuilt, refurbished, or relocated equipment is not eligible.

SWHC057-02.Offering ID: A gas absorption heat pump (GAHP) water heater system, sized to satisfy the peak load of the space heating system and improve the efficiency of the conventional gas-fired boiler system, with coefficients of performance (COP) ≥1.25 at standard conditions (i.e., 70°F outdoor air and 130°F outlet temperature).
SWHC057-02.Base Case Description: A centralized natural gas hot water boiler meeting the 2022 Title 24 standards.
SWHC057-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:

SWHC058-01.Offering ID: An energy efficient patio heater with a heating index (i.e., effective heated area) higher than the threshold of 2.2 ft2/kBtu/hr.
SWHC058-01.Base Case Description: A patio heater that has a heating index less than the threshold of 2.2 ft2/kBtu/hr.
SWHC058-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
All patio heaters must be tested by an ISO 17025-accredited laboratory using American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) Standard Test Method F2644-07.
Used or rebuilt equipment are not eligible.
Electric and propane patio heaters are not eligible.

SWHC058-02.Offering ID: An energy efficient patio heater with a heating index (i.e., effective heated area) higher than the threshold of 2.2 ft2/kBtu/hr.
SWHC058-02.Base Case Description: A patio heater that has a heating index less than the threshold of 2.2 ft2/kBtu/hr.
SWHC058-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /: All patio heaters must be tested by an ISO 17025-accredited laboratory using American Society for Testing and Materials (ASTM) F2644-07.

SWHC059-02.Offering ID: An installation of a smart fan controller (SFC) to operate the HVAC fan for extended variable fan-off delays on systems with or without a smart communicating thermostat (SCT).
SWHC059-02.Base Case Description: A residential central package or split HVAC system with or without a SCT.
SWHC059-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The base case may also include an HVAC system with a previously installed non-smart "legacy" fan controller that only monitors the fan "G" signal and provides fan-off delays after every fan-on duration which will increase energy use and interfere with smart thermostat savings. To avoid double-counting savings when replacing a "legacy" fan controller, the SFC measure will only be installed to replace a "legacy" fan controller with installation date earlier than 5 years prior to installing the SFC (e.g., for 2024 the installation date would be 2019, etc.). The manufacturing date on the UL label will be collected to identify when the "legacy" fan controller was installed.
Eligible products must work with smart thermostats and monitor all thermostat signals including: a cooling AC "Y" signal, a heat "W" signal, and a fan "G" signal. Eligible products must not provide a fan-off delay after fan-only durations to avoid increasing fan-only energy use. Research studies indicate 9 to 31% of users select continuous or hourly fan-on controls increasing electricity use by 1054 to 3520 kWh/yr.
All installations shall comply with all current applicable regulations, code, and standards including (but not limited to) the California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), the California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20), and the National Electrical Code (NEC).

SWHC061-01.Offering ID: The installation of a fully solar powered attic fan. The offerings vary based on whether the home has an existing attic fan and/or air conditioning (AC) system.
SWHC061-01.Base Case Description: The base case systems are differentiated by the existence of either an electrically powered attic fan, meeting federal code requirement, and/or air conditioning system. Offerings that replace an existing powered attic fan use the normal replacement (NR) measure application type, while those that add a solar attic fan where no attic fan existed use the add-on equipment (AOE) measure application type.
SWHC061-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The following eligibility requirements apply:
  • The solar attic fan must be UL certified for relevant UL safety standards by a Nationally Recognized Testing Laboratory (NRTL).
  • The solar attic fan must be permanently installed (connected to the roofing/framing of the house)
  • Any type of permanently installed solar attic fan is eligible, including but not limited to, through-the-roof, gable, or curb mounted solar attic fans.
  • The solar attic fan must come as single product, not assembled from parts from separate manufacturers. Systems where the solar panel is installed remote from the fan system, such as is common for gable fans, are eligible as long as the fan and solar panel are designed to operate together by the manufacturer.
  • The solar attic fan (or the solar panel powering the fan for remote solar models) must be installed in largely unshaded areas.
  • Only one solar attic fan can be installed per home, dwelling unit, or tenant space with an attic.
  • In order to ensure proper ventilation airflow in the attic, it is recommended that the installer confirm that attic vent sizes and locations are appropriate for attic fan installation.
SWHC061-01.Quality Assurance: Verify that the fan’s solar panel is optimally positioned to maximize its power output.
SWHC061-01.Important Notes: This is a POU-only measure; this measure package has not been reviewed and approved by the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) but has been affirmed by the California Technical Forum.

SWHC062-01.Offering ID: The installation of an occupancy fan control (OFC) to detect when a conditioned space is unoccupied, detect continuous fan operation (ON condition), and set the fan operation from ON to AUTO where the fan is only operated when space conditions call for cooling or heating.
SWHC062-01.Base Case Description: A HVAC equipment with supply fan operating continuously during unoccupied periods.
SWHC062-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible OFC products receive wired or wireless signals from occupancy sensors in a building. Occupancy sensors may use one or more sensing technologies. 1) Passive infrared (PIR) technology detects occupancy by sensing the change in the surrounding thermal radiation based on occupancy. 2) Ultrasonic sensors emit high-frequency sound waves outside human hearing range and use the doppler effect of returning sound waves to detect occupancy. 3) Time of flight infrared sensors emit invisible infrared light and onboard software detects occupancy. 4) Camera-based occupancy sensors use image processing to activate and record detected occupancy. 5) Geofencing uses location based sensor (LBS) technology such as mobile phones, GPS, or radio frequency identification (RFID) to create a virtual boundary around a physical location to detect occupancy. Advanced occupancy sensors count people and provide accurate data for occupancy levels and space use.
When operating in AUTO mode, eligible OFC products also provide a variable fan-off delay at the end of each cooling or heating cycle to over satisfy the thermostat, lengthen off cycle times, and save energy. The OFC works with any sensor technology that can provide a 24VAC/VDC signal. The OFC and occupancy sensor manufacturers provide instructions for sensor placement, operation, adaptive functions, calibration, and settings. The occupancy sensors detect when a conditioned space building is unoccupied, detect continuous fan operation (ON condition), and set the fan operation from ON to AUTO where the fan is only operated when zone conditions call for cooling or heating. The OFC returns fan operation to continuous during occupied periods per building occupancy for minimum outdoor airflow per ASHRAE 62.1. Geofencing must be calibrated, and occupancy sensors must be placed to capture occupancy for each HVAC unit. Geofencing will be calibrated with a WIFI-enabled device placed in conditioned space and verified by installer. Occupancy sensors are verified by changing delayed off time selection to 30 seconds and verifying fan switches from ON to AUTO, and then setting to 20 minutes.
Systems serving process loads are not eligible.
SWHC062-01.Important Notes:
The following building types are excluded in the analysis and do not have savings reported, due to modeled HVAC types not currently included in the modeled building prototypes:
  • Offering C heat pump (cDXHP) excludes refrigerated warehouse (WRf).
  • Offering D Variable Volume AC Unit with Gas Heat (cPVVG) excludes assembly (Asm), primary school (EPr), relocatable classroom (ERC), grocery (Gro), light industrial manufacturing (MLI), fast-food restaurant (RFF), sit-down restaurant (RSD), big box retail (RtL), small retail (RtS), conditioned storage (SCn), and refrigerated warehouse (WRf).

SWLG019-01.Offering ID: A high efficiency LED light fixtures specifically designed for non-stacked separation, stack separation, or greenhouse indoor agriculture.
SWLG019-01.Base Case Description: Standard LED for controlled environment non-stacked, stacked, and greenhouse.
SWLG019-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The fixtures must meet the specification in the Offering ID table.
As the standard practice baseline for NR measures is LED, NR measures may replace any existing non-LED or LED fixture.
The following are ineligible:
  • Light engines / retrofit kits.
  • Non-LED fixture sources.
  • Dynamically configurable AC products.
  • LED A lamps used for photoperiodic lighting during vegetative growth period.
SWLG019-01.Important Notes:
The eTRM savings analysis is based on the following assumptions:
  • The measure case meets the 2024 photosynthetic photon efficacy (PPE) and efficacy set forth by the California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) 2023 Cannabis Lighting Industry Standard Practice.
  • The measure case qualified by the Designed Lighting Consortium (DLC) Horticultural Technical Requirements (HTR) V3.0.
  • The base case lighting type and characteristics for cannabis indoor horticulture lighting were based on the “CPUC Staff Response to the Cannabis Lighting Standard Practice Studies Performed by Willdan and TRC” memo.
  • The lighting type and characteristics for non-cannabis indoor horticulture lighting referenced 2022 Title 24 code requirements.

SWLG020-02.Offering ID: A high efficiency UL Type B mogul base screw-in LED lamp that meets or exceeds the minimum lamp efficacy of 130 lumens per watt (LPW) replacing an existing high intensity discharge (HID) – high pressure sodium (HPS) or pulse start metal halide (PSMH) – lamps and ballasts in exterior pole/area and decorative fixtures.
SWLG020-02.Base Case Description: The existing base case is an outdoor area or decorative fixtures with either existing HPS or PSMH lamps and ballasts, representing the most common types of non-LED mogul base lamps in the market.
SWLG020-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Existing lighting equipment must operate from dusk to dawn using a timeclock, astronomical timeclock, photocell, or equivalent controls. Existing lighting that is scheduled to operate less than dusk to dawn or using a motion sensor is not eligible.
Installation in host fixture housing types that differ from the LED lamp's DLC category fixture types are eligible only if:
  • It is a fixture in an outdoor ambient lighting application.
  • The host fixture conforms to the requirements described in the LED lamp's manufacturer data and UL certified installation instructions.
Requirement for AR:
  • The PSMH were selected because they are the most efficient lamp technology in the metal halide family. However, many other less efficient metal halide lamps may exist in the market. The replacement of non-pulse start metal halide lamps is eligible under the PSMH baseline.
  • Although the most common HPS and PSMH lamp nominal wattages were used in the analysis, some lamps may have slightly different nominal wattages. Replacement of lamps with wattages not exactly matching the baseline wattages are eligible as long as claimed savings are conservative, and measure case lamp wattages fall within the appropriate wattage bins for claimed offerings. To accomplish this, the claims for other baseline wattages should use the offering with the next lowest baseline wattage bin of the same lamp type. For example, the replacement of 50W non-pulse start metal halide lamps should be claimed using the 39W PSMH baseline.
Requirement for NR:
  • Since the standard practice baseline for the NR measures is a screw-in LED, NR measures may replace any existing non-LED or LED mogul based screw-in lamps.
  • The new LED lamp must be of a lower wattage than the existing fixture system.
Qualified products must meet the following requirements:
  • The LED lamp must be for Outdoor Pole/Arm-mounted Area and Roadway Luminaires (Type B) or Decorative Luminaires (Type B)
  • Lamps must be mapped to the appropriate wattage bin for each measure.
  • Adjustable output lamps are eligible for this measure with the following additional requirements:
    • The lamp must meet minimum efficacy at the light output level used to claim the incentive.
    • For AR measures, the lamp must be set to an appropriate wattage for the base case equipment as described in the measure description. If multiple output settings are applicable for the base case equipment, any applicable output can be used dependent upon the customer's lighting requirements.
    • For NR measures, savings claims and incentives must be made for the lowest wattage setting of the lamp. This is to align it with the lowest applicable measure offering output bin and ensure that savings claims are conservative.
  • Specific safety related requirements include:
    • Products must be rated for fully enclosed fixtures.
    • Products must be suitable for damp or wet locations or have an ingress protection (IP) water ingress rating of 4 or better.
SWLG020-02.Important Notes:
The eTRM savings analysis is based on the following assumptions:
  • The minimum measure case efficiency exceeds the 75th percentile of Design Lights Consortium (DLC) qualified products and is in alignment with the minimum qualifying lighting product guidance used in the Modified Lighting Calculator (MLC) version 13.1.2.
  • The LED lamp uses a mogul base (E39, EX39, or E40) as required by DLC.
  • Measure case wattages represent the eligible LED lamp wattages that meet equivalent light output for the existing case HID fixture.
  • The standard base case is a UL Type-B mogul base screw-in LED lamp with an efficacy between 107 to 112 LPW, representing the 25th percentile of DLC products in the eligible product categories and aligning with outdoor lighting standard practice efficacies used in the most current MLC v13.1.2.

SWLG020-03.Offering ID: A high efficiency UL Type B mogul base screw-in LED lamp that meets or exceeds the minimum lamp efficacy of 130 lumens per watt (LPW) replacing an existing high intensity discharge (HID) – high pressure sodium (HPS) or pulse start metal halide (PSMH) – lamps and ballasts in exterior pole/area and decorative fixtures.
SWLG020-03.Base Case Description: The existing base case is an outdoor area or decorative fixtures with either existing HPS or PSMH lamps and ballasts, representing the most common types of non-LED mogul base lamps in the market.
SWLG020-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Existing lighting equipment must operate from dusk to dawn using a timeclock, astronomical timeclock, photocell, or equivalent controls.
Host fixture housing types that differ from the LED lamp's DLC category fixture types are eligible if:
It is a fixture in an outdoor ambient lighting application.
The host fixture conforms to the LED lamp's manufacturer requirements and UL certified installation instructions.
Requirement for AR:
PSMH is the most efficient lamp technology in the metal halide family, but other less efficient lamps may exist in the market and are eligible under the PSMH baseline.
Measure case W not exactly matching the baseline W are eligible as long as claimed savings are conservative, and measure case W falls within the claimed offering's wattage bins. Claims should use the offering with the next lowest baseline watt bin of the same lamp type. For example, the replacement of 50W non-PSMH lamps should use the 39W PSMH baseline.
Since the standard practice baseline for NR is a screw-in LED, NR measures may replace any existing non-LED or LED mogul based screw-in lamps.Qualified products must meet the following requirements:
Must be for Outdoor Pole/Arm-mounted Area and Roadway Luminaires (Type B) or Decorative Luminaires (Type B).
Must be mapped to the appropriate wattage bin for each measure and meet or exceed the minimum efficacy requirement.
Adjustable output lamps are eligible with the following additional requirements:
  • The lamp must meet minimum efficacy requirements at the light output level used to claim the incentive.
  • AR must select the appropriate base case W. If the base case has multiple output settings, any applicable output can be used based on the customer's lighting requirements.
  • NR must use the lowest W setting of the lamp, aligning it with the lowest applicable measure offering output bin, to ensure conservative claimed savings.
Must meeting safety related requirements: rated for fully enclosed fixtures, suitable for damp or wet locations, or have ≥4 ingress protection (IP) water ingress rating.
The following are ineligible:
Residential single-family, multifamily, and mobile home building types.
“Abandon-in-place” demolition of existing ballasts.
The replacement of integrated LED fixtures or LED retrofit kits.
The replacement of LED lamps, induction, compact fluorescent, mercury vapor, low pressure sodium, and incandescent lamps under the AR. However, these replacements are eligible under the NR if operating in appropriate fixtures with mogul bases.
Event lighting that operates less than dusk to dawn or using a motion sensor.
LED lamps in the DLC “Very High Output” general application category.
SWLG020-03.Important Notes:
The eTRM savings analysis is based on the following assumptions:
  • The measure case minimum efficacy exceeds the 75th percentile of Design Lights Consortium (DLC) qualified products and is in alignment with standard practice efficacies from SCE's 2024 industry standard practice (ISP) study.
  • Measure case wattages represent the eligible LED lamp wattages that meet equivalent light output for the existing case HID fixture.
  • The LED lamp must use a mogul base (E39, EX39, or E40) as required by DLC.
  • The standard base case is a UL Type-B mogul base screw-in LED lamp with an efficacy between 108 to 114 LPW, representing the 25th percentile of DLC products in the eligible product categories and aligning with the standard practice efficacies from SCE's 2024 ISP study.

SWMI003-01.Offering ID: A high-efficiency toilet that uses less than or equal to 0.8 gallons per flush.
SWMI003-01.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a low-efficiency toilet that uses 1.6 gallons per flush and the standard base case is a toilet that uses 1.28 gallons per flush.
SWMI003-01.Important Notes: Rider measures are measures that capture incremental embedded energy savings based on measures already quantified and approved through existing measures and/or Metropolitan Water District’s (MWD) water savings list. This measure is a type of rider measure and will adhere to the implementation, installation and program restriction requirements as identified in those approved already measures or MWD program requirements.

SWMI003-02.Offering ID: A high-efficiency toilet that uses less than or equal to 0.8 gallons per flush.
SWMI003-02.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a low-efficiency toilet that uses 1.6 gallons per flush and the standard base case is a toilet that uses 1.28 gallons per flush.

SWPR001-02.Offering ID:
A high-efficiency agricultural ventilation fan meeting the minimum efficiency, in term of airflow per watt (cfm/W), for the fan size range.
  • 24 in - 26 in: 13.18 cfm/W
  • 36 in: 16.62 cfm/W
  • 48 in: 19.27 cfm/W
  • 50 in - 62 in: 20.75 cfm/W
  • 72 in: 22.03 cfm/W
  • 84 in: 23.78 cfm/W
SWPR001-02.Base Case Description: A standard efficiency ventilation fan. The baseline fan efficiency was determined by calculating the 25th percentile of efficiencies for each fan size in the BESS Lab fan data.
SWPR001-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The custom measure data shows that 50 in - 62 in fans are not replaced one-to-one with 72 in or 84 in fans, because the larger fans have higher airflows (cfm). Therefore, the 72 in and 84 in fan offerings assume that a lesser number of 72 in or 84 in fans are replacing a greater number of 50 in - 62 in fans, such that the same airflow is maintained between base and measure case.
Fan sizes 24 in through 62 in must have been tested by ANSI/AMCA 230-12 or later agricultural fan testing protocol and must meet the minimum required efficiencies. Since 72 in and 84 in fans are assumed to be inherently more efficient than the smaller baseline fans, there is no minimum efficiency requirement for the 72 in and 84 in offerings so long as they exceed the standard efficiency of 52 in - 62 in offering. In addition, a lower number of 72 in and 84 in measure case fans are eligible to replace a larger number of existing fans, so long as measure eligibility regarding airflow (cfm) is followed.
To accommodate fan sizes that do not exactly match measure case fan sizes, the following shows the range of fans sizes that are eligible for each fan size category:
  • Offering Fan Sizes: Bin Fan Sizes
  • 24 in - 26 in: 22 in - 30 in
  • 36 in: 31 in - 40 in
  • 48 in: 41 in - 49 in
  • 50 in - 62 in: 50 in - 62 in
  • 72 in: 63 in - 76 in
  • 84 in: 77 in - 86 in
Inherently variable speed fans, such as those with electronically commutated motors (ECMs) are eligible for this measure and can be programmed to operate in variable speed mode when installed. However, the savings associated with this measure do not assume variable speed operation, only increased fan efficiency.
SWPR001-02.Important Notes: The implementer should collect the base case fan nameplate, nominal, or BESS tested airflows (cfm) and calculate the total airflow for the existing fan system. A similar process would be done for the new measure case fan system in order to compare total base and measure case fan system airflows

SWPR002-02.Offering ID: Installation of a new variable frequency drive (VFD) on a constant speed glycol circulation pump
SWPR002-02.Base Case Description: An existing glycol circulation pump operating at a constant speed, without a VFD.
SWPR002-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Application of this measure is for the addition of a VFD on an existing glycol pump motor operating at constant speed. Chilled fluid, other than glycol, can be considered only if the unit energy savings (UES) is equal to or exceeds the UES of this measure.
The VFD must have automated controls based on pressure feedback, fluid temperature feedback, and/or flow rate feedback.
This measure is only applicable to the winery industry for process end-use applications.
This measure is not eligible for a back-up pump or redundant pump.
SWPR002-02.Quality Assurance:
Customer must provide documentation that the VFD is not installed on a back-up pump or redundant pump
Determine if the application of VFD control is appropriate.Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that pump speed is controlled appropriately.

SWPR002-03.Offering ID: Installation of a new variable frequency drive (VFD) on a constant speed glycol circulation pump
SWPR002-03.Base Case Description: An existing glycol circulation pump operating at a constant speed, without a VFD.
SWPR002-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure involves the installation of a variable frequency drive (VFD) on an existing glycol pump motor. Chilled fluid, other than glycol, can be considered only if the unit energy savings (UES) is equal to or exceeds the UES of this measure.
This measure is for the addition of a VFD on an existing glycol pump motor. If the existing constant speed pump motor must be replaced because it cannot accommodate a VFD, such as it not being inverter rated, then a new pump motor may be installed that works with the VFD. This is allowed because pump motor replacements are considered repairs and do not trigger Title 24 code. However, no additional energy savings shall be claimed through this measure package for increased pump motor efficiency and the pump motor replacement cost shall not be considered as part of the measure install cost.
The existing glycol pump(s) must operate at constant speed. The VFD must have automated controls based on pressure feedback, fluid temperature feedback, and/or flow rate feedback.
The VFD is recommended to meet requirements as specified by IEEE Standard 519-2022.
This measure is only applicable to the winery industry for process end-use applications.
This measure is not eligible for a back-up pump or redundant pump.
SWPR002-03.Quality Assurance:
Customer must provide documentation that the VFD is not installed on a back-up pump or redundant pump
Determine if the application of VFD control is appropriate.Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that pump speed is controlled appropriately.

SWPR003-02.Offering ID: A steam trap replacement in a commercial “12 - 24 hours per day” facility – with a steam plant operating a steam boiler for 12 to 24 hours per day.
SWPR003-02.Base Case Description: A failed steam trap (in either the leaking failure or the blow-through failure mode).
SWPR003-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The customer may be required to provide the location of the new steam trap in the steam loop, make and model number, a specification sheet, approximate (±5 psig) steam line (not boiler) pressure, and receipts showing the cost and purchase date
Steam traps designed for any pipe size are eligible.
This measure is only applicable for commercial “12-24 hours per day” facilities – primarily large commercial facilities with a steam plant operating a steam boiler for 12 to 24 hours per day. Small commercial facilities which operate their steam systems less than 12 hours per day are excluded
SWPR003-02.Quality Assurance:
Obtain a copy of any ultrasonic testing and mapping of failed steam traps, including information on steam trap pressure, steam trap type, location, and load/end use served.
Verify the quantity of steam traps failed in the closed position. Verify quantity and type of new steam traps installed.
