SWPR004-04.Offering ID: A recirculation pump with an electric resistance heater to a backup diesel generator. Offerings are tiered based upon the backup generator sizes and dependent upon whether the existing block heater is properly sized (>1 kW) or undersized (≥1 kW).
SWPR004-04.Base Case Description: A thermosiphon heater with a recirculation pump and a shorter runtime electric resistance heater that relies on the change in density (impacting buoyancy) to circulate the heated coolant.
SWPR004-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
An existing backup generator is eligible for this measure if it is not currently fitted with a circulating block heater or device utilizing a similar electro-mechanical system to heat and circulate generator block pre-warming fluid. Furthermore, the replacement of an existing generator equipped with a thermosiphon system with a new generator equipped with a circulating block heater is also considered eligible.Additional eligibility requirements regarding the installation include:
  • Installation of circulating block heater should be performed by a qualified technician (i.e. generator maintenance technician or mechanical service technician).
  • Installer should follow manufacturer’s installation requirements and assess and perform (if necessary) fluid hose adjustments that may be associated with the retrofit to enable the circulating block heater to function at optimal energy efficiency.
  • Installation shall meet all applicable regulations including but not limited to latest NFPA Code 110 for Emergency Power Systems and NEC.

This measure is not eligible for heat pump generator block heaters.

SWPR004-05.Offering ID: A recirculation pump with an electric resistance heater to a backup diesel generator. Offerings are tiered based upon the backup generator sizes and dependent upon whether the existing block heater is properly sized (>1 kW) or undersized (≥1 kW).
SWPR004-05.Base Case Description: A thermosiphon heater with a recirculation pump and a shorter runtime electric resistance heater that relies on the change in density (impacting buoyancy) to circulate the heated coolant.
SWPR004-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:

Furthermore, the replacement of an existing generator equipped with a thermosiphon system with a new generator equipped with a circulating block heater is also considered eligible.Additional eligibility requirements regarding the installation include: Installer should follow manufacturer’s installation requirements and assess and perform (if necessary) fluid hose adjustments that may be associated with the retrofit to enable the circulating block heater to function at optimal energy efficiency.
This measure is not eligible for heat pump generator block heaters.

SWPR005-02.Offering ID: The installation of a variable speed drive (VSD) on an existing dust collection fan.
SWPR005-02.Base Case Description: An existing, fixed-speed dust collection fan with a rated individual motor capacity from 10 hp to 150 hp. A customer survey found the 10 hp to 50 hp motors typically have continuous or ON/OFF operation and the 60 hp to 150 hp motors have ON/OFF controls with dampers used for balancing.
SWPR005-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The customer must have an existing electrically operated fixed-speed fan installed on site or plans to install a new electrically operated fixed-speed fan. The existing fan motors cannot be two- speed and cannot have an existing VSD, including failed VSD.
The existing baghouse, fan, and motor must be in proper operating condition. The baghouse fan motor must be compatible with a variable speed drive (VSD). (Motors that are compatible with VSDs vary by manufacturer. Compatible motors may be labeled as inverter duty, VSD ready, or have insulation classification F.)
The installed VSD must be controlled based on static pressure, airflow rate (cfm), or velocity at the lowest required rate to keep particulates suspended in the air stream.
The VSD is recommended to meet requirements as specified by IEEE Standard 519-2014.
Both agricultural and industrial sectors are applicable for fan motors 50 hp or smaller, whereas fan motors larger than 50 hp are limited to the agricultural sector.
The following are ineligible:
  • HVAC fan
  • A designed high-pressure blower. High pressure blowers have designed capacities of <150 cfm/hp. (Applicable only to fan motors larger than 50 hp).
  • An individual fan motor rated <10 hp or >150 hp
  • A two-speed fan motor or a motor with an existing VSD, including failed VSD.
SWPR005-02.Quality Assurance:
Determine if the application of VFD control is appropriate.
Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that fan speed is controlled appropriately.

SWPR006-02.Offering ID: The installation of a variable speed drive (VSD) on an existing barn ventilation fan. The VSD varies the speed of the fan automatically based on ambient dry bulb (DB) temperature.
SWPR006-02.Base Case Description: An existing fixed-speed fan, continuously operated or have ON/OFF control, with a rated individual motor capacity ≤5 hp
SWPR006-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The VSD application requires controlling multiple fans from 1 to 5 hp each in arrays with one VSD or one VSD for each fan.
The customer must have an existing electrically operated fixed speed fan installed on site or plans to install a new electrically operated fixed speed fan.
The VSD must be installed as close to the motor as possible when applying VSD to a standard duty NEMA motor. Failure to do so may result in premature motor failure. Sufficient airflow must be maintained through the motor to prevent overheating.
The VSD is recommended to meet requirements as specified by IEEE Standard 519-2014.
This measure is applicable for all existing agriculture facilities used to house livestock, which includes Other Agricultural (AgOth) and Livestock Farms (ALF).
The measure is only applicable for climate zones CZ06 - CZ15. The historical Dairy and Winery Industry Efficiency Solutions (DWIES) project data, used in this measure analysis, only estimates fan operating hours in CZ12 and CZ13. Analysis for the temperature bin hours found CZ06 - CZ15 show similar operating characteristics as CZ12 and CZ13, while CZ01 - CZ05 and CZ16 exhibited different characteristics. In addition, PG&E program data showed there were no applicable projects in the data set for climate zones CZ01 to CZ05.
The following are ineligible:
  • HVAC, exhaust, pressurization, or other process applications unrelated to barn ventilation.
  • Motors >5 hp or <1 hp
  • A two-speed fan motor or a motor with an existing VSD, including failed VSD.

SWPR007-01.Offering ID: A natural gas process boiler that is retrofit with either a feedwater or condensing economizer and is meeting the specified thermal efficiency (TE).
SWPR007-01.Base Case Description: A natural gas steam boiler that operates with no flue stack heat recovery system and is meeting Title 20 and Title 24 minimum TE requirement.
SWPR007-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure is only applicable to steam boilers with an input rating ≤20 million Btu/hr. Hot water boilers are not eligible.
For a dual-stage economizer:
  • The disposal of combustion condensate must meet local codes regarding sanitary drain or storm sewer.
  • Some applications may require a neutralizer for the acidic combustion condensate.

There are no boiler efficiency eligibility requirements for the measure, for both single-stage and dual-stage economizers. The efficiency gains were averaged from past custom projects (estimated improvement at 2% and 8% for single and dual stage economizer, respectively).
The following conditions provide good candidates for an economizer retrofit:
  • Steam boilers that exceed 100 boiler horsepower (>3.3 million Btu/hr).
  • Steam boiler exiting stack temperature at greater than 400°F.
  • Operating at steam pressures of 75 psig or above.
  • Boiler load factor of at least 40%.
  • Average temperature of the exiting flue stack from the economizer must be below 200°F to cause water vapor condensation in dual-stage economizer.
  • Minimum 50% make-up water for dual-stage economizer.
SWPR007-01.Quality Assurance: The boiler manufacturer, model, and spec sheet should be submitted for verification.

SWPR008-01.Offering ID: The installation of variable frequency drive (VFD) controls on a constant speed rod beam pump without pump off controllers (POC) or existing VFD controls.

The measure case VFD controls must have automated controls based on pressure feedback, fluid temperature feedback, and/or flow rate feedback.
The pump must meet a minimum of 2,000 operating hours.
The following are not eligible:
  • Pumping applications outside of the oil and gas production facilities and refineries and associated processes.
  • Back-up or redundant pump.

This measure must constitute installation of new VFD controls on an existing constant speed rod beam pump motor. SWRE001-03.Offering ID: The installation of a pool cover on an uncovered outdoor or indoor pool heated with natural gas.
SWRE001-03.Base Case Description: An uncovered indoor or outdoor pool, heated with natural gas, at a commercial or multifamily facility
SWRE001-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires that there is no existing pool cover, or the existing pool cover is no longer effective.
Eligibility requirements include:
  • The pool must be heated with natural gas.
  • The new pool cover must cover at least 95% of the pool.
  • The new pool cover must have a minimum R-value of 0.5 ft2-hr-F/Btu (R=L/K). R-value of 0.5 is the thermal resistance of the cover itself.
  • Proof of minimum five-year warranty (any combination of manufacturers and/or extended applies).
  • Proof of cover thickness and K factor (found on the manufacturer specification sheet).
  • Olympic size pools must have a storage reel system either manual or automatic winding.

SWRE001-04.Offering ID: The installation of a pool cover on an uncovered outdoor or indoor pool heated with natural gas.
SWRE001-04.Base Case Description: An uncovered indoor or outdoor pool, heated with natural gas, at a commercial or multifamily facility
SWRE001-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires that there is no existing pool cover, or the existing pool cover is no longer effective.
Eligibility requirements include:
  • The pool must be heated with natural gas.
  • The new pool cover must cover at least 95% of the pool.
  • The new pool cover must have a minimum R-value of 0.5 ft2-hr-F/Btu (R=L/K). R-value of 0.5 is the thermal resistance of the cover itself.
  • Proof of minimum five-year warranty (any combination of manufacturers and/or extended applies).
  • Proof of cover thickness and K factor (found on the manufacturer specification sheet).
  • Olympic size pools must have a storage reel system either manual or automatic winding.

SWRE003-03.Offering ID: A natural gas pool water heater with a thermal efficiency (TE) rating of at least 84%.
SWRE003-03.Base Case Description: A pool heater that meets 2013 federal code code-minimum TE of 82% TE.
SWRE003-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure is available for swimming pool heating and the unit must replace pre-existing pool heater.
The commercial pool heater must have a TE ≥84% and an ON/OFF switch and no pilot light.
Single-family residences (residential homeowners) are not eligible.
SWRE003-03.Important Notes: This measure is applicable to all pool sizes to accommodate the energy savings opportunity associated with the significant number of smaller and larger heated commercial pools.

SWRE003-04.Offering ID: A natural gas pool water heater with a thermal efficiency (TE) rating of at least 84%.
SWRE003-04.Base Case Description: A pool heater that meets 2013 Title 10 minimum TE of 82% TE.
SWRE003-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure is available for swimming pool heating and the unit must replace pre-existing pool heater.
The commercial pool heater must have a TE ≥84% and an ON/OFF switch and no pilot light.
Single-family residences (residential homeowners) are not eligible.
SWRE003-04.Important Notes: This measure is applicable to all pool sizes to accommodate the energy savings opportunity associated with the significant number of smaller and larger heated commercial pools.

SWRE004-03.Offering ID: An energy efficient natural gas pool or spa heater that meets the specified thermal efficiency (TE). Tier 1 denotes non-condensing heaters and Tier 2 denotes condensing boilers, which may require additional modifications to the existing system configuration as the flue requirements are substantially different than the non-condensing alternative.
SWRE004-03.Base Case Description: A natural gas pool or spa water heater meeting 2019 Title 20 and 2013 Title 10 minimum TE requirement.
SWRE004-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /: If the heater will be installed in a low NOx area, the replacement unit must also meet NOx emissions standards set forth by the governing air quality management district.

SWRE004-04.Offering ID: An energy efficient natural gas pool or spa heater that meets the specified thermal efficiency (TE). Tier 1 denotes non-condensing heaters and Tier 2 denotes condensing boilers, which may require additional modifications to the existing system configuration as the flue requirements are substantially different than the non-condensing alternative.
SWRE004-04.Base Case Description: A natural gas pool or spa water heater meeting 2021 Title 20 and 2013 Title 10 minimum TE requirement.
SWRE004-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /: If the heater will be installed in a low NOx area, the replacement unit must also meet NOx emissions standards set forth by the governing air quality management district.
SWRE004-04.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
Measure savings are weighted average of pools with no covers and the average of pools with vinyl, bubble/solar, and insulated covers.

SWRE005-03.Offering ID: An energy efficient heat pump (HP) pool heater with a minimum coefficient of performance (COP) of 5.5 replacing a standard efficiency natural gas-fired pool water heater.
SWRE005-03.Base Case Description: A natural gas pool or spa water heater meeting 2021 Title 20 and 2013 Title 10 minimum TE requirement.
SWRE005-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The COP value used to verify the eligibility of the product should be the High Air Temperature - Mid Humidity value from the AHRI database.
Only single heat pump systems are eligible. Multi-HP pool heaters are not eligible due to noise level and cost, among other concerns. Spa equipment is also not eligible.
Since this measure is assumed to be installed outdoors, it is eligible for installation in unconditioned spaces.
Heat pump pool heaters are typically larger in size than the base case natural gas pool heaters, which may necessitate site upgrades, such as larger concrete pads or supports and/or additional structural requirements. Implementers should consider all necessary installation requirements when pursuing this measure.
Pool heaters have minimum flow requirements that may be greater than minimum pool filter pump minimum flows. Implementers shall ensure that the pool heater has an integrated safety shut-off feature that disables equipment when minimum flows are not met. An alternative is to install an interlock (according to the manufacturer’s recommendation) with existing pool pump(s) to ensure minimum flows are met during equipment operation.
Measure implementation shall comply with all applicable codes and regulations, including but not limited to the latest versions of California Title 24, Part 6; the California Building Code; and the National Electric Code. Since the installation of pool covers is required for alterations of pool equipment systems, the implementer shall insure that the existing pool already has a pool cover or that a pool cover is installed in full compliance with code during implementation.

SWRE005-04.Offering ID: A heat pump (HP) pool heater that meets 2025 Title 24 minimum requirement of 5.5 coefficient of performance (COP) (i.e., code standard offerings) or exceeds the requirement with ≥5.8 COP (i.e., efficient offerings) replacing a standard efficiency natural gas-fired pool water heater.
SWRE005-04.Base Case Description: A natural gas pool or spa water heater meeting 2021 Title 20 and 2013 Title 10 minimum TE requirement.
SWRE005-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The COP value used to verify the eligibility of the product should be the High Air Temperature - Mid Humidity value from the AHRI database.
Only single heat pump systems are eligible. Multi-HP pool heaters are not eligible due to noise level and cost, among other concerns. Spa equipment is also not eligible.
Since this measure is assumed to be installed outdoors, it is eligible for installation in unconditioned spaces.
Measure implementation shall comply with all applicable codes and regulations, including but not limited to the latest versions of California Title 24, Part 6; the California Building Code; and the National Electric Code. Since the installation of pool covers are required for alterations of pool equipment systems, the implementer shall ensure that the existing pool already has a pool cover or that a pool cover is installed in full compliance with code during implementation.
SWRE005-04.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
Measure savings are weighted average of pools with no covers and the average of pools with vinyl, bubble/solar, and insulated covers.

SWRE005-06.Offering ID: A heat pump (HP) pool heater that meets 2025 Title 24 minimum requirement of 5.5 coefficient of performance (COP) (i.e., code standard offerings) or exceeds the requirement with ≥5.8 COP (i.e., efficient offerings) replacing a standard efficiency natural gas-fired pool water heater.
SWRE005-06.Base Case Description: A natural gas pool water heater meeting 2021 CA Title 20 and 2013 code of federal regulations (10 CFR 430) minimum thermal efficiency (TE) requirement.
SWRE005-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The COP value used to verify the eligibility of the product should be the High Air Temperature - Mid Humidity value from the AHRI database.
Only single heat pump systems are eligible. Multi-HP pool heaters are not eligible due to noise level and cost, among other concerns. Spa equipment is also not eligible.
Since this measure is assumed to be installed outdoors, it is eligible for installation in unconditioned spaces.
Measure implementation shall comply with all applicable codes and regulations, including but not limited to the latest versions of California Title 24, Part 6; the California Building Code; and the National Electric Code. Since the installation of pool covers are required for alterations of pool equipment systems, the implementer shall ensure that the existing pool already has a pool cover or that a pool cover is installed in full compliance with code during implementation.
SWRE005-06.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
Measure savings are weighted average of pools with no covers and the average of pools with vinyl, bubble/solar, and insulated covers.

SWSV001-06.Offering ID: This measure is defined as duct sealing to reduce total leakage from a “high” or “medium” leakage rate to a “low” leakage rate, as specified by residence type (single family, multifamily, and double-wide mobile home).
SWSV001-06.Base Case Description: A single family, multifamily, or double-wide mobile home with a "high" or "medium" duct leakage rate, as specified by residence type. Leakage rate is the sum of supply and return.
SWSV001-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Homes constructed prior to 2006 are classified as building weatherization measures (BW), whereas those constructed in 2006 or later are classified as retrocommissioning (BRO-RCx) measures. All mobile home measures are classified as BW measures.
The following shows the required duct leakage reduction:
  • For the Med-Low offering, the leakage reduction must be ≥12% but <28% for single family and multifamily dwelling units and ≥10% but <20% for mobile homes.
  • For the High-Low offering, the leakage reduction must be ≥28% for single family and multifamily dwelling units and ≥20% for mobile homes.
  • For all offerings, the final leakage rate cannot be >14% for single family and multifamily dwelling units and >17% for mobile homes.
SWSV001-06.Quality Assurance:
Up to two duct tests must be conducted. The first or “test-in” is a system check to determine whether the duct system tightness meets the California Title 24 specifications. If the test-in shows that duct leakage exceeds minimum tightness specifications, and the technicians correct the situation, then a second test or “test-out” must be conducted to verify proper duct tightness was achieved. Energy savings are associated with the test-out procedure; energy savings are not assigned to the “test-in” procedure.
Existing duct leakage must be recorded after comprehensive field testing. The testing can be performed while the system is pressurized using a duct blower fan to ensure the target leakage thresholds are achieved. Duct sealing shall be performed on the unpressurized system. After installation, testing shall be performed to ensure the target leakage thresholds are achieved. Duct leakage tests titled “Duct Pressurization and Leakage Flow Measurement” and “Duct Leakage to Outside Measurement” are the only two eligible duct leakage tests referenced in California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) Reference Appendix 3, which contains the same standards referenced in ANSI/ RESNET/ICC Standard 380-2019. However, only one of the two types of duct leakage tests, such as the total leakage or the leakage to outside tests, is required to justify duct leakage for this measure. Photos and test results can serve as documentation that the work was performed on an existing HVAC system.
The following prerequisites must be met before the Quality Maintenance (QM) treatments, such as airflow adjustments, can be implemented to ensure an HVAC system is running optimally.
  • The unit and system must be capable of delivering a supply airflow rate of at least 350 cfm/ton after treatments related to airflow are completed and before refrigerant charge is tested and/or adjusted. The requirement ensures that the refrigerant system can be properly diagnosed and charged. If the system is not delivering 350 cfm/ton upon initial inspection, an assessment should be made to determine if the system will be able to deliver 350 cfm/ton by implementing some or all of the QM treatments related to airflow. If it is determined that the supply fan and duct system in place do not have the capability to deliver 350 cfm/ton after the airflow treatments have been performed, the refrigerant charge cannot be properly diagnosed, rendering the QM process incomplete and the savings of this measure invalid.
  • The unit must be drawing power.
  • The unit must have a condenser over ambient temperature (COAT) of at least 3 degrees.
  • An assessment and report are required in accordance with Air Conditioning Contractors of America (ACCA) Standard 4 prior to any treatments being applied to determine the baseline conditions and to develop QM treatment recommendations.
Duct connections must not be sealed with duct tape (cloth-backed rubber adhesive tapes). Mastic or aerosol sealant materials are preferred, and butyl tape can be used where mastic or aerosol are impractical.
If some or all of the existing ductwork is beyond repair and more than 25 linear feet of ductwork must be replaced, the replaced ductwork must comply with applicable requirements of subsections i and ii in Section 150.2(b) of the 2022 California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), or the replaced ductwork must be installed and tested in accordance with the applicable requirements in 2022 Title 24 Section 150.2(b)1D.

SWSV001-07.Offering ID: This measure is defined as duct sealing to reduce total leakage from a “high” or “medium” leakage rate to a “low” leakage rate, as specified by residence type (single family, multifamily, and double-wide mobile home).
SWSV001-07.Base Case Description: A single family, multifamily, or double-wide mobile home with a "high" or "medium" duct leakage rate, as specified by residence type. Leakage rate is the sum of supply and return.
SWSV001-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Homes constructed prior to 2006 are classified as building weatherization measures (BW), whereas those constructed in 2006 or later are classified as retrocommissioning (BRO-RCx) measures. All mobile home measures are classified as BW measures.
The following shows the required duct leakage reduction:
  • For the Med-Low offering, the leakage reduction must be ≥12% but <28% for single family and multifamily dwelling units and ≥10% but <20% for mobile homes.
  • For the High-Low offering, the leakage reduction must be ≥28% for single family and multifamily dwelling units and ≥20% for mobile homes.
  • For all offerings, the final leakage rate cannot be >14% for single family and multifamily dwelling units and >17% for mobile homes.

After installation, testing shall be performed to ensure the target leakage thresholds are achieved. Duct leakage tests titled “Duct Pressurization and Leakage Flow Measurement” and “Duct Leakage to Outside Measurement” are the only two eligible duct leakage tests referenced in California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) Reference Appendix 3, which contains the same standards referenced in ANSI/ RESNET/ICC Standard 380-2019. However, only one of the two types of duct leakage tests, such as the total leakage or the leakage to outside tests, is required to justify duct leakage for this measure. The requirement ensures that the refrigerant system can be properly diagnosed and charged. If the system is not delivering 350 cfm/ton upon initial inspection, an assessment should be made to determine if the system will be able to deliver 350 cfm/ton by implementing some or all of the QM treatments related to airflow. If it is determined that the supply fan and duct system in place do not have the capability to deliver 350 cfm/ton after the airflow treatments have been performed, the refrigerant charge cannot be properly diagnosed, rendering the QM process incomplete and the savings of this measure invalid.The unit must be drawing power.The unit must have a condenser over ambient temperature (COAT) of at least 3 degrees.An assessment and report are required in accordance with Air Conditioning Contractors of America (ACCA) Standard 4 prior to any treatments being applied to determine the baseline conditions and to develop QM treatment recommendations.Duct connections must not be sealed with duct tape (cloth-backed rubber adhesive tapes). Mastic or aerosol sealant materials are preferred, and butyl tape can be used where mastic or aerosol are impractical.
If some or all of the existing ductwork is beyond repair and more than 25 linear feet of ductwork must be replaced, the replaced ductwork must comply with applicable requirements of subsections i and ii in Section 150.2(b) of the 2022 California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24), or the replaced ductwork must be installed and tested in accordance with the applicable requirements in 2022 Title 24 Section 150.2(b)1D.

SWSV005-03.Offering ID: This measure is defined as the restoration of economizer functionality through repairs. There is an option of adding an advanced digital economizer controller (ADEC).
SWSV005-03.Base Case Description: An existing HVAC equipment, with or without ADEC, with non-functional, either failed closed or partially open, economizer. Common as-found conditions in the field include broken or worn linkages, stuck dampers, inadequate wiring, inoperable damper actuators, or malfunctioning controllers.
SWSV005-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Only licensed California contractors are permitted to implement this measure.
This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied. The HVAC system must have an existing, non-functional economizer that could be repaired to a functional state. This includes economizers with broken or worn linkages, non-operational dampers, wiring issues, and malfunctioning controllers.
Additional technician verification of thermostat wiring and the number of cooling stages that should be performed to ensure that the first stage of cooling is dedicated to economizer operation and two-stage thermostat operation is enabled where possible.
The controller changeover setpoint should be adjusted appropriately based on the available number of thermostat cooling stages.
This measure does not apply if the packaged or split HVAC system has a fully operational economizer.

SWSV005-04.Offering ID: This measure is defined as the restoration of economizer functionality through repairs or the replacement of an existing economizer control sensor. The economizer control sensor replacement offerings have been migrated to this measure package from the SWSV010 because the control sensor replacement scope required by the offerings better aligns with the economizer repair scopes outlined in this measure package.
SWSV005-04.Base Case Description:
The base case for the economizer repair offerings is an existing HVAC equipment with non-functional, either failed closed or partially open, economizer. Common as-found conditions in the field include broken or worn linkages, stuck dampers, inadequate wiring, inoperable damper actuators, or malfunctioning controllers.
The base case for the economizer sensor replacement offerings is an existing economizer equipped with an outdated snap disc thermostat, a malfunctioning sensor, or an uncalibrated sensor.
SWSV005-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied. The HVAC system must have an existing, malfunctioning, or non-functional economizer that could be repaired to a functional state.
Requirements for the economizer repair offerings include:
  • The existing economizer must have broken or worn linkages, non-operational dampers, wiring issues, and malfunctioning controllers.
  • The economizer must be repaired to a fully functional state.
  • The HVAC systems that require controller upgrades to enable fully functioning economizer are still eligible under this measure but the costs for controller upgrades are not included as part of the measure cost.
Requirements for the economizer sensor replacement offerings include:
  • The economizer must have either a snap disc thermostat, a malfunctioning analog sensor, or an uncalibrated sensor. The types of sensors include dry bulb temperature sensors for outside air temperature or return air temperature and humidity sensors.
  • HVAC systems that require multiple sensor replacements are eligible under this measure.
In addition to the above requirements, both the measure offerings should also perform additional technician verification of thermostat wiring and the number of cooling stages to ensure that the first stage of cooling is dedicated to economizer operation and two-stage thermostat operation is enabled where possible. The controller changeover setpoint should be adjusted appropriately based on the available number of thermostat cooling stages.
Although not required as part of this measure package, it is recommended that the economizer high limit should be optimized for the climate per Title 24 2025 Table 140.4-G. Economizer setpoint adjustment may be incentivized through the measure package SWSV010.
This measure is applicable for nonresidential buildings served by unitary direct expansion (DX) and split systems that do not serve process or refrigeration loads. Only the following host packaged or split single zone HVAC system types are eligible: AC unit with a gas furnace (cDXGF), cooling only AC unit (cDXOH), heat pump (cDXHP), or variable volume AC unit with gas furnace (cPVVG).
The following are ineligible:
  • The economizer control replacement measure does not apply if the packaged or split HVAC system has a fully operational economizer control sensor.
  • The economizer repair measure does not apply if the packaged or split HVAC system has a fully operational economizer.
  • HVAC systems that serve refrigeration or process loads
  • Storage - Unconditioned (SUn) and Warehouse - Refrigerated (WRf) since energy savings were not modeled.

SWSV010-03.Offering ID: The replacement of an existing economizer control sensor or the optimization of the existing economizer controls by adjusting the changeover setpoint.
SWSV010-03.Base Case Description:
The base case for the economizer control replacement measure is an existing economizer equipped with either a snap disc or malfunctioning analog sensor.
The base case for the economizer control adjustment measure is an existing, fully operational economizer with un-optimized economizer controls, such as either low economizer changeover setpoints or inadequate sensors.
SWSV010-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Contractors and technicians implementing the measure must meet all certification and training requirements.
Requirements for the economizer control replacement offerings include: Economizer must have either a snap disc or malfunctioning analog sensor.
Requirements for the economizer control adjustment offerings include:
  • Economizer must be fully operational with an un-optimized analog sensor or controls that require adjustment, such as modifying the changeover setpoint.
  • Additional technician verification of thermostat wiring and the number of cooling stages should be performed to ensure that the first stage of cooling is dedicated to economizer operation and two-stage thermostat operation is enabled where possible.
  • The controller changeover setpoint should be adjusted appropriately based on the available number of thermostat cooling stages.
SWSV010-03.Quality Assurance: This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied. There must be an existing economizer on the applicable HVAC system.

SWSV010-04.Offering ID:
The optimization of the existing economizer controls by adjusting the changeover setpoint.
The economizer control sensor replacement offerings have been migrated to the SWSV005 measure package because the control sensor replacement scope required by the offerings better aligns with the economizer repair scopes outlined in SWSV005.
SWSV010-04.Base Case Description: An existing, fully operational economizer with un-optimized economizer controls, such as low economizer changeover setpoints.
SWSV010-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Contractors and technicians implementing the measure must meet all certification and training requirements.
Requirements for the economizer control adjustment offerings include:
  • Economizer must be fully operational with an un-optimized analog sensor or controls that require adjustment, such as modifying the changeover setpoint.
  • Economizer repairs should be completed before this measure so that the existing economizer is fully functional. If needed, some economizer repair scopes may be eligible under SWSV005.
  • Additional technician verification of thermostat wiring and the number of cooling stages should be performed to ensure that the first stage of cooling is dedicated to economizer operation and two-stage thermostat operation is enabled where possible.
  • The controller changeover setpoint should be adjusted appropriately based on the available number of thermostat cooling stages.
  • The economizer high limit should be optimized for each climate zone as per Title 24 2025 Table 140.4-G.

HVAC systems that serve refrigeration or process loads are not eligible.
SWSV010-04.Quality Assurance: This measure requires field documentation of the existing conditions that verify the measure was necessary and that the measure was successfully applied. There must be an existing economizer on the applicable HVAC system.
SWSV010-04.Important Notes: Storage - Unconditioned (SUn) and Warehouse - Refrigerated (WRf) were not modeled and do not have savings reported.

SWSV013-04.Offering ID: The measure case is defined as duct optimization to reduce total leakage from a high leakage rate to a low leakage rate.
SWSV013-04.Base Case Description: An existing crossover duct or open cavity return duct with the specified leakage rate.
SWSV013-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
When installing these measures, technicians must abide by all applicable codes.
Duct leakage tests titled “Duct Pressurization and Leakage Flow Measurement” and “Duct Leakage to Outside Measurement” are the only two eligible duct leakage tests referenced in California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) Reference Appendix 3, which are the same standards referenced in ANSI/ RESNET/ICC Standard 380-2019. However, only one type of duct leakage test is required to justify duct leakage for the following two offerings:
Return Duct Retrofit
  • Shall be installed by a residential HVAC contractor in a mobile home with an existing open belly return with a supply system in the ceiling. The open belly return is a non-ducted return air system with high leakage. Mobile homes with a ducted return are not eligible for this measure.
  • Installed return ducts shall be made from either galvanized steel, tinplated steel, or aluminum, or shall be listed as Class 0 or Class 1 air ducts under Underwriters' Laboratories, Inc. Standard for Air Ducts, UL181-2013. This measure requires a hard metal ducted return installed directly at the return air plenum.
  • Return ducts in unconditioned spaces shall be insulated with material having an effective thermal resistance (R) of not less than 4.0.
  • Testing: Belly return systems must be verified by a pre and post total leakage duct test at 25 pascals on the return side of the duct system only. This shall be achieved by separating the supply from return side of the duct system by sealing at the air handler/furnace, typically at the filter slot to eliminate supply side leakage from the test.
Crossover Duct Replacement
  • Shall be installed by a residential HVAC contractor in a double-wide mobile home where two or more sections of the home has been joined. The crossover duct connects the supply ducting of two or more sections. The existing ducting must be deteriorated, damaged, or not installed correctly to be eligible for this measure.
  • Crossover ducts shall be made from galvanized steel, tinplated steel, or aluminum.
  • The crossover duct shall be supported and or prevented from ground contact.
  • If located in interior areas, not exposed to outdoor air, crossover ducts shall be insulated with material having an effective thermal resistance (R) of not less than 4.0. If the crossover duct is installed in an area that is exposed to outdoor air, such as outside the building’s envelope, the ducts shall be insulated with material having effective thermal resistance (R) of not less than 8.0.
  • Testing: Crossover duct replacement leakage reduction must be verified by a total leakage test pre and post replacement in accordance with current RESNET standards by a qualified technician. Crossover replacement is done in conjunction with duct sealing covered in the statewide measure SWSV001.
Mobile homes with existing duct leakage reduction less than 20% are not eligible. The final duct leakage rate cannot be more than 17%.

SWSV013-05.Offering ID: The measure case is defined as duct optimization to reduce total leakage from a high leakage rate to a low leakage rate.
SWSV013-05.Base Case Description: An existing crossover duct or open cavity return duct with the specified leakage rate.
SWSV013-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
When installing these measures, technicians must abide by all applicable codes.
Duct leakage tests titled “Duct Pressurization and Leakage Flow Measurement” and “Duct Leakage to Outside Measurement” are the only two eligible duct leakage tests referenced in California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) Reference Appendix 3, which are the same standards referenced in ANSI/ RESNET/ICC Standard 380-2019. However, only one type of duct leakage test is required to justify duct leakage for the following two offerings:
Return Duct Retrofit
  • Shall be installed by a residential HVAC contractor in a mobile home with an existing open belly return with a supply system in the ceiling. The open belly return is a non-ducted return air system with high leakage. Mobile homes with a ducted return are not eligible for this measure.
  • Installed return ducts shall be made from either galvanized steel, tinplated steel, or aluminum, or shall be listed as Class 0 or Class 1 air ducts under Underwriters' Laboratories, Inc. Standard for Air Ducts, UL181-2013. This measure requires a hard metal ducted return installed directly at the return air plenum.
  • Return ducts in unconditioned spaces shall be insulated with material having an effective thermal resistance (R-value) of not less than 8.0.
  • Testing: Belly return systems must be verified by a pre and post total leakage duct test at 25 pascals on the return side of the duct system only. This shall be achieved by separating the supply from return side of the duct system by sealing at the air handler/furnace, typically at the filter slot to eliminate supply side leakage from the test.
Crossover Duct Replacement
  • Shall be installed by a residential HVAC contractor in a double-wide mobile home where two or more sections of the home has been joined. The crossover duct connects the supply ducting of two or more sections. The existing ducting must be deteriorated, damaged, or not installed correctly to be eligible for this measure.
  • Crossover ducts shall be made from galvanized steel, tinplated steel, or aluminum.
  • The crossover duct shall be supported and or prevented from ground contact.
  • If located in interior areas, not exposed to outdoor air, crossover ducts shall be insulated with material having an effective thermal resistance (R) of not less than 4.0. If the crossover duct is installed in an area that is exposed to outdoor air, such as outside the building’s envelope, the ducts shall be insulated with material having effective thermal resistance (R) of not less than 8.0.
  • Testing: Crossover duct replacement leakage reduction must be verified by a total leakage test pre and post replacement in accordance with current RESNET standards by a qualified technician. Crossover replacement is done in conjunction with duct sealing covered in the statewide measure SWSV001.
Mobile homes with existing duct leakage reduction less than 20% are not eligible. The final duct leakage rate cannot be more than 17%.

SWSV014-02.Offering ID: The lifecycle refrigerant management (LRM) provides: (1) electronic refrigerant leak detection, (2) proper training on how to detect, repair, and prevent refrigerant venting, (3) locking Schrader caps (LSC) to prevent 5.3% leakage due to venting and refrigerant huffing, (4) non-invasive temperature diagnostic (NTD) software, and (5) adding at least 7.5% refrigerant charge to undercharged units and preventing 5.3% undercharge for all units. Offerings are characterized by the HVAC unit, the measure application type, the refrigerant type, and the amount of refrigerant charge adjustment (RCA).
SWSV014-02.Base Case Description: The base case is defined as a non-treated AC or HP system.

SWSV014-03.Offering ID: The lifecycle refrigerant management (LRM) provides: (1) Training on refrigerant responsibility and stewardship for recovery, reclamation, and reuse. (2) For new installations, low- and high-pressure leak testing; and evacuating to less than (<) 500 microns using ¾” diameter engineered hoses to remove moisture and non-condensable. (3) Electronic refrigerant leak detection, repair, and prevention. (4) Tightening Schrader core valves to 5 inch-pounds (in-lbs) per manufacturer specifications and installing Nylog blue sealant and Locking Schrader caps (LSC) to prevent 5.3% leakage due venting and refrigerant huffing. (5) Non-invasive temperature diagnostic (NTD) software. (6) Adding at least 7.5% or more refrigerant charge when needed to undercharged units or units with airflow from 200 to 350 cfm/ton. Offerings are characterized by the HVAC unit, the measure application type, the refrigerant type, and the amount of refrigerant charge adjustment (RCA).
SWSV014-03.Base Case Description: The base case is defined as a non-treated AC or HP system.
SWSV014-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The following eligibility requirements must be met, in accordance with the Lifecycle Refrigerant Management Training Manual, before the LRM measure is implemented.
Refrigerant leak detection must be performed using an electronic leak detector. If there is refrigerant leak, repair and preventive measures are required.
Qualified EPA-certified technicians must use refrigerant hoses with EPA-approved low-loss fittings. Technicians are not allowed to connect to high pressure Schrader valves and must adhere to "De minimis" quantities of refrigerant released while making good faith attempts to recapture and recycle or safely dispose of refrigerant including releases that occur when connecting or disconnecting hoses per US EPA regulations (40 CFR Part 82, Subpart F) under Section 608 of the Clean Air Act.
Clean air filters must be installed. Condensing and evaporator (if possible) coils must be cleaned per manufacturer specifications to ensure correct diagnostic measurements.
Non-invasive temperature diagnostics (NTD) software must be used to evaluate airflow and refrigerant charge.
NTD software must be capable of accurately identifying faults (e.g., undercharged/overcharged amount, heat exchanger faults, refrigerant restrictions, and non-condensable) only based on temperature measurements and without connecting refrigerant pressure gauges.
Refrigerant charge measures are only allowed if system is undercharged by ≥7.5% and airflow ≥200 cfm/ton. 200 to 350 cfm/ton airflow is allowed if preventing ice formation on the evaporator coil.
Program exclusions include: AC or heat pump system not operating due to compressor, fan motor, reversing valve, expansion valve, or electrical system failure, significant non-condensable, significant restrictions, and significant unrepairable refrigerant leaks.

(3) Electronic refrigerant leak detection, repair, and prevention. (4) Tightening Schrader core valves to 5 inch-pounds (in-lbs) per manufacturer specifications and installing Nylog blue sealant and Locking Schrader caps (LSC) to prevent 5.3% leakage due venting and refrigerant huffing. (5) Non-invasive temperature diagnostic (NTD) software. (6) Adding at least 7.5% or more refrigerant charge when needed to undercharged units or units with airflow from 200 to 350 cfm/ton. Offerings are characterized by the HVAC unit, the measure application type, the refrigerant type, and the amount of refrigerant charge adjustment (RCA).

SWWB008-02.Offering ID: An all-electric home with electric HVAC, domestic hot water, cooking, and clothes dryer equipment. All of building systems in the all-electric home must be powered by electricity, but individual system eligibility is determined separately and depends on specific minimum eligible efficiencies for each system type.
SWWB008-02.Base Case Description: A mixed fuel home with some of its end uses being served by natural gas equipment.

SWWB008-04.Offering ID: An all-electric home with electric HVAC, domestic hot water, cooking, and clothes dryer equipment. All of building systems in the all-electric home must be powered by electricity, but individual system eligibility is determined separately and depends on specific minimum eligible efficiencies for each system type.
SWWB008-04.Base Case Description: A mixed fuel home with some of its end uses being served by natural gas equipment.
SWWB008-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Measure case and base case must meet or exceed the product and system requirements specified in the Offering ID table. The requirements are adopted mainly from analogous fuel substitution measure packages.
Cooking Appliances:
  • Equipment must have 4 to 5 heating elements
  • Efficiency verification isn’t needed as induction are assumed to be more efficient than standard technologies.
Heat Pump Clothes Dryer:
  • Measure case mut meet ENERGY STAR V1.1 specification or newer
  • Measure case must meet or exceed the minimum combined efficiency factor (CEF) of 4.50.
Storage Heat Pump Water Heater:
  • Only storage heat pump water heaters serving individual dwelling units are. Central storage water heaters that serve more than one dwelling unit or common areas are not eligible.
Ductless Heat Pump HVAC Systems:
  • Measure case must be a ductless split heat pump system. Both mini-split or multi-split systems are eligible.
  • Equipment may not be installed in the same home as central heat pump HVAC system measures.
Central Heat Pump HVAC System:
  • Measure case must be a split-system central or central packaged heat pump.
  • Equipment may not be installed in the same home as the ductless heat pump HVAC system measures.
SWWB008-04.Quality Assurance: Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the requirements in the Offering ID table.

SWWH001-04.Offering ID: A low-flow faucet aerator or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV) on a lavatory or kitchen faucet. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, installed location, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm), and measure application type.
SWWH001-04.Base Case Description: The existing base case is an existing residential lavatory faucet or kitchen faucet without an aerator or FCV, with a flow rate of 2.2 gpm. The standard base case is a kitchen faucet with a flow rate of 1.8 gpm or a lavatory faucet with a flow rate of 1.2 gpm.
SWWH001-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing faucet must have a flow rate of at least 2.2 gpm. The low-flow faucet aerator and flow control valve FCV must meet the measure case maximum flow rate.
For accelerated replacement application types, the retrofit faucet must have an existing faucet aerator. For all other application types, the faucet must not have an aerator installed.
The FCV is only applicable to add-one equipment (AOE) measure application type.
The measure is not applicable to new faucets that meet or exceed the 2021 California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20) code requirement of 1.2 gpm flow rate for lavatory faucet and 1.8 gpm flow rate for kitchen faucet.

SWWH001-05.Offering ID: A low-flow faucet aerator or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV) on a lavatory or kitchen faucet. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, installed location, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm), and measure application type.
SWWH001-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is an existing residential lavatory faucet or kitchen faucet without an aerator or FCV, with a flow rate of 2.2 gpm. The standard base case is a kitchen faucet with a flow rate of 1.8 gpm or a lavatory faucet with a flow rate of 1.2 gpm.
SWWH001-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The existing faucet must have a flow rate of at least 2.2 gpm. The low-flow faucet aerator and flow control valve FCV must meet the measure case maximum flow rate.

The FCV is only applicable to add-one equipment (AOE) measure application type.
The measure is not applicable to new faucets that meet or exceed the 2021 California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20) code requirement of 1.2 gpm flow rate for lavatory faucet and 1.8 gpm flow rate for kitchen faucet.

SWWH002-04.Offering ID: An efficient showerhead or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV) that meets the specified flow rate. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm), and measure application type.
SWWH002-04.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a showerhead with a flow rate of 2.25 gpm. The standard base case is a low-flow showerhead that meets 2021 Title 20 maximum flow rate requirements of 1.8 gpm.

SWWH002-05.Offering ID: An efficient showerhead or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV) that meets the specified flow rate. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm), and measure application type.
SWWH002-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a showerhead with a flow rate of 2.25 gpm. The standard base case is a low-flow showerhead that meets 2021 Title 20 maximum flow rate requirements of 1.8 gpm.
SWWH002-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The low-flow showerhead or flow control valve must meet the EPA WaterSense Specification for Showerheads to ensure it meets the EPA’s criteria for water efficiency and performance.

TSV without a low-flow showerhead is only applicable to add-one equipment (AOE) measure application type.

Low-Flow Showerhead: The product must meet the EPA WaterSense Specification for Showerheads to ensure it meets the EPA’s criteria for water efficiency and performance.
This measure is not applicable where recirculation pumps are used or where potential crossover between hot water and cold water may occur.
The measure is not applicable where tankless water heaters are used; instantaneous tankless water heaters may have different effect on savings with temperature-initiated flow-restriction valves.

SWWH004-03.Offering ID: The installation of a laminar flow restrictor (LFR), also known as a laminar flow device (LFD), that is at the outlet of a faucet spout in a health care facility to reduce the flow rate.

SWWH004-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The device must be installed only in health care facilities that are subject to the Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development (OSHPD) code and regulation/inspection requirements. The device must meet the OSHPD code and regulation.
The LFR must be labeled as “Vandal Proof” or must not be removable without a proprietary tool, except for dialysis and scrub sink locations.

SWWH005-06.Offering ID: An efficient instantaneous water heater or a commercial hot water boiler meeting the uniform energy factor (UEF) requirement for rated input <200 kBtu/hr and the thermal efficiency (TE) requirement for rated input ≥300 kBtu/hr.
SWWH005-06.Base Case Description: The existing and standard base case is an instantaneous water heater or a commercial hot water boiler, in each capacity category, meeting the efficiency specified below, which is consistent with federal, Title 20, and Title 24 regulations.
SWWH005-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Boilers must meet the following eligibility requirements:
  • Meet minimum qualifying efficiency ratings and comply with emission limits per air district, if applicable. Note that higher efficiency boilers are often condensing and may require flue modifications to handle the condensate.
  • Meet the definition of a tankless water heater, as defined by the California Energy Commission:
    • Be used primarily for domestic hot water
    • Provide hot water only when there is a hot water draw from the end use.
    • Have an input rating of at least 4,000 Btu/hr/gal of stored water.
This measure does not include water heaters or hot water boilers used for space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use applications, pools, or spas.
SWWH005-06.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

  • Meet Title 24 Section 140.5 and Section 170.2 prescriptive requirements.

SWWH006-09.Important Notes:
Due to Title 24 prescriptive requirements, this measure package and its savings are not applicable for the following cases
  • Schools: For buildings <25,000 sq.ft. and <4 stories in climate zones 2 - 15. Section 140.5[a][1] requires a heat pump water-heating system for these sites.
  • Hotel/Motel: Occupancies using large central water-heating systems with input ≥100 kBtu/hr in climate zones 1 - 9. Section 170.2[d] or Section 140.5[b] typically mandates solar water heating, site-recovered energy, or a ≥90% TE system in these zones.
  • High-Capacity Service Water-Heating Systems: Gas water-heating systems with a total input rating ≥1 MMBtu/hr (1,000,000 Btu/hr). Under Section 140.5[c], systems must use ≥90% TE gas equipment, provide ≥25% of the annual water-heating load from solar or recovered energy, or meet/exceed code through another advanced approach
  • Any situation where Title 24 or local ordinances require heat pump or solar fraction.
