  • Schools: For buildings <25,000 sq.ft. and <4 stories in climate zones 2 - 15. Section 140.5[a][1] requires a heat pump water-heating system for these sites.
  • Hotel/Motel: Occupancies using large central water-heating systems with input ≥100 kBtu/hr in climate zones 1 - 9. Section 170.2[d] or Section 140.5[b] typically mandates solar water heating, site-recovered energy, or a ≥90% TE system in these zones.
  • High-Capacity Service Water-Heating Systems: Gas water-heating systems with a total input rating ≥1 MMBtu/hr (1,000,000 Btu/hr). Under Section 140.5[c], systems must use ≥90% TE gas equipment, provide ≥25% of the annual water-heating load from solar or recovered energy, or meet/exceed code through another advanced approach
  • Any situation where Title 24 or local ordinances require heat pump or solar fraction.

SWWH008-01.Offering ID: A high-efficiency process boiler with an input rating ≤20,000 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified combustion efficiency (CE) requirement.
SWWH008-01.Base Case Description: The base case is a standard efficiency process water or steam boiler, meeting the 2013 Title 24 minimum efficiency requirement.
SWWH008-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The measure analysis assumed that both the base case and the measure case boiler have the same input rating (in kBtu/hr), deliver the same amount of hot water or steam on an annual basis (i.e., customer demand for hot water or steam does not change).
Water boilers must have an input rating ≤20,000 kBtu/hr and a documented ≥85% CE under full load conditions. Steam boilers must have an input rating ≤20,000 kBtu/hr and a documented ≥83% CE under full load conditions.
This measure is not applicable to boilers used for space heating, domestic hot water, pools, or spas, and is not eligible for domestic hot water or space heating.
SWWH008-01.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH008-02.Offering ID: A high-efficiency process boiler with an input rating ≤20,000 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified combustion efficiency (CE) and thermal efficiency (TE) requirement. It is assumed that there is a 2% loss in converting from CE to TE ratings. Hot water boilers <=2,500,000 Btu/h should be rated and tested at the specified TE standard and boilers >2,500,000 Btu/h should be rated and tested at the specified CE standard.
SWWH008-02.Base Case Description: The base case is a standard efficiency process water or steam boiler, meeting the 2022 Title 24 minimum efficiency requirement.
SWWH008-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The measure analysis assumed that both the base case and the measure case boiler have the same input rating (in kBtu/hr), deliver the same amount of hot water or steam on an annual basis (i.e., customer demand for hot water or steam does not change).
All boilers must have an input rating ≤20,000 kBtu/hr and meet or exceed either the combustion efficiency (CE) or thermal efficiency (TE) under full load conditioners.
This measure is not applicable to boilers used for space heating, domestic hot water, pools, or spas.
SWWH008-02.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH010-02.Offering ID: A high-efficiency instantaneous water heater or hot water boiler of a multifamily central water heating system, meeting the specified thermal efficiency (TE) requirement.
SWWH010-02.Base Case Description: The base case is an instantaneous water heater or hot water boiler of a multifamily central water heating system, meeting the specified minimum efficiency.
SWWH010-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for multifamily DHW boilers defined for this measure include:
  • Measure offerings are applicable to multifamily properties.
  • The boiler must serve two or more dwelling units.
  • Only a gas-for-gas equipment replacement is permitted.
  • A qualifying boiler must meet the minimum efficiency requirements and emission requirements per their AQMD regulations that apply.
  • Any size boiler is eligible.
This measure does not include water heaters or hot water boilers used for space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use applications, pools, or spas.
Storage water heaters are not eligible.
The California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20) and Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) define an instantaneous water heater as “a water heater that has an input rating of at least 4,000 Btu/hr/gallon of stored water”
SWWH010-02.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH010-03.Offering ID: A high-efficiency instantaneous water heater or commercial hot water boiler of a multifamily central water heating system, meeting the specified thermal efficiency (TE) requirement.
SWWH010-03.Base Case Description: An instantaneous water heater or commercial hot water boiler of a multifamily central water heating system, meeting the specified minimum efficiency.
SWWH010-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for multifamily DHW boilers defined for this measure include:
  • Measure offerings are applicable to multifamily properties.
  • The boiler must serve two or more dwelling units.
  • Only a gas-for-gas equipment replacement is permitted.
  • A qualifying boiler must meet the minimum efficiency requirements and emission requirements per their AQMD regulations that apply.
  • Any size boiler is eligible.
This measure does not include water heaters or hot water boilers used for space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use applications, pools, or spas.
SWWH010-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH011-02.Offering ID: An energy efficient central storage water heater with rated input >75 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified thermal efficiency (TE).
SWWH011-02.Base Case Description: A central storage water heater meeting the specified TE, consistent with Title 20, Title 24, and federal codes.
SWWH011-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for storage water heaters defined for this measure include:
  • Measure offerings are applicable to multifamily properties.
  • The large natural gas central water heaters must serve two or more dwelling units.
  • Only gas-for-gas equipment replacement is permitted.
  • A qualifying boiler must meet the minimum efficiency requirements and emission requirements per their AQMD regulations that apply.
  • The rated input for a natural gas fueled storage water heater for this multifamily residential measure must exceed 75 kBtu/hr.
  • Qualifying storage water heaters must have a storage tank size of least 80 gallons.
This measure does not include water heaters or hot water boilers used for space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use applications, pools, or spas.
Tankless water heaters or hot water boilers with a separate storage tank that are factory mounted on the same skid are not eligible.
SWWH011-02.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH011-03.Offering ID: An energy efficient central storage water heater with rated input >75 kBtu/hr, meeting the specified thermal efficiency (TE).
SWWH011-03.Base Case Description: A central storage water heater meeting the specified TE, consistent with Title 20, Title 24, and federal codes.
SWWH011-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for storage water heaters defined for this measure include:
  • Measure offerings are applicable to multifamily properties.
  • The large natural gas central water heaters must serve two or more dwelling units.
  • Only gas-for-gas equipment replacement is permitted.
  • A qualifying boiler must meet the minimum efficiency requirements and emission requirements per their AQMD regulations that apply.
  • The rated input for a natural gas fueled storage water heater for this multifamily residential measure must exceed 75 kBtu/hr.
  • Qualifying storage water heaters must have a storage tank size of least 80 gallons.
This measure does not include water heaters or hot water boilers used for space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use applications, pools, or spas.
Tankless water heaters or hot water boilers with a separate storage tank that are factory mounted on the same skid are not eligible.
SWWH011-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH012-03.Offering ID: An electric or gas storage water heater with 30, 40, or 50 gallon storage capacity, meeting the specified uniform (U) energy factor (EF).
SWWH012-03.Base Case Description: An electric or gas storage water heater with 30, 40, or 50 gallon storage capacity, meeting the specified EF or UEF requirement.
SWWH012-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible water heaters must comply with the measure case specification (see Measure Case Description).
Only storage water heaters as defined by the California Energy Commission qualify, and they must:
  • Be used primarily for domestic hot water.
  • Have an input rating of less than 4,000 Btu/hr/gal of stored water.
Equipment must comply with the test methods referenced in the California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) and the California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20). Additionally, the equipment must meet minimum emissions requirements as defined by local air quality management districts.
Water heaters used for commercial domestic hot water, space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use, pool, or spa applications are not eligible.
Measure requires “like-for-like” replacement (i.e., electric replacing electric and gas replacing gas).
SWWH012-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH012-05.Offering ID: An electric or gas storage water heater with 30, 40, or 50 gallon storage capacity, meeting the specified uniform (U) energy factor (EF).
SWWH012-05.Base Case Description: An electric or gas storage water heater with 30, 40, or 50 gallon storage capacity, meeting the specified EF or UEF requirement.
SWWH012-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible water heaters must comply with the measure case specification (see Measure Case Description).
Only storage water heaters as defined by the California Energy Commission qualify, and they must:
  • Be used primarily for domestic hot water.
  • Have an input rating of less than 4,000 Btu/hr/gal of stored water.
Equipment must comply with the test methods referenced in the California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) and the California Appliance Efficiency Regulations (Title 20). Additionally, the equipment must meet minimum emissions requirements as defined by local air quality management districts.
Water heaters used for commercial domestic hot water, space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use, pool, or spa applications are not eligible.
Measure requires “like-for-like” replacement (i.e., electric replacing electric and gas replacing gas).
SWWH012-05.Quality Assurance:
Due to Title 24 prescriptive requirements, this measure package and its savings are not applicable for the following cases
  • Section 150.1(c)(8) requires 240V heat pump (HP) water heater (WH) for single-family in all climate zones, except 3, 4, 13, and 14 which may instead have a gas tankless WH installed with an input of ≤200,000 Btu/hr when space-heating system is a heat pump per section 150.1(c)(6). Small homes <500 sq.ft. may use a tankless electric water heater in all climate zones. Studio and one-bedroom homes may use 120V HP WH in all climate zones.
  • Under Section 170.2(d)(1), either 240V HPWH or gas or propane tankless water heaters can be used for multifamily dwelling in all climate zones.

SWWH013-03.Offering ID: A tankless water heater in a residential application, meeting the specified uniform (U) energy factor (EF), replacing a storage or instantaneous water heater.
SWWH013-03.Base Case Description: A natural gas storage or instantaneous water heater meeting the specified EF or UEF, consistent with Title 20, Title 24, and federal code.
SWWH013-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Equipment must meet the minimum qualifying UEF for a small or medium (<200 kBtu/hr) tankless water heater.
Equipment must meet the minimum emission requirements per air quality management district, if applicable.
Only tankless water heaters, as defined by the California Energy Commission (CEC), qualify; they must:
  • Be used primarily for domestic hot water
  • Be installed at the point of use
  • Provide hot water only when there is a hot water draw from the end use
  • Have an input rating of at least 4,000 Btu per hour per gallon of stored water
  • Not be connected to an external storage tank
  • Never be used to supply hot water to a circulation loop
Water heaters or hot water boilers used for commercial domestic hot water, space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use, pool, or spa applications are not eligible.
SWWH013-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH013-03.Important Notes: Since it is considered industry standard practice to purchase a storage water heater, offerings B and C are at code.

SWWH013-05.Offering ID: A tankless water heater in a residential application, meeting the specified uniform (U) energy factor (EF), replacing a storage or instantaneous water heater.
SWWH013-05.Base Case Description: A natural gas storage or instantaneous water heater meeting the specified EF or UEF, consistent with Title 20, Title 24, and federal code.
SWWH013-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Equipment must meet the minimum qualifying UEF for a small or medium (<200 kBtu/hr) tankless water heater.
Equipment must meet the minimum emission requirements per air quality management district, if applicable.
Only tankless water heaters, as defined by the California Energy Commission (CEC), qualify; they must:
  • Be used primarily for domestic hot water
  • Be installed at the point of use
  • Provide hot water only when there is a hot water draw from the end use
  • Have an input rating of at least 4,000 Btu per hour per gallon of stored water
  • Not be connected to an external storage tank
  • Never be used to supply hot water to a circulation loop
Water heaters or hot water boilers used for commercial domestic hot water, space conditioning, industrial (process) end-use, pool, or spa applications are not eligible.
SWWH013-05.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH013-05.Important Notes:
Due to Title 24 prescriptive requirements, this measure package and its savings are not applicable for the following cases
  • Section 150.1(c)(8) requires 240V heat pump (HP) water heater (WH) for single-family in all climate zones, except 3, 4, 13, and 14 which may instead have a gas tankless WH installed with an input of ≤200,000 Btu/hr when space-heating system is a heat pump per section 150.1(c)(6). Small homes <500 sq.ft. may use a tankless electric water heater in all climate zones. Studio and one-bedroom homes may use 120V HP WH in all climate zones.
  • Under Section 170.2(d)(1), either 240V HPWH or gas or propane tankless water heaters can be used for multifamily dwelling in all climate zones.

SWWH014-05.Offering ID: An efficient heat pump (HP) water heater (WH) meeting the specified uniform energy factor (UEF) replacing a base case electric storage or heat pump water heater.
SWWH014-05.Base Case Description: An electric storage (for 30, 40, or 50 gallons) or HP (60 or 75 gallons) water heater meeting the specified UEF requirement, consistent with Title 10 code. Baselines reflect code UEFs and those on the market based on AHRI/CEC database analysis.
SWWH014-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The new HPWH must meet the storage capacity and minimum efficiency requirements. The installed WHs storage capacity must be ≥45 gallons.
The 60- and 75-gallons offerings use HPWH baseline assumptions in the calculations. Therefore, these offerings can be used to replace less efficient base case equipment, such as electric resistance storage water heaters, since the savings are conservative.
To accommodate water heater storage capacities that do not exactly match the nominal storage capacity, the following shows the range of rated storage capacities that are eligible for each nominal storage capacity listed for baseline equipment:
  • 30 gal: 26 gal - 35 gal
  • 40 gal: 36 gal - 45 gal
  • 50 gal: 46 gal - 55 gal
  • 60 gal: 56 gal - 69 gal
  • 75 gal: 70 gal - 89 gal

The new construction (NC) measure application type is only eligible for offerings that use a HPWH baseline.
The following are ineligible:
  • Replacement of an “instantaneous” or “tankless” water heater
  • A new HPWH with <45 gallons
  • Adding a new water heater to serve new load associated with added conditioned floor area in an existing building
SWWH014-05.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify the unit capacity is adequately sized for the base case replacement.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH014-05.Important Notes:
Only electric storage WHs are eligible as baselines. For the fuel substitution measure, see SWWH025.
HPWHs use the surrounding air as a heat source. If the HWHP is installed within the home, it imposes aheating load on the home’s air conditioning system. Unit efficiency decreases as the ambient temperature falls below 68°F. If ambient space temperature falls below 45°F, the unit’s electrical resistance heaters are enabled, significantly lowering the heater’s energy performance.

SWWH014-07.Offering ID: An efficient heat pump (HP) water heater (WH) meeting the specified uniform energy factor (UEF) replacing a base case electric storage or heat pump water heater.
SWWH014-07.Base Case Description: An electric storage water heater with a storage volume of 30, 40, or 50 gallons or a heat pump water heater with a storage volume of 60 or 75 gallons meeting the specified federal code UEF requirement.
SWWH014-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The new HPWH must meet the storage capacity and minimum efficiency requirements. In order to accommodate upsizing projects, the new heat pump water heater storage capacity may exceed the measure case storage capacity ranges. For example, for offering R "HP water heater: 45 to 55 gal (UEF = 3.30) replacing electric storage water heater: 50 gal (UEF = 0.92)", a new heat pump water heater of 60 or 80 gallons may be installed if needed.
The installed WHs storage capacity must be ≥45 gallons.
The 60- and 75-gallons offerings use HPWH baseline assumptions in the calculations. Therefore, these offerings can be used to replace less efficient base case equipment, such as electric resistance storage water heaters, since the savings are conservative.
To accommodate water heater storage capacities that do not exactly match the nominal storage capacity, the following shows the range of rated storage capacities that are eligible for each nominal storage capacity listed for baseline equipment:
  • 30 gal: 26 gal - 35 gal
  • 40 gal: 36 gal - 45 gal
  • 50 gal: 46 gal - 55 gal
  • 60 gal: 56 gal - 69 gal
  • 75 gal: 70 gal - 89 gal

The new construction (NC) measure application type is only eligible for offerings that use a HPWH baseline, which are offerings Y, Z, AA, AB, AC, and AD.
The following are ineligible:
  • Replacement of an “instantaneous” or “tankless” water heater
  • A new HPWH with <45 gallons
  • Adding a new water heater to serve new load associated with added conditioned floor area in an existing building
SWWH014-07.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify the unit capacity is adequately sized for the base case replacement.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH014-07.Important Notes:
Only electric storage WHs are eligible as baselines. For the fuel substitution measure, see SWWH025.
HPWHs use the surrounding air as a heat source. If the HWHP is installed within the home, it imposes aheating load on the home’s air conditioning system. Unit efficiency decreases as the ambient temperature falls below 68°F. If ambient space temperature falls below 45°F, the unit’s electrical resistance heaters are enabled, significantly lowering the heater’s energy performance.

SWWH015-03.Offering ID: An on-demand domestic hot water (DHW) pump that utilizes flow control methods, such as variable frequency drives (VFDs), integrated or remote sensors, and control systems that respond to demand by controlling the hot water recirculation loop temperature.
SWWH015-03.Base Case Description: A centralized, natural gas DHW system with a recirculation pump that operates continuously in multifamily and commercial building.
SWWH015-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure is only applicable to existing buildings with systems that utilize stand‐alone domestic hot water systems. Hot water generation systems must be used primarily for domestic hot‐water heating uses. Hot‐water generation systems used for pool or spa heating are not eligible for this measure.
The hot water supply and hot water return lines of the recirculation loop must maintain a temperature difference of at least 20°F. For example, for the energy modeling purposes of this workpaper, the supply temperature was set at 135 °F and the return temperature was maintained at 105°F.
Facilities that must maintain hot water temperature of 140°F to control legionella bacteria are not eligible. Local, state and federal regulations must be followed to avoid the risk of legionella growth as well as scalding.
The domestic hot water recirculation loop is located exclusively within the building envelope’s conditioned space.

SWWH016-03.Offering ID: The installation of a “temperature modulation” boiler controller in the central domestic hot water (DHW) system to minimize the supply and return water temperatures and piping heat losses. Some facilities might require more than one boiler controller; offerings are characterized by the number of dwelling units controlled by the controller.
SWWH016-03.Base Case Description: A DHW system without “temperature modulation” boiler control or demand-controlled recirculation pump. The base case typically maintains a continuous ≥135°F supply temperature, which is sufficient to meet the demand requirements at design conditions but is excessive otherwise and will result in high distribution system losses.
SWWH016-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure qualifies only for gas central hot-water systems used primarily for domestic hot-water heating. Central hot water systems with demand-controlled recirculation pumps are not eligible.
Facilities that must maintain hot water temperature of 140°F to control legionella bacteria are not eligible. Local, state and federal regulations must be followed to avoid the risk of legionella growth as well as scalding.

SWWH016-05.Offering ID: The installation of a “temperature modulation” boiler controller in the central water heating system of qualifying facilities (multifamily, hotel, motel, nursing home and university dormitories) to minimize the supply and return water temperatures and piping heat losses. Some facilities might require more than one boiler controller; offerings are characterized by the number of dwelling units controlled by the controller.
SWWH016-05.Base Case Description: An existing gas storage water heating system of qualifying facilities without “temperature modulation” boiler control or demand-controlled recirculation pump. The base case typically maintains a continuous ≥135°F supply temperature, which is sufficient to meet the demand requirements at design conditions but is excessive otherwise and will result in high distribution system losses.
SWWH016-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure qualifies only for gas central hot-water systems used primarily for domestic hot-water heating. Central hot water systems with demand-controlled recirculation pumps are not eligible.
Facilities that must maintain hot water temperature of 140°F to control legionella bacteria are not eligible. Local, state and federal regulations must be followed to avoid the risk of legionella growth as well as scalding.

SWWH017-05.Offering ID: The installation of a 1-inch insulation layer to an existing bare pipe system that transports hot liquid to reduce the amount of heat loss throughout the pipe length or through the fitting surface area. Offerings are characterized by the pipe location, pipe diameter, fitting type and water heater type.
SWWH017-05.Base Case Description: A bare (un-insulated) pipe system used to transport hot liquid at a non-residential or multifamily facility.
SWWH017-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for hot water pipe insulation include:
  • The pipe must transfer hot water, low-pressure steam, or medium-pressure steam directly from gas-fired, electric, or HPWH equipment, and the fluid type must be indicated. If the fluid is steam, the pressure of the steam must also be indicated.
  • The minimum qualifying pipe diameter is ½-inch.
  • A minimum of one inch of pipe insulation must be added to existing bare commercial or industrial steel or copper pipe.
  • Acceptable types of insulation for hot water pipes include elastomeric foam rubber, polyethylene foam, UV-resistant polyethylene foam, and rigid polyurethane foam.
  • Acceptable types of insulation for steam pipes include silicone foam rubber, melamine foam, rigid urethane-based foam, cellular glass, rigid fiberglass, and rigid mineral wool.

The following conditions and applications are ineligible:
  • New construction applications or retrofit/additions that trigger compliance with current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) requirements. The 2022 Title 24, Section 110.3 establishes requirements for tank insulation in the design and installation of space-conditioning and service water heating systems and equipment. (This would be the case for both new facilities and facilities undergoing renovations that would trigger code compliance.)
  • Insulation required by employee safety laws (Occupational Safety and Health Administration, OSHA). The California OSHA Standards Board, Section 3308 requires employers to insulate or guard hot pipes and hot surfaces of 140°F or higher that are located within seven feet of the floor or within 15 inches from stairways, ramps, or fixed ladders.
  • Replacement of damaged (existing) insulation
  • Residential steam pipe and fitting insulations

SWWH017-06.Offering ID: The installation of an insulation layer to an existing bare pipe system that transports hot liquid to reduce the amount of heat loss throughout the pipe length or through the fitting surface area. Offerings are characterized by the pipe location, pipe diameter, fitting type (pipe v. fittings), water heater type, and pipe surface temperature. Insulation thickness varies by the pipe diameter and surface temperature, as stipulated in 2022 Title 24 Table 120.3, Table 160.3, and Table 160.4.
SWWH017-06.Base Case Description: A bare (un-insulated) pipe system used to transport hot liquid at a non-residential or multifamily facility.
SWWH017-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for hot water pipe insulation include:
  • The minimum qualifying pipe diameter is ½-inch.
  • The pipe must transfer hot water, low-pressure steam, or medium-pressure steam directly from gas-fired, electric, or HPWH equipment, and the fluid type must be indicated. If the fluid is steam, the pressure of the steam must also be indicated.
  • Fitting installations are for simple fittings (tees, couplings, elbows).
  • The pipe insulation must meet the required thickness and must be added to existing bare steel or copper pipe.
  • Acceptable types of insulation for hot water pipes include elastomeric foam rubber, polyethylene foam, UV-resistant polyethylene foam, and rigid polyurethane foam.
  • Acceptable types of insulation for steam pipes include silicone foam rubber, melamine foam, rigid urethane-based foam, cellular glass, rigid fiberglass, and rigid mineral wool.
The following are ineligible:
  • Fittings which require regular service (flanges, valves)
  • New construction applications or retrofit/additions that trigger compliance with current California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) requirements. The 2022 Title 24, Section 110.3 establishes requirements for tank insulation in the design and installation of space-conditioning and service water heating systems and equipment. (This would be the case for both new facilities and facilities undergoing renovations that would trigger code compliance.)
  • Insulation required by employee safety laws (Occupational Safety and Health Administration, OSHA). The California OSHA Standards Board, Section 3308 requires employers to insulate or guard hot pipes and hot surfaces of 140 °F or higher that are located within seven feet of the floor or within 15 inches from stairways, ramps, or fixed ladders
  • Replacement of damaged (existing) insulation
  • Residential steam pipe and fitting insulations

SWWH018-04.Offering ID: The installation of 1-in or 2-in insulation in an existing uninsulated hot water tank. Offerings are characterized by the insulation thickness, the process temperature range (i.e., temperature inside the tank), water usage (defined by the hours of operation), and tank location (i.e., indoor v. outdoor).
SWWH018-04.Base Case Description: An existing bare, uninsulated hot water tank used for domestic, service or process liquid or solutions applications in commercial, industrial, agriculture facilities, and multifamily central water heating facilities. Tank usually stores ≥100 gallons.
SWWH018-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires the installation of 1-inch or 2-inch fiberglass or foam insulation to an existing, bare liquid solution storage or transfer tank. The tank must be coupled to gas-fired commercial, industrial, agriculture, or multifamily central equipment that transfers heat to the contained liquid or solution.
The following are ineligible:
  • New construction applications or retrofit/additions that trigger compliance with current California Title 24 requirements. The 2016 Title 24, Section 110.3 establishes requirements for tank insulation in the design and installation of space-conditioning and service water heating systems and equipment. (This would be the case for both new facilities and facilities undergoing renovations that would trigger code compliance.)
  • Insulation required by California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) or employee safety laws (Occupational Safety and Health Administration, OSHA).
  • Tanks insulated to prevent burns
  • The California OSHA Standards Board, Section 3308 requires employers to insulate or guard hot pipes and hot surfaces of 140°F or higher that are located within seven feet of the floor or within 15 inches from stairways, ramps, or fixed ladders.
  • Replacement of old or damaged insulation

SWWH018-05.Offering ID: The installation of 1-in or 2-in insulation in an existing uninsulated hot water tank. Offerings are characterized by the insulation thickness, the process temperature range (i.e., temperature inside the tank), water usage (defined by the hours of operation), and tank location (i.e., indoor v. outdoor).
SWWH018-05.Base Case Description: An existing bare, uninsulated hot water tank used for domestic, service or process liquid or solutions applications in commercial, industrial, agriculture facilities, and multifamily central water heating facilities. Tank usually stores ≥100 gallons.
SWWH018-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
This measure requires the installation of 1-inch or 2-inch fiberglass or foam insulation to an existing, bare liquid solution storage or transfer tank. The tank must be coupled to gas-fired commercial, industrial, agriculture, or multifamily central equipment that transfers heat to the contained liquid or solution.
The following are ineligible:
  • New construction applications or retrofit/additions that trigger compliance with current California Title 24 requirements. The 2016 Title 24, Section 110.3 establishes requirements for tank insulation in the design and installation of space-conditioning and service water heating systems and equipment. (This would be the case for both new facilities and facilities undergoing renovations that would trigger code compliance.)
  • Insulation required by California Building Energy Efficiency Standards (Title 24) or employee safety laws (Occupational Safety and Health Administration, OSHA).
  • Tanks insulated to prevent burns
  • The California OSHA Standards Board, Section 3308 requires employers to insulate or guard hot pipes and hot surfaces of 140°F or higher that are located within seven feet of the floor or within 15 inches from stairways, ramps, or fixed ladders.
  • Replacement of old or damaged insulation

SWWH019-05.Offering ID: A low-flow faucet aerator or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV) on an existing faucet in a public or private lavatory in a commercial building. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, installed location, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), and flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm).
SWWH019-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a faucet with no aerator in a public or private lavatory in a commercial building. The base case flow rate is 1.67 gpm.
SWWH019-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A private lavatory faucet is located in an individual dwelling unit such as a hotel/motel guest room, dorm room, or nursing home room. A public lavatory faucet is located in a bathroom shared by a communal area, such as a school, restaurant, hotel lobby, or office building.
This energy efficiency measure is applicable to an existing faucet in a commercial building that has a flow rate of ≥1.67 gpm.
Faucets at health care facilities that are subject to the Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development (OSHPD) code and regulation (e.g. hospitals, clinics, skilled nursing facilities) are not applicable for this measure. The use of aerators is banned in the health care industry due to aerator flow control methods and components. The mixing of air and water within the aerator allows airborne bacteria to become waterborne and, in warm stagnant conditions, promote bacterial growth. Non-aerating laminar flow restrictors (LFRs) must be installed on faucets in these facilities.
The FCV is only applicable to add-one equipment (AOE) measure application type.

SWWH019-06.Offering ID: A low-flow faucet aerator or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV) on an existing faucet in a public or private lavatory in a commercial building. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, installed location, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), and flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm).
SWWH019-06.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a faucet with no aerator in a public or private lavatory in a commercial building. The base case flow rate is 1.67 gpm.
SWWH019-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
A private lavatory faucet is located in an individual dwelling unit such as a hotel/motel guest room, dorm room, or nursing home room. A public lavatory faucet is located in a bathroom shared by a communal area, such as a school, restaurant, hotel lobby, or office building.
This energy efficiency measure is applicable to an existing faucet in a commercial building that has a flow rate of ≥1.67 gpm.
Faucets at health care facilities that are subject to the Office of Statewide Health Planning and Development (OSHPD) code and regulation (e.g. hospitals, clinics, skilled nursing facilities) are not applicable for this measure. The use of aerators is banned in the health care industry due to aerator flow control methods and components. The mixing of air and water within the aerator allows airborne bacteria to become waterborne and, in warm stagnant conditions, promote bacterial growth. Non-aerating laminar flow restrictors (LFRs) must be installed on faucets in these facilities.
The FCV is only applicable to add-one equipment (AOE) measure application type.

SWWH020-05.Offering ID: An efficient showerhead or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV), in a commercial facility, that meets the specified flow rate. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm), and measure application type.
SWWH020-05.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a showerhead with a flow rate of 2.25 gpm, derived from flow rate data collected by Blackstone at five hotel/motel properties in Southern California. The standard base case is a low-flow showerhead that meets 2021 Title 20 maximum flow rate requirements of 1.8 gpm.
SWWH020-05.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The measure for the accelerated replacement of low-flow showerheads is applicable for the replacement of the existing showerhead of 2.5 gpm flow rate or greater with a low-flow showerhead of 1.8 gpm or lower flow rate. The measure for the installation of low-flow showerheads is applicable for the normal replacement/new construction of the existing showerhead of 1.8 gpm flow rate or greater with a low-flow showerhead of 1.7 gpm or lower flow rate. The installed low-flow showerhead shall meet the requirements of test procedure ANSI/ASME A112.18.1-2000, Section 5.5
The measure for the installation of flow control valves is applicable for the add-on equipment onto an existing showerhead with the flow rate of 2.5 gpm or greater, reducing the flow rate to 1.8 gpm or lower.
The facility must have natural gas, heat pump water heater, or electric as the water heating source fuel.

SWWH020-06.Offering ID: An efficient showerhead or the installation of a flow control valve (FCV), in a commercial facility, that meets the specified flow rate. Offerings are characterized by the equipment type, water heater type and fuel (heat pump, electric resistance, or natural gas), flow rate in gallons per minute (gpm), and measure application type.
SWWH020-06.Base Case Description: The existing base case is a showerhead with a flow rate of 2.25 gpm, derived from flow rate data collected by Blackstone at five hotel/motel properties in Southern California. The standard base case is a low-flow showerhead that meets 2021 Title 20 maximum flow rate requirements of 1.8 gpm.
SWWH020-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The measure for the accelerated replacement of low-flow showerheads is applicable for the replacement of the existing showerhead of 2.5 gpm flow rate or greater with a low-flow showerhead of 1.8 gpm or lower flow rate. The measure for the installation of low-flow showerheads is applicable for the normal replacement/new construction of the existing showerhead of 1.8 gpm flow rate or greater with a low-flow showerhead of 1.7 gpm or lower flow rate. The installed low-flow showerhead shall meet the requirements of test procedure ANSI/ASME A112.18.1-2000, Section 5.5.
The measure for the installation of flow control valves is applicable for the add-on equipment onto an existing showerhead with the flow rate of 2.5 gpm or greater, reducing the flow rate to 1.8 gpm or lower.
The facility must have natural gas, heat pump water heater, or electric as the water heating source fuel.

SWWH021-01.Offering ID: The installation of a timer control to a recirculation loop in a commercial building. Offerings are characterized by the recirculation pump horsepower.
SWWH021-01.Base Case Description: A continuously operating recirculation pump from which hot water is always available at the fixture, irrespective of hot water loop temperature or system demand.
SWWH021-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligibility requirements for this measure include the following:
  • The building must have a hot water recirculation system that can be turned OFF entirely for a set period of the day and/or week.
  • The building must have a centralized, gas-fired, hot-water system with a constant-flow, fractional-horsepower, uncontrolled recirculation pump.
  • The timer device must have seven-day (or better) scheduling capabilities.
  • There is no restriction on manufacturer or brand-naming.

SWWH021-02.Offering ID: The installation of a timer control to a recirculation loop in a commercial building. Offerings are characterized by the recirculation pump horsepower.
SWWH021-02.Base Case Description: A continuously operating recirculation pump from which hot water is always available at the fixture, irrespective of hot water loop temperature or system demand.
SWWH021-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligibility requirements for this measure include the following:
  • The building must have a hot water recirculation system that can be turned OFF entirely for a set period of the day and/or week.
  • The building must have a centralized, gas-fired, hot-water system with a constant-flow, fractional-horsepower, uncontrolled recirculation pump.
  • The timer device must have seven-day (or better) scheduling capabilities.
There is no restriction on manufacturer or brand-naming.

SWWH023-02.Offering ID: An auto-diverting tub spout (ADTS) (5.0 gallon per minute - gpm) with a thermostatic shut-off valve (TSV) that purges cold water through tub spout until the water rises to 95°F and then diverts the water to the showerhead at a trickle until full flow (1.5 gpm) is activated.
SWWH023-02.Base Case Description: For new construction and normal replacement, the standard base case is a 5-gpm tub spout and showerhead meeting the 2016 Title 20 requirement of 2.0 gpm. For accelerated replacement, the existing baseline is a 5-gpm tub spout and a 2.25 gpm showerhead; the standard base case is a 5 gpm tub spout and showerhead meeting the 2018 Title 20 requirement of 1.8 gpm.
SWWH023-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The ADTS with TSV must meet the required specification. Additionally, the product must be certified by International Association of Plumbing and Mechanical Officials (IAPMO).
The measure cannot be applied where a tankless water heater is used.
Instantaneous tankless water heaters may have different effects on savings with thermostatic shut-off valves.
This measure is not compatible with showers with a wall-mounted diverter.

SWWH023-03.Offering ID: An auto-diverting tub spout (ADTS) (5.0 gallon per minute - gpm) with a thermostatic shut-off valve (TSV) that purges cold water through tub spout until the water rises to 95°F and then diverts the water to the showerhead at a trickle until full flow (1.5 gpm) is activated.
SWWH023-03.Base Case Description: For new construction and normal replacement, the standard base case is a 5-gpm tub spout and showerhead meeting the 2016 Title 20 requirement of 2.0 gpm. SWWH025-07.Offering ID: A residential heat pump water heater meeting the specified uniform energy factor (UEF) requirement replacing a base case natural gas water heater.
SWWH025-07.Base Case Description: A residential natural gas storage or instantaneous water heater meeting the minimum federal code UEF requirement.
SWWH025-07.Other: Program Exclusion / /: Replacement of electric baseline equipment is not eligible. For replacing existing electric water, see SWWH014.
SWWH025-07.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH025-07.Important Notes: This is a fuel substitution measure.

SWWH025-09.Offering ID: A residential heat pump water heater meeting the specified uniform energy factor (UEF) requirement replacing a base case natural gas water heater.
SWWH025-09.Base Case Description: A residential natural gas storage or instantaneous water heater meeting the minimum federal code UEF requirement.
SWWH025-09.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The new heat pump water heater must meet the storage capacity and minimum efficiency requirements. In order to accommodate upsizing projects, the new heat pump water heater storage capacity may exceed the measure case storage capacity ranges. For example, for offering AH "Heat pump water heater, >= 45 to <= 55 gal, UEF = 3.30 replacing storage natural gas water heater, 50 gal, UEF = 0.63", a new heat pump water heater of 60 or 80 gallons may be installed if needed.
To accommodate water heater storage capacities that do not exactly match the nominal storage capacity, the following shows the range of rated storage capacities that are eligible for each nominal storage capacity listed for baseline equipment:
  • 30 gal: 26 gal - 35 gal
  • 40 gal: 36 gal - 45 gal
  • 50 gal: 46 gal - 55 gal
  • 60 gal: 56 gal - 69 gal
  • 75 gal: 70 gal - 89 gal
Replacement of electric baseline equipment is not eligible. For replacing existing electric water, see SWWH014.
SWWH025-09.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH025-09.Important Notes: This is a fuel substitution measure.

SWWH026-03.Offering ID: The installation of minimum 1-inch insulation to an existing residential bare pipe system on a storage water heater. Offerings are characterized by the pipe location, water heater fuel, and water heater type.
SWWH026-03.Base Case Description: An existing bare (un-insulated) pipe within the first three feet of both hot water and cold-water supply lines connected to a residential storage water heater.
SWWH026-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for the addition of insulation to hot water and cold-water pipes, within the first three feet of a water heater include:
  • A minimum of 1-inch of pipe insulation must be added to existing bare pipe
  • Acceptable types of insulation for hot water pipes include elastomeric foam rubber, polyethylene foam, UV-resistant polyethylene foam, and rigid polyurethane foam
  • Must be installed by an authorized implementer
  • Must verify no existing pipe insulation as the existing condition
  • Unconditioned garage spaces are considered “indoor” installations

SWWH026-04.Offering ID: The installation of minimum 1-inch insulation to an existing residential bare pipe system on a storage water heater. Offerings are characterized by the pipe location, water heater fuel, and water heater type.
SWWH026-04.Base Case Description: An existing bare (un-insulated) pipe within the first three feet of both hot water and cold-water supply lines connected to a residential storage water heater.

  • Must be installed by an authorized implementer
  • Must verify no existing pipe insulation as the existing condition
  • Unconditioned garage spaces are considered “indoor” installations

SWWH026-06.Offering ID: The installation of at least 1-inch insulation on the first three feet of both hot and cold-water supply lines connected to an uninsulated 1-inch or smaller pipe system of an in-dwelling storage water heater. Offerings are characterized by the pipe location, water heater fuel, and water heater type.
SWWH026-06.Base Case Description: An existing bare (un-insulated) 1-inch or smaller pipe within the first three feet of both hot water and cold-water supply lines connected to a residential storage water heater.
SWWH026-06.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Program eligibility requirements for the addition of insulation to hot water and cold water pipes, within the first three feet of a water heater include:

  • Acceptable types of insulation for hot water pipes include elastomeric foam rubber, polyethylene foam, UV-resistant polyethylene foam, and rigid polyurethane foam
  • Must be installed by an authorized implementer
  • Must verify no existing pipe insulation as the existing condition
  • Unconditioned garage spaces are considered “indoor” installations
This measure is not applicable for commercial or industrial applications.

SWWH027-04.Offering ID: A commercial heat pump water heater meeting the specified uniform energy factor (UEF) requirement replacing a base case natural gas water heater.
SWWH027-04.Base Case Description: A commercial natural gas storage or instantaneous water heater meeting the minimum federal code UEF requirement.
SWWH027-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The HPWH must meet the storage capacity and minimum efficiency requirements.
This measure is only applicable to 1-for-1 replacements. Projects where multiple HPWHs are replacing a different quantity of existing natural gas WH should refer to SWWH028.
Split-system heat pump unit assemblies are eligible and shall have the same eligibility and data collection requirements as integrated tanked systems. However, they shall be treated as a single product with a single product efficiency and storage volume. Efficiency and performance ratings for the entire package shall be provided by the manufacturer.
To accommodate baseline water heater storage capacities that do not exactly match the nominal storage capacity, the following shows the range of rated storage capacities that are eligible for each nominal storage capacity listed for baseline equipment. Furthermore, tankless natural gas baseline water heaters of any draw pattern (very small, low, medium, or high) are eligible as baselines.
  • 30 gal: 26 gal - 35 gal
  • 40 gal: 36 gal - 45 gal
  • 50 gal: 46 gal - 55 gal
  • 60 gal: 56 gal - 69 gal
  • 75 gal: 70 gal - 89 gal
Partial retrofits of existing water heating systems are eligible (i.e. replacing only some natural gas water heaters in a system with heat pumps, but not all) as long as the HPWHs are not backup/redundant systems and the natural gas water heaters that are being replaced are fully demolished.
This measure is not eligible for water heaters used for non-domestic hot water uses, such as space conditioning, industrial (process) loads, and pools and/or spas applications. Combination systems that serve domestic hot water and space heating are not eligible.
Redundancy (backup) systems are not eligible. However, heat pumps that are staged based on load or set up in a lead-lag configuration are eligible for this measure package.
For AR offerings, the baseline systems must not be high efficiency condensing systems.
SWWH027-04.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH027-04.Important Notes: This is a fuel substitution measure.

To accommodate baseline water heater storage capacities that do not exactly match the nominal storage capacity, the following shows the range of rated storage capacities that are eligible for each nominal storage capacity listed for baseline equipment.   • 30 gal: 26 gal - 35 gal

  • 50 gal: 46 gal - 55 gal
  • 60 gal: 56 gal - 69 gal
  • 75 gal: 70 gal - 89 gal
Partial retrofits of existing water heating systems are eligible (i.e. replacing only some natural gas water heaters in a system with heat pumps, but not all) as long as the HPWHs are not backup/redundant systems and the natural gas water heaters that are being replaced are fully demolished.
The following are ineligible:
  • Non-domestic hot water uses, such as space conditioning, industrial (process) loads, and pools and/or spas applications. Combination systems that serve domestic hot water and space heating are not eligible.
  • Redundancy (backup) systems. However, heat pumps that are staged based on load or set up in a lead-lag configuration are eligible.
  • For AR offerings, replacement of high efficiency condensing systems
SWWH027-06.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH028-04.Offering ID: A large commercial heat pump water heater or central heat pump water heating system, with output heating capacities >150 kBtu/hr for multifamily and ≥75 gallons for commercial, meeting the specified coefficient of performance (COP) requirement replacing a base case natural gas water heater.
SWWH028-04.Base Case Description: A large commercial natural gas central boiler, central storage, or instantaneous water heater, with output heating capacities >150 kBtu/hr for multifamily and ≥75 gallons for commercial, meeting the minimum federal code thermal efficiency (Et) requirement.
SWWH028-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The HPWH must meet the storage capacity, heating output capacity, and minimum efficiency requirements.
Central domestic hot water systems in commercial or multifamily buildings often consist of one or more HPs connected to one or more storage tanks. Efficiencies, capacities, and other operating parameters for these types of systems differ from their individual components and vary based on system configurations and local conditions. However, this system level information is often not readily available. Therefore, for this MP, the COP at full output capacity of the individual HPWH shall be used to establish if the equipment meets measure efficiency requirements. Measure offering selections, based on storage volume and/or heating capacity, and incentivized output capacity should be calculated using the total number of eligible HPWH.
The COP and output capacity at its highest output should be used to establish eligibility. Heating capacity related to tank hot water storage capacity or supplemental electric resistance heaters that are not integrated into heat pumps, should not be included. When available, should use manufacturer specification sheets or obtained from databases such as ENERGY STAR, AHRI, or the CEC Appliance Database. If not available, use published values of TE/100 or RE/100 to estimate COP.
Tanked, split system, and standalone (i.e. instantaneous or tankless) heat pumps are eligible and should claim offerings based on their specific efficiency (UEF or COP) and connected storage volumes, and the baseline system eligibility.
Measure installation shall fully comply with all state and local requirements and regulations: National Electric Code and California Plumbing Code, Electric Code, and Energy Standards.
Partial retrofits of existing WH systems are eligible (i.e. replacing only some natural gas WHs in a system with HPWHs, but not all) as long as the HPWHs are not backup/redundant systems and the natural gas WHs that are being replaced are fully demolished.
The following are ineligible:
  • Non-domestic hot water uses, such as space conditioning, industrial (process) loads, and pools and/or spas applications.
  • Combination systems that serve domestic hot water and space heating
  • Baseline systems that use steam boilers
  • Heat pump systems with >1,000 kBtu/hr total output heating capacity. Required ancillary heating equipment such as electric resistance or temperature maintenance or swing tanks do not count towards this system size limit. The limit should apply to each individual hot water system loop separately.
  • Redundancy (backup) systems. However, HPWHs that are staged based on load or set up in a lead-lag configuration are eligible.
  • For AR offerings, high efficiency condensing systems baseline systems
SWWH028-04.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
SWWH028-04.Important Notes: This is a fuel substitution measure.

SWWH028-06.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
Verify that the new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Central domestic hot water systems in commercial or multifamily buildings often consist of one or more HPs connected to one or more storage tanks. Efficiencies, capacities, and other operating parameters for these types of systems differ from their individual components and vary based on system configurations and local conditions; this data is often not readily available. Therefore, the COP at full/highest output capacity of the individual HP units should be used to verify eligibility (DOE 10 CFR 431 Subpart G). Measure offering selections, based on storage volume and/or heating capacity, and incentivized output capacity should be calculated using the total number of eligible HPWHs. Heating capacity related to tank hot water storage capacity or supplemental electric resistance heaters that are not integrated into HPs, should not be included.
Integrated tank hot water storage capacity, used for HPWH energy saving claims, is established by climate zone based on the nominal storage volume. Below lists the allowed storage capacity (kBtu) for energy saving claims per gallon of storage volume; multiply this value by the nominal storage volume in gallons, from product specifications, to calculate the allowed storage capacity for relevant heat pump water heater energy saving claims:
  • CZ01: 0.6134 kBtu/gal
  • CZ02: 0.5896
  • CZ03: 0.5746
  • CZ04: 0.5663
  • CZ05: 0.5746
  • CZ06: 0.5413
  • CZ07: 0.5386
  • CZ08: 0.5333
  • CZ09: 0.5407
  • CZ10: 0.5619
  • CZ11: 0.5856
  • CZ12: 0.5809
  • CZ13: 0.5750
  • CZ14: 0.5883
  • CZ15: 0.5059
  • CZ16: 0.6562
When available, COP values should be used directly from manufacturer specification sheets or obtained from databases such as ENERGY STAR, AHRI, or the CEC Appliance Database. If there is no published COP values, use published values of thermal efficiency or recovery efficiency divided by 100 to estimate COP and establish eligibility.
Tanked, split system, and standalone (i.e. instantaneous or tankless) heat pumps are eligible for this measure package and are required to provide the same product eligibility information. Tankless and split HPWH technologies should claim offerings based on their specific efficiency (UEF or COP) and connected storage volumes, and the baseline system eligibility.
SWWH028-06.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
Multifamily measures were developed in EnergyPlus and are defined using heat pump water heater heating output capacity (kBtu/hr) and coefficient of performance (COP). Commercial measures were developed in the DEER Water Heater Calculator v5.1 and are characterized by storage capacity (in gallons) and either the uniform energy factor (UEF) (<100 gallons) or coefficient of performance (COP) (≥100 gallons).

SWWH028-08.Offering ID:
A large commercial heat pump (HP) water heater (WH) or central heat pump water heating system, meeting the specified coefficient of performance (COP) or uniform energy factor (UEF) requirement, replacing a base case natural gas water heater.
  • Commercial WHs must have storage volume ≥75 gallons. WHs with <100 gallons are rated by UEF, and WHs with ≥100 gallons are rated using COP.
  • For multifamily, smaller multifamily central HPWH installations use split HP systems with <150 kBtuh/hr. To accommodate these smaller systems and since savings are normalized by heating output capacity (kBtu/hr), there is no minimum system heating capacity requirement for the multifamily offerings.
SWWH028-08.Base Case Description: A large natural gas storage hot water heater or boiler systems, meeting the minimum federal code thermal efficiency (Et) requirement for the capacity type and range.
SWWH028-08.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The heat pump (HP) water heater (WH) must meet the storage capacity, heating output capacity (commercial only), and minimum efficiency requirements.
Integrated, split system, and standalone (i.e. instantaneous or tankless) heat pumps are eligible. Tankless and split HPWH technologies should claim offerings based on their specific efficiency (UEF or COP) and connected storage volumes, and the baseline system eligibility.
Measure installation shall fully comply with all state and local requirements and regulations, such as the National Electric Code and California Plumbing Code, Electric Code, and Energy Standards.
Partial retrofits of existing water heating systems are eligible (i.e. replacing only some natural gas water heaters in a system with heat pumps, but not all) as long as the HPWHs are not backup/redundant systems and the natural gas water heaters that are being replaced are fully demolished.
The following are ineligible:
  • Non-domestic hot water uses, such as space conditioning, industrial (process) loads, and pools and/or spas applications
  • Combination systems that serve domestic hot water and space heating.
  • Baseline systems that use steam boilers
  • Heat pump systems with >1,000 kBtu/hr total output heating capacity. Required ancillary heating equipment such as electric resistance or temperature maintenance or swing tanks do not count towards this limit. If the building has multiple separate hot water system loops, the limit should be applied to each individual loop.
  • Redundancy (backup) systems. However, HPWHs that are staged based on load or set up in a lead-lag configuration are eligible.
  • For AR offerings, high efficiency condensing systems baseline systems.
SWWH028-08.Quality Assurance:
Verify that the new unit is properly installed and commissioned.
Verify that the new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table. COP values should be taken directly from manufacturer specification sheets or reputable databases such as ENERGY STAR®, AHRI, or the California Energy Commission Appliance Database. If there are no published COP values, divide the published values of thermal efficiency or recovery efficiency by 100 to estimate COP.
Central domestic hot water systems often consist of one or more HPs connected to ≥1 storage tanks. System efficiencies, capacities, and other operating parameters differ from their individual components and vary based on system configurations and local conditions; this data is often not readily available. Therefore, the COP at full/highest output capacity of the individual HP units should be used to verify eligibility (DOE 10 CFR 431 Subpart G). Measure offering selections, based on storage volume and/or heating capacity, and incentivized output capacity should be calculated using the total number of eligible HPWHs and eligible storage volume of the system. Output capacity for claims shall exclude any capacity derived from electric resistance heating elements.
SWWH028-08.Important Notes:
This is a fuel substitution measure.
Multifamily measures were developed in EnergyPlus and are defined using heat pump water heater heating output capacity (kBtu/hr) and coefficient of performance (COP). Commercial measures were developed in the DEER Water Heater Calculator v5.1 and are characterized by storage capacity (in gallons) and either the uniform energy factor (UEF) (<100 gallons) or coefficient of performance (COP) (≥100 gallons).

SWWH031-03.Offering ID: A commercial heat pump water heater meeting the specified uniform energy factor (UEF).
SWWH031-03.Base Case Description: An electric storage water heater, with a 30, 40, or 50 gallon storage volume, meeting the minimum federal code UEF requirement.
SWWH031-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The heat pump water heater must meet the storage capacity and minimum efficiency requirements. Existing base equipment must be disposed.
Split-system heat pump products are eligible. However, they shall be treated as a single product with a single product efficiency and storage volume. Efficiency and performance ratings for the entire package shall be provided by the manufacturer.
This measure is only applicable to 1-for-1 replacements.
Larger heat pump water heater offerings with nominal capacities in the 65 and 80 gallons categories use heat pump water heater baseline assumptions in the calculations. Therefore, these offerings can be used to replace less efficient base case equipment, such as electric resistance storage water heaters, since the savings are conservative.
To accommodate baseline water heater storage capacities that do not exactly match the nominal storage capacity, the following shows the range of rated storage capacity that are eligible for each nominal storage capacity listed for baseline equipment.
  • 30 gal: 26 gal - 35 gal
  • 40 gal: 36 gal - 45 gal
  • 50 gal: 46 gal - 55 gal
  • 60 gal: 56 gal - 69 gal
  • 75 gal: 70 gal - 89 gal
SWWH031-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

SWWH031-06.Offering ID: An efficient commercial heat pump water heater meeting the specified requirement. For units that are <100 gallons, the efficiency is rated using uniform energy factor (UEF). SWWH032-01.Offering ID: An ENERGY STAR® solar water heating system, with a solar uniform energy factor (SUEF) ≥1.8 for gas backup, that will offset the use of a gas-fired domestic water heater.
SWWH032-01.Base Case Description: A standard efficiency storage or tankless gas water heater meeting the federal code minimum uniform energy factor (UEF).
SWWH032-01.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible products must meet the required specifications. Systems must conform to the manufacturer specifications and with all applicable electrical, plumbing, and building codes and standards. Permits are required for all solar water heating (SWH) system installations. Solar water heating systems used for space or pool heating are not eligible.

SWWH033-02.Offering ID: A gas absorption heat pump (GAHP) water heater system, with coefficients of performance (COP) ≥1.25 at standard conditions (70°F outdoor air and 130°F outlet temperature), sized to satisfy the base load of the domestic hot water (DHW) system and improve the efficiency of the conventional gas-fired domestic water heating system.
SWWH033-02.Base Case Description: A natural gas standard efficiency centralized hot water boiler meeting the federal code minimum thermal efficiency (TE).
SWWH033-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible products must conform to manufacturer specifications and with all applicable mechanical, plumbing and building codes and standards.
Sizing the gas absorption heat pump (GAHP) for the domestic hot water (DHW) heating load is extremely important for the performance of the system. The sizing of the GAHP system will affect the energy output, response time and temperature differential across the heat pumps. In this measure the GAHP is sized to serve the base load of the building, not the whole building load.
  • The gas heat pump water heater must operate as the primary or a baseload equipment and supplemented by an existing DHW boiler(s) operating as a peak shaving unit in this hybrid system (for measure case boiler & GAHP heating rates.) Baseload is defined as the constant water heater demand due to environmental losses and times of low hot water use. In general, the gas heat pump should not have output capacity larger than half of the existing boiler which will serve as the peak shaving unit.
  • The intermittent nature of DHW loads provided undesirable conditions for the heat pump performance due to constant cycling of the hot water temperature differential.
  • One source used for selecting a DHW system in multifamily building is the 2019 ASHRAE Applications textbook.
  • Multifamily buildings smaller than 20 apartments would not be applicable due to their lower hot water demand.

All system components (gas heat pump, piping, valves, tanks, pumps, insulation, and controllers) must be new and must not have been previously placed in service in any other location or for any other application. Rebuilt, refurbished, or relocated equipment is not eligible to receive incentives.
The efficiency requirement of COP ≥1.25 is at standard conditions, defined as 70°F outdoor air temperature and 130°F outlet temperature.
SWWH033-02.Quality Assurance:
Verify that new unit meets or exceeds the required efficiency in the Offering ID table.
Verify that new unit is properly installed and commissioned.

  • Multifamily buildings smaller than 20 apartments would not be applicable due to their lower hot water demand.

SWWH034-02.Offering ID: A multi-panel forced circulation solar water heating system that will offset the use of a natural gas domestic water heater.
SWWH034-02.Base Case Description: A central storage gas water heater meeting the federal code thermal efficiency (TE) requirement of 82%.
SWWH034-02.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Eligible products must conform to the manufacturer specifications and with all applicable electrical, plumbing, and building codes and standards. Permits are required for all solar water heating (SWH) system installations. All systems must be installed in compliance with the Solar Rating & Certification Corporation (ICC-SRCC™) or the International Association of Plumbing & Mechanical Officials (IAPMO) standards and guidelines.
Solar thermal collectors panels installed must be certified by SRCC as OG-100 solar thermal collectors.
For more information on effective area please reference the SRCC Overview of California Effective Area Metric, Effective Area Calculator: www.solar-rating.org/effectivearea [solar-rating.org].
Solar water heating systems used for space or pool heating are not eligible.

SWWH034-03.Offering ID: A multi-panel forced circulation solar water heating system that will offset the use of a natural gas domestic water heater.
SWWH034-03.Base Case Description: A central storage gas water heater meeting the federal code thermal efficiency (TE) requirement of 82%.
SWWH034-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /: Eligible products must conform to the manufacturer specifications and with all applicable electrical, plumbing, and building codes and standards. Permits are required for all solar water heating (SWH) system installations. SWWP002-03.Offering ID: Installation of a variable frequency drive (VFD) to an agricultural irrigation well pump (≤300 hp) or booster pump (≤150 hp) in place of throttling the flow.
SWWP002-03.Base Case Description: A constant speed well or booster agricultural pump that is controlled by throttling the flow based on irrigation needs.
SWWP002-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The customer must have an existing electrically operated agricultural booster or well pump installed on site or plans to install a new agricultural booster or well pump.
The VFD must be used to adjust operation of a pump to meet flow/pressure requirements and not be used simply as a soft starter or for cavitation control.
The pumping application must currently have the means to vary the pressure/flow (i.e. throttle valve, control valve, etc.).
The VFD must be installed on the pump motor with a minimum operation of 1,000 hours per year.
The VFD must be installed on a pressurized irrigation system (no flood irrigation).
The VFD is recommended to meet requirements as specified by IEEE Standard 519TM-2014.
The following are ineligible:
  • A well pump used to fill a reservoir,
  • A well pump discharging directly into a canal, and/or
  • A mixed flow pump (high volume, low head).
SWWP002-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify the preexisting fan or pump system.
Determine if the application of VFD control is appropriate.
Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that pump speed is controlled appropriately.

SWWP004-03.Offering ID: A clean water pump meeting the specified pump energy index (PEI). PEI tiers were set by an incremental improvement of 0.02, based on the PEI distribution of eligible pumps in the Hydraulic Institute (HI) database. Offerings are characterized by the pump horsepower, PEI, and load type (variable or constant)
SWWP004-03.Base Case Description: A standard clean water pump meeting the specified PEI.
SWWP004-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Only clean water pumps that are intended for agricultural, commercial, and industrial sectors with a nominal pump motor horsepower rating of ≤250 and meeting the PEI requirements. The PEI should be confirmed on the Hydraulic Institute (HI) database (https://er.pumps.org/ratings/search).
Any of the following clean water rotodynamic pump classes are eligible:
  • End Suction Frame Mount (ESFM)
  • End Suction Close Coupled (ESCC)
  • In-line (IL)
  • Radially Split multi-stage vertical in-line diffuser casing (RSV)
  • Vertical Turbine Submersible (ST)

SWWP004-04.Offering ID: A clean or wastewater pump meeting the specified pump energy index (PEI). PEI tiers were set by an incremental improvement of 0.02, based on the PEI distribution of eligible pumps in the Hydraulic Institute (HI) database. Offerings are characterized by the pump horsepower, PEI, and load type (variable or constant).
SWWP004-04.Base Case Description: A standard clean or wastewater pump meeting the specified PEI.
SWWP004-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
Only clean or wastewater water pumps that are intended for agricultural, commercial, and industrial sectors with a nominal pump motor horsepower rating of ≤250 and meeting the PEI requirements are eligible. The PEI should be confirmed on the Hydraulic Institute (HI) database or the Department of Energy (DOE) Pump Compliance Certification Database.
Any of the following clean water rotodynamic pump classes are eligible:

  • End Suction Close Coupled (ESCC)
  • In-line (IL)
  • Radially Split multi-stage vertical in-line diffuser casing (RSV)
  • Submersible Turbine (ST)

SWWP005-03.Offering ID:
Installation of a variable frequency drive (VFD) to an agricultural irrigation well pump (≤600 hp) or booster pump (≤150 hp) in place of throttling the flow.
A “Tier 1 basic VFD systems”, which does not have any minimum performance standard requirement, was covered in the SWWP002 measure. In 2017, Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E) contracted the Irrigation Training & Research Center (ITRC) at California Polytechnic State University (Cal Poly) to develop the technical specifications requirements for an enhanced VFD. A Mid-level Tier 2 and Advanced Tier 3 were created based on ITRC VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) Specifications for On-Farm Pumps report.
SWWP005-03.Base Case Description: A constant speed well or booster agricultural pump that is controlled by throttling the flow based on irrigation needs.
SWWP005-03.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The customer must install a VFD system on an existing electrically operated agricultural booster or well pump motor installed on site or install VFDs on new pumps or existing pumps where the horsepower of the serving motor is increased (added load).
The customer must install a VFD system on the pump motor.
The pumping application must be able to vary the flow/pressure of the pump (i.e. throttle valve, control valve, etc.) and the VFD must be used to adjust operation of pump to meet flow/pressure requirements, not simply as a soft starter, or for cavitation control.
The VFD must be installed on a pressurized irrigation system (no flood irrigation).
The pumping application must operate for a minimum of 1,000 hours per year.
Tier 2 offerings must conform to the requirements outlined in the ITRC Report Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Specifications for On-Farm Pumps, except for the requirement specified in item 3.E.7c in section “3.E.7 Cooling” of the Design Specifications. Tier 3 must conform to the requirements outlined in the ITRC Report Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Specifications for Agricultural Irrigation Pumping.
The following are not eligible:
  • A well pump used to fill a reservoir
  • A well pump discharging directly into a canal
  • A mixed flow pump (high volume, low head)
SWWP005-03.Quality Assurance:
Verify the preexisting fan or pump system.
Determine if the application of VFD control is appropriate.
Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that pump speed is controlled appropriately.

SWWP005-04.Offering ID:
Installation of a variable frequency drive (VFD) to an agricultural irrigation well pump (≤600 hp) or booster pump (≤150 hp) in place of throttling the flow.
A “Tier 1 basic VFD systems”, which does not have any minimum performance standard requirement, was covered in the SWWP002 measure. In 2017, Pacific Gas and Electric Company (PG&E) contracted the Irrigation Training & Research Center (ITRC) at California Polytechnic State University (Cal Poly) to develop the technical specifications requirements for an enhanced VFD. A Mid-level Tier 2 and Advanced Tier 3 were created based on ITRC VFD (Variable Frequency Drive) Specifications for On-Farm Pumps report. The Tier 1 offerings were added to this measure from SWWP002, which will then be sunsetted starting in 2026.
SWWP005-04.Base Case Description: A constant speed well or booster agricultural pump that is controlled by throttling the flow based on irrigation needs.
SWWP005-04.Other: Program Exclusion / /:
The customer must install a VFD system on an existing electrically operated agricultural booster or well pump motor installed on site or install VFDs on new pumps or existing pumps where the horsepower of the serving motor is increased (added load).
The pumping application must be able to vary the flow/pressure of the pump (i.e. throttle valve, control valve, etc.) and the VFD must be used to adjust operation of pump to meet flow/pressure requirements, not simply as a soft starter, or for cavitation control.
The VFD must be installed on a pressurized irrigation system (no flood irrigation).
The pumping application must operate for a minimum of 1,000 hours per year.
Tier 1 offerings must meet the specifications set forth by IEEE Standard 519TM-2014 or newer. IEEE Standard 519TM-2021 was approved in 2022 but is not a requirement for this measure. Tier 2 and Tier 3 calculation studies were performed using IEEE Standard 519TM-2014, so Tier 1 requirements are being maintained to match the other measure cases. Tier 2 must conform to the requirements outlined in the ITRC Report Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Specifications for On-Farm Pumps, except for the requirement specified in item 3.E.7c in section “3.E.7 Cooling” of the Design Specifications. Tier 3 must conform to the requirements outlined in the ITRC Report Variable Frequency Drive (VFD) Specifications for Agricultural Irrigation Pumping.
The VFD is not eligible for the following pumping applications:
  • A well pump used to fill a reservoir
  • A well pump discharging directly into a canal
  • A mixed flow pump (high volume, low head)
SWWP005-04.Quality Assurance:
Verify the preexisting fan or pump system.
Determine if the application of VFD control is appropriate.
Ensure that the VFD is installed correctly, and that pump speed is controlled appropriately.
